Name: Unit No: 
 
Admission Date: Discharge Date: 
 
Date of Birth: Sex: F
 
Service: NEUROLOGY
 
Allergies: 
amoxicillin
 
Attending: .
 
Chief Complaint:
Weakness and lethargy 
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

 
History of Present Illness:
Patient is a year old female with history of chronic
abdominal pain and anemia w/ recent Fe sucrose infusion ( )
complicated by infusion reaction (mottling and discoloration of
feet) status post IV steroids who presents with increasing lethargy and
headache.

History per records and per her Aunt as patient cannot
provide much history. Per her aunt, has been doing well
recently without any illness, behavioral change. She had a cold
she was getting over but otherwise doing well. She had an Fe
transfusion on around 3:30P at and the aunt
accompanied her to the visit. Towards the end of the infusion,
 developed mottle purple lower extremities and was having
nausea, heart racing. She was sent to where she had SBP
up to 144. Her exam per records notable for "shivering...
doesn't open her eyes...mottled hands and feet". There was no
concern for respiratory distress. She was given 50 mg IV
Benadryl, 100 mg IV hydrocortisone and observed for 5 hours and
then discharged. At that time was reportedly "lethargic"
meaning sleeping frequently but able to sit up and walk without
issue. They arrived home and went to bed. Around 6:00 AM
on , aunt check on her and she was still sleeping.
She was reporting a headache (which the transfusion place said
would happen) but did not mention other characteristic. 
 
aunt gave her acetaminophen and water and went back to
sleep. Over the next few hours, she heard awake several
times and use the bathroom. Then as it got later and later into
the day and was not up and about yet, her aunt became
worried. She told to call the which she
was able to do. She reportedly said she continued to have a
headache and felt sleepy. She was instructed to go to the ED. 

She went to the ED at for evaluation. At she
had T 99.5, P 58, RR 15 and sat 100%. BP 110/67. She was
reportedly drowsy and arousing to voice, "alert oriented x3",
consistent eye fluttering, pupils reactive to light and
extraocular eye movements full, with reportedly "non focal 
exam".
Lab work notable for WBC 7.2, Hgb 13.2, Na 142, bicarb 26, Cr
0.9, Ca 9.4, normal LFTs, Fe 360, Ferritin 438. VBG pH was 7.43,
PCO2 was 45.She had a normal and LP with WBC 2, 100%
monocytes glucose 57, protein 24, no xanthochromia. She
ultimately had MRA and MRV which showed no thrombus or venoous
thrombosis. She was transferred to for further management.

On my interview she cannot provide much history other than to 
say
she is here because "I'm tired". She keeps her eyes closed 
during
questioning. She reports headache but cannot describe where it 
is
or features other than +photophobia. She is unable to 
participate
in other questioning, often getting tearful and saying "the
questions are hard". 

Per her father and aunt, she has no history of seizures, or CNS
infection. She did have a concussion at years old. 
 
Past Medical History:
Anemia 
 
Social History:
 
Family History:
Mother with a celiac disease and autoimmune hypothyroidism. Dad
is healthy. She has a cousin with seizures.
 
Physical Exam:
Admission exam:
98.1 76 130/78 14 96% RA 
General: appears to be sleeping, occasional eye lid fluttering,
lip movements, occasional slow movements of head from side to
side
HEENT: no trauma, no jaundice, no lesions of oropharynx 
CV: RRR, wwp
Pulm: breathing comfortably on RA 
Ext: clammy, warm and no rash

Neurologic:

-Mental Status: She frequently gets upset during exam and is
tearful at times, then abulic at other times. Eyes open only
briefly to voice. She is oriented to but not full
date. Knows she is in a "hospital" but not the name. She says 
she
is in the hospital because, "I'm tired". She is unable to 
provide
history. Speech is not dysarthric, says words when asked
questions, no spontaneous speech output. Follows simple commands
like open eyes, lift legs. She is able to name "key" and
"feather" on stroke card but then stops naming and closes her
eyes. She reads the first sentence on stroke card but then no
more and closes her eyes. When asked to describe stroke card
picture she says, "dishes". She does not participate in further
exam.

-Cranial Nerves:
 II, III, IV, VI: Pupils 8 mm->6 mm. EOMI without nystagmus. 
VFF
to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam revealed no papilledema,
exudates, or hemorrhages.
 VII: No facial droop, facial musculature symmetric with 
grimace.
 VIII: Hearing intact to exam
 IX, X: Palate elevates symmetrically.
 XII: Tongue protrudes in midline.

-Motor: Normal bulk, tone throughout. She says she cannot move
her arms. When arms placed over her head, her arms slowly miss
her face and slowly drops to the bed in a controlled fashion. 
She
does lift her arms to hold the side rails of the bed
spontaneously. She lifts her legs antigravity and holds them
without drift.

-Sensory: slightly withdrawals in upper extremities and says
"ouch", briskly withdrawals in lower extremities to noxious
stimuli and says "ouch"

-DTRs:
 Tri Pat Ach
 L 2 2 0 3 2
 R 2 2 0 3 2
 Plantar response was flexor bilaterally.

-Coordination: patient could not participate

-Gait: could not assess as patient would not get out of bed

Discharge Exam:
General: sitting up in her chair with eyes closed
HEENT: no trauma, no jaundice, no lesions of oropharynx 
CV: sinus bradycardia, no m/r/g
Pulm: Breathing comfortably on RA 
Ext: Warm and well perfused, no rash or mottling

Neurologic:

-Mental Status: Answering questions with slow short sentence.
More humor and complex sentences observed today.
Eyes intermittently close while she is talking. Speech
is not dysarthric, no spontaneous speech output. Follows
simple commands.

-Cranial Nerves:
II, III, IV, VI: Pupils 8 mm->4 mm. EOMI without nystagmus. 
V: facial sensation intact throughout
VII: No facial droop, facial musculature symmetric with grimace
but limited facial movements.
IX, X, XII: palate elevates symmetrically, tongue midline

-Motor: Normal bulk, tone throughout. Lifting arms and legs
against gravity but not against resistance ( )

-Sensory: Sensation intact to touch and temperature throughout

-DTRs: 1+ patellar, biceps, brachioradialis throughout

-Coordination: No dysmetria or tremor. 

-Gait: Ambulated well with a .

IMPRESSION: This telemetry captured no pushbutton activations. 
The background showed normal waking and sleep patterns. There 
were no focal abnormalities, epileptiform features, or 
electrographic seizures. A bradycardia was noted. 

IMAGING:
========
MRI BRAIN WITH/WITHOUT CONTRAST 

FINDINGS: 
 
A 5 mm FLAIR hypointense and T1 isointense lesion at midline 
between the 
anterior and posterior pituitary is noted. There is no evidence 
of 
hemorrhage, edema, mass effect, midline shift or infarction. The 
ventricles and sulci are normal in caliber and configuration. 
There is no abnormal enhancement after contrast administration. 
 
IMPRESSION: 
 
A 5 mm FLAIR hypointense and T1 isointense lesion at midline 
between the 
anterior and posterior pituitary likely represents a Rathke's 
cleft cyst. 
Further evaluation is needed, dedicated pituitary MR may be 
obtained. 
 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
See worksheet

famotidine 40 mg daily
birth control per her aunt
 
 :

2. Famotidine 40 mg PO DAILY 
3. Metoprolol Tartrate 12.5 mg PO DAILY 
4. Multivitamins W/minerals 1 TAB PO DAILY 
5. Nortriptyline 10 mg PO QHS 
6. 1 item miscellaneous ONCE 
Prognosis: Good
 : 13 months 
RX Once Disp #*1 Each Refills:*0 

 
Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care
 
Facility:
 
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Functional neurological syndrome 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Lethargic but arousable.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at 
 .

You were in the hospital because of headache, lethargy, and 
weakness after an iron infusion.

You had a number of tests performed in the hospital, all of 
which were reassuring. An MRI of your brain showed no evidence 
of stroke or inflammation. An EEG to monitor your brain waves 
showed no evidence of seizure. Your weakness gradually improved 
over the course of your hospitalization and will continue to 
improve after you leave the hospital. 

After leaving the hospital, you should continue to work on 
improving your strength. It will improve as long as you work 
hard!

We wish you the best,
Your Care Team

section_text:
Patient is a year old female with history of chronic
abdominal pain and anemia w/ recent Fe sucrose infusion ( )
complicated by infusion reaction (mottling and discoloration of
feet) status post IV steroids who presents with increasing lethargy and
headache.

History per records and per her Aunt as patient cannot
provide much history. Per her aunt, has been doing well
recently without any illness, behavioral change. She had a cold
she was getting over but otherwise doing well. She had an Fe
transfusion on around 3:30P at and the aunt
accompanied her to the visit. Towards the end of the infusion,
 developed mottle purple lower extremities and was having
nausea, heart racing. She was sent to where she had SBP
up to 144. Her exam per records notable for "shivering...
doesn't open her eyes...mottled hands and feet". There was no
concern for respiratory distress. She was given 50 mg IV
Benadryl, 100 mg IV hydrocortisone and observed for 5 hours and
then discharged. At that time was reportedly "lethargic"
meaning sleeping frequently but able to sit up and walk without
issue. They arrived home and went to bed. Around 6:00 AM
on , aunt check on her and she was still sleeping.
She was reporting a headache (which the transfusion place said
would happen) but did not mention other characteristic. 
 
aunt gave her acetaminophen and water and went back to
sleep. Over the next few hours, she heard awake several
times and use the bathroom. Then as it got later and later into
the day and was not up and about yet, her aunt became
worried. She told to call the which she
was able to do. She reportedly said she continued to have a
headache and felt sleepy. She was instructed to go to the ED. 

She went to the ED at for evaluation. At she
had T 99.5, P 58, RR 15 and sat 100%. BP 110/67. She was
reportedly drowsy and arousing to voice, "alert oriented x3",
consistent eye fluttering, pupils reactive to light and
extraocular eye movements full, with reportedly "non focal 
exam".
Lab work notable for WBC 7.2, Hgb 13.2, Na 142, bicarb 26, Cr
0.9, Ca 9.4, normal LFTs, Fe 360, Ferritin 438. VBG pH was 7.43,
PCO2 was 45.She had a normal and LP with WBC 2, 100%
monocytes glucose 57, protein 24, no xanthochromia. She
ultimately had MRA and MRV which showed no thrombus or venoous
thrombosis. She was transferred to for further management.

On my interview she cannot provide much history other than to 
say
she is here because "I'm tired". She keeps her eyes closed 
during
questioning. She reports headache but cannot describe where it 
is
or features other than +photophobia. She is unable to 
participate
in other questioning, often getting tearful and saying "the
questions are hard". 

Per her father and aunt, she has no history of seizures, or CNS
infection. She did have a concussion at years old.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. was intubated upon arrival and was found to be 
hypotensive, minimally responsive to recussitation with blood 
and crystalloid. She had a positive FAST exam and was taken 
emergently to the operating room. She was found to have a 
splenic laceration and left kidney laceration, and underwent 
splenectomy, and left nephrectomy. The remainder of the abdomen 
was explored and no other sources of bleeding were identified. 
Please see dictated operative report for further details. The 
patient was transferred to the ICU for further recussitation. 
Upon neurological exam in the ICU it was found that her left 
pupil was now also fixed and dilated along with the right which 
had been since initial evaluation in the ED. She had no gag 
reflex or corneal reflex, and no longer had purposeful movement, 
but was at times overbreathing the ventillator. She continued to 
be hypotensive on pressors and with blood products being 
transfused. Her abdomen was opened in the ICU for a bedside 
ex-lap. Diffuse oozing was seen, likely due to coagulopathy, but 
no large sources of bleeding that could be corrected. Her 
abdomen was left open. Initially upon re-entering her abdomen 
her pressures improved, but then again slowly continued to trend 
down. Given the cumulative blood loss from her abdomen, head, 
b/l lower extremeties, it was difficult to keep her adequately 
perfused even with crystalloid, blood products, and pressors. 
Massive transfusion protocol was initiated upon arrival and she 
received >38 Units of blood product. She remained hypothermic 
throughout most of the recussitation even with warmed product, 
fluids, and bear hugger.
A discussion was had with her husband, sister, neice, and family 
friends in the ICU meeting room, and given the extent of her 
injuries, and likely unrecoverable neurological status, the 
decision was made to change her code status to comfort measures 
only, and the patient expired shortly thereafter.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. was intubated upon arrival and was found to be 
hypotensive, minimally responsive to recussitation with blood 
and crystalloid. She had a positive FAST exam and was taken 
emergently to the operating room. She was found to have a 
splenic laceration and left kidney laceration, and underwent 
splenectomy, and left nephrectomy. The remainder of the abdomen 
was explored and no other sources of bleeding were identified. 
Please see dictated operative report for further details. The 
patient was transferred to the ICU for further recussitation. 
Upon neurological exam in the ICU it was found that her left 
pupil was now also fixed and dilated along with the right which 
had been since initial evaluation in the ED. She had no gag 
reflex or corneal reflex, and no longer had purposeful movement, 
but was at times overbreathing the ventillator. She continued to 
be hypotensive on pressors and with blood products being 
transfused. Her abdomen was opened in the ICU for a bedside 
ex-lap. Diffuse oozing was seen, likely due to coagulopathy, but 
no large sources of bleeding that could be corrected. Her 
abdomen was left open. Initially upon re-entering her abdomen 
her pressures improved, but then again slowly continued to trend 
down. Given the cumulative blood loss from her abdomen, head, 
b/l lower extremeties, it was difficult to keep her adequately 
perfused even with crystalloid, blood products, and pressors. 
Massive transfusion protocol was initiated upon arrival and she 
received >38 Units of blood product. She remained hypothermic 
throughout most of the recussitation even with warmed product, 
fluids, and bear hugger.
A discussion was had with her husband, sister, neice, and family 
friends in the ICU meeting room, and given the extent of her 
injuries, and likely unrecoverable neurological status, the 
decision was made to change her code status to comfort measures 
only, and the patient expired shortly thereafter.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. was intubated upon arrival and was found to be 
hypotensive, minimally responsive to recussitation with blood 
and crystalloid. She had a positive FAST exam and was taken 
emergently to the operating room. She was found to have a 
splenic laceration and left kidney laceration, and underwent 
splenectomy, and left nephrectomy. The remainder of the abdomen 
was explored and no other sources of bleeding were identified. 
Please see dictated operative report for further details. The 
patient was transferred to the ICU for further recussitation. 
Upon neurological exam in the ICU it was found that her left 
pupil was now also fixed and dilated along with the right which 
had been since initial evaluation in the ED. She had no gag 
reflex or corneal reflex, and no longer had purposeful movement, 
but was at times overbreathing the ventillator. She continued to 
be hypotensive on pressors and with blood products being 
transfused. Her abdomen was opened in the ICU for a bedside 
ex-lap. Diffuse oozing was seen, likely due to coagulopathy, but 
no large sources of bleeding that could be corrected. Her 
abdomen was left open. Initially upon re-entering her abdomen 
her pressures improved, but then again slowly continued to trend 
down. Given the cumulative blood loss from her abdomen, head, 
b/l lower extremeties, it was difficult to keep her adequately 
perfused even with crystalloid, blood products, and pressors. 
Massive transfusion protocol was initiated upon arrival and she 
received >38 Units of blood product. She remained hypothermic 
throughout most of the recussitation even with warmed product, 
fluids, and bear hugger.
A discussion was had with her husband, sister, neice, and family 
friends in the ICU meeting room, and given the extent of her 
injuries, and likely unrecoverable neurological status, the 
decision was made to change her code status to comfort measures 
only, and the patient expired shortly thereafter.

hypovolemic hemorrhagic shock and traumatic brain injury -

Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs
 
Attending: .
 
Chief Complaint:
palpitations and right sided chest pain 
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

 
History of Present Illness:
The patient woke on the morning of admission and felt 
palpitations in his chest. He has awoken in the past with 
palpitations, however, he had dull right sided, non-radiating, 
non-exertional chest pain. He came to the emergency room 
for further evaluation. His chest pain resolved spontaneously en 
route to the ED. He reports no associated diaphoresis, shortness 
of breath, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness/lightheadedness. He 
denies any pain with activity. His prior anginal episode before 
CABG was chest tightness. He's prescribed sublingual nitro and 
never uses it. 
. 
In the ED, the vitals on presentation were T: 97.8, LABTEST HR: 114 BP: 
154/? RR: 20 02LABTEST sat: 95 %RA. While in atrial flutter with a 2:1 
block, the emergency room gave him metoprolol 5 mg IV x 1 
followed by PO metoprolol 70 mg PO and then sotalol 120 mg PO. 
His heart rate was well-controlled and the ED decided to observe 
the patient in their unit. On the next troponin draw, the 
patient had second set with troponin 0.12 and CK/MB 180/14 
(first set was normal). The patient was admitted for a higher 
troponin level. In the ED, the patient also received aspirin 325 
mg PO. CXR 1V reportedly within normal limits. CTA without 
evidence of PE. EKG with atrial fibrillation. 
. 
On review of systems, he denies any prior history of stroke, 
TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the 
time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black 
stools or red stools. He denies recent fevers, chills or rigors. 
He denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other 
review of systems were negative. 
. 
Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain, 
dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, 
ankle edema, syncope or presyncope. 

 
Past Medical LABTEST History: 1. CARDIAC RISK FACTORS: Dyslipidemia, Hypertension 
2. CARDIAC HISTORY: 
-LABTEST CABG: 4 V CABG (LIMA->LAD, SVG->RCA, SVG->D1, SVG->OM/RI) 
-PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTIONS: 
-PACING/ICD: EnRhythm dual-chamber pacemaker 
3. OTHER PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: 
--Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation - first noted post-op and 
complicated by complete heart block 
--Ulcerative Colitis (status post polypectomy w/ high grade 
dysplasia) 
--GERD 
--Diverticulosis 
--Inguinal hernia 
--Internal Hemorrhoids 
--Benign prostatic hypertrophy

Family History:
No family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or 
sudden cardiac death; otherwise non-contributory. 
. 
The patient's father as well as two of his uncles had coronary 
artery disease. His maternal aunt had colon cancer. There is no 
family history of premature coronary artery disease or sudden 
death.

 . 
 
Brief Hospital Course:
 with CAD status post CABG x4, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (on 
Coumadin), hypertension, dyslipidemia, ulcerative colitis 
presenting with palpitations in his chest and right sided chest 
pain. 

Atrial flutter with rapid venticular rate: The patient has a 
history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and was found to have 
atrial flutter with rapid ventricular rate while in the ER. He 
was given Lopressor 5 mg IV, metoprolol 40 mg PO and his home 
sotalol and he converted to sinus rhythm and stayed in sinus 
rhythm during his hopital stay. He noted that his atrial 
fibrillation developed in the past upon waking and wondered if 
it can be related to sleep apnea (sister witnessed patient's 
apneic episodes). In order to suppress future events, the 
patient was started on diltiazem ER 120 mg. Since his episodes 
are related to sleeping, an outpatient sleep study was 
recommended. He was continued on his home dose of metoprolol, 
sotalol, and warfarin.

Increased troponins/MB-fraction: The patient has known CAD and 
after the patient's last catherterization showed diffuse disease 
and at that time, medical management was opted. After his rapid 
ventricular rate, the patient was noted to have an increase in 
his CK, CK-MB and troponin which peaked at 14, 7.8, 0.19 
respectively. The patient presented with atypical right sided 
chest pain, an NSTEMI was less likely, though the degree of 
troponin leaks were indicative of an NSTEMI. Since the cardiac 
enzyme leak happened in the setting of rapid ventricular rate 
and the patient has known coronary artery disease, his increase 
in cardiac enzymes were attributed to demand ischemia (since he 
had very minor chest pain that resolved prior to the 
hospitalization). 

Dyslipidemia: His lipid panel was not available until after 
discharge. The patient was noted to have a HDL=27 and LDL=76. 
Since the patient has active myocardial ischemia, would 
recommend better control of LDL as outpatient. 

Outpatient LABTEST followup: 1. Consider further cardiac testing for ischemia
2. Consider further therapy for cholesterol
3. Outpatient sleep study
4. Better control of atrial fibrillation (patient asked if he 
would be able to manage a fast heartbeat at home--deferred to 
outpatient)

Discharge Instructions:
You came to the hospital because you had palpitations and right 
sided chest pain. Your chest pain resolved before coming to the 
hospital. You were found to have atrial fibrillation in the ER 
and it was controlled there. Blood tests revealed that your 
heart had some low oxygen flow. You were admitted to the floor 
and we think that the low oxygen was in the setting of your fast 
heart rate. We decided to start you on a diltiazem, a calcium 
channel blocker, that will better control your atrial 
fibrillation (it will also help with your blood pressure).
.
You should have an outpatient sleep study because sleep apnea 
can trigger your atrial fibrillation.
.
Your medications have changed in the following way:
-Start diltiazem

 year old female w PMH of right breast cancer status post partial 
mastectomy and radiation and axillary dissection in 
c/b RUE chronic lymphedema.
 
Past Medical History:
BREAST CANCER 
LYMPHEDEMA 
GOITER 
 
Social History:
 
Family History:
Mother ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE 

Father ATHEROSCLEROTIC 
 CARDIOVASCULAR 
 DISEASE

 CARDIOVASCULAR 
 DISEASE 
 
Physical Exam:
Physical exam per post op check note LABTEST Temp: 98.2 PO LABTEST BP: 115 /75 L Lying LABTEST HR: 75 RR: 18 O2
LABTEST sat: 97 % O2 LABTEST delivery: 2 L nc 
Gen: AAOx3 NAD comfortable
CV: NRRR
Resp: Breathing comfortably
Abd: Soft, benign
Extrem: RUE is well perfused, with strong dopplerable signal at
incision site; sensation and motor are in tact

 
Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the plastic surgery service on 
 and had an OMENTAL LYMPH NODE HARVEST FROM ABDOMEN; 
TRANSPLANT OMENTAL LYMPH NODE TO RIGHT FOREARM. The patient 
tolerated the procedure well. The patient was placed in a bulky 
right arm splint with open area for monitoring of pulses. 
Pulses were monitored Q4H. On POD#1, OT fashioned a custom 
orthoplast splint in full extension. Patient was maintained in 
this splint for the remainder of her inpatient stay and her RUE 
was elevated at all times.
 .
 Neuro: Post-operatively, the patient received a morphine PCA 
with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral 
intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications. 
 .
 CV: The patient was stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 Pulmonary: The patient was stable from a pulmonary standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 GI/GU: Post-operatively, the patient was given IV fluids until 
tolerating oral intake. Her diet was advanced when appropriate, 
after passing flatus, which was tolerated well. She was also 
started on a bowel regimen to encourage bowel movement. Foley 
was removed on POD#2. Intake and output were closely monitored. 
 .
 ID: Post-operatively, the patient was started on IV cefazolin, 
then switched to PO cefadroxil for discharge home. The patient's 
temperature was closely watched for signs of infection. 
 .
 Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin during 
this stay, and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as 
possible. 
 .
 At the time of discharge on POD#6, the patient was doing well, 
afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, 
ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled.
 
Medications on Admission:
alendronate 70 mg tablet
1 Tablet(s) by mouth once per week take upon awakening one day 
per week; remain sittnig or standing for 30 min. then resume 
usual day
.
anastrozole 1 mg tablet
1 Tablet(s) by mouth daily 
 
Discharge LABTEST Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H 
2. Aspirin 121.5 mg PO DAILY 
RX *aspirin 81 mg 1.5 tablet(s) by mouth once a day Disp #*45 
Tablet Refills:*0 
3. cefaDROXil 500 mg oral twice a day 
RX *cefadroxil 500 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*8 
Capsule Refills:*0 
.
 restart alendronate.
Please hold anastrazole for 2 weeks.
 
Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service
 
Facility:
 
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
chronic right upper extremity lymphedema 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Personal Care: 
-You may keep your incisions open to air or covered with a 
clean, sterile gauze that you change daily. 
-You may shower daily with assistance as needed. You should 
cover your right arm with a plastic bag when showering. Your 
abdominal suture line can get wet. Pat dry after shower.
.
Activity: 
-You may resume your regular diet. If any symptoms of nausea, 
burping, or not passing a bowel movement in 2 days after 
discharge then resume liquid diet and take stool 
softeners/laxative until return of bowel function.
-DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity for 6 weeks following surgery. 
-Keep arm straight in splint and elevated for 1 more day and 
then you may remove splint. You should elevate your right arm 
as much as possible until follow up visit. 
.
 Medications: 
-Take aspirin 121.5 daily (1.5 baby aspirins) for 1 month after 
surgery. 
-Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise and 
take any new meds as ordered . 
- You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol 
for mild pain as directed on the packaging. 
-Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol. 
- Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking the 
prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softener if you wish. 
- Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber. .
.
 Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the LABTEST following: 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s). 2. A large amount of bleeding 
from the incision(s) or drain(s). 3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF 
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication. . 
.
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications. 
 * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you. 
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you. 
.
****Please call to schedule your Lymphedema 
Treatment Clinic Our lymphedema therapists 
would like to see you 4 weeks after your operation and then at 3 
months after your They will formally follow 
you every 3 months for years. 
 
Followup Instructions:

 and had an OMENTAL LYMPH NODE HARVEST FROM ABDOMEN; 
TRANSPLANT OMENTAL LYMPH NODE TO RIGHT FOREARM. The patient 
tolerated the procedure well. The patient was placed in a bulky 
right arm splint with open area for monitoring of pulses. 
Pulses were monitored Q4H. On POD#1, OT fashioned a custom 
orthoplast splint in full extension. Patient was maintained in 
this splint for the remainder of her inpatient stay and her RUE 
was elevated at all times.
 .
 Neuro: Post-operatively, the patient received a morphine PCA 
with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral 
intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications. 
 .
 CV: The patient was stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 Pulmonary: The patient was stable from a pulmonary standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 GI/GU: Post-operatively, the patient was given IV fluids until 
tolerating oral intake. Her diet was advanced when appropriate, 
after passing flatus, which was tolerated well. She was also 
started on a bowel regimen to encourage bowel movement. Foley 
was removed on POD#2. Intake and output were closely monitored. 
 .
 ID: Post-operatively, the patient was started on IV cefazolin, 
then switched to PO cefadroxil for discharge home. The patient's 
temperature was closely watched for signs of infection. 
 .

this stay, and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as 
possible.

afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, 
ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled.

Facility:
 
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
chronic right upper extremity lymphedema 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Personal Care: 
-You may keep your incisions open to air or covered with a 
clean, sterile gauze that you change daily. 
-You may shower daily with assistance as needed. You should 
cover your right arm with a plastic bag when showering. Your 
abdominal suture line can get wet. Pat dry after shower.
.
Activity: 
-You may resume your regular diet. If any symptoms of nausea, 
burping, or not passing a bowel movement in 2 days after 
discharge then resume liquid diet and take stool 
softeners/laxative until return of bowel function.
-DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity for 6 weeks following surgery. 
-Keep arm straight in splint and elevated for 1 more day and 
then you may remove splint. You should elevate your right arm 
as much as possible until follow up visit. 
.
 Medications: 
-Take aspirin 121.5 daily (1.5 baby aspirins) for 1 month after 
surgery. 
-Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise and 
take any new meds as ordered . 
- You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol 
for mild pain as directed on the packaging. 
-Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol. 
- Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking the 
prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softener if you wish. 
- Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber. .
.
 Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the LABTEST following: 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s). 2. A large amount of bleeding 
from the incision(s) or drain(s). 3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF 
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication. . 
.
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications. 
 * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you. 
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you. 
.
****Please call to schedule your Lymphedema 
Treatment Clinic Our lymphedema therapists 
would like to see you 4 weeks after your operation and then at 3 
months after your They will formally follow 
you every 3 months for years.

Discharge Diagnosis:
chronic right upper extremity lymphedema 

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Personal Care: 
-You may keep your incisions open to air or covered with a 
clean, sterile gauze that you change daily. 
-You may shower daily with assistance as needed. You should 
cover your right arm with a plastic bag when showering. Your 
abdominal suture line can get wet. Pat dry after shower.
.
Activity: 
-You may resume your regular diet. If any symptoms of nausea, 
burping, or not passing a bowel movement in 2 days after 
discharge then resume liquid diet and take stool 
softeners/laxative until return of bowel function.
-DO NOT lift anything heavier than 5 pounds or engage in 
strenuous activity for 6 weeks following surgery. 
-Keep arm straight in splint and elevated for 1 more day and 
then you may remove splint. You should elevate your right arm 
as much as possible until follow up visit. 
.
 Medications: 
-Take aspirin 121.5 daily (1.5 baby aspirins) for 1 month after 
surgery. 
-Resume your regular medications unless instructed otherwise and 
take any new meds as ordered . 
- You may take your prescribed pain medication for moderate to 
severe pain. You may switch to Tylenol or Extra Strength Tylenol 
for mild pain as directed on the packaging. 
-Take prescription pain medications for pain not relieved by 
tylenol. 
- Take Colace, 100 mg by mouth 2 times per day, while taking the 
prescription pain medication. You may use a different 
over-the-counter stool softener if you wish. 
- Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking any 
narcotic pain medication. You may have constipation when taking 
narcotic pain medications (oxycodone, percocet, vicodin, 
hydrocodone, dilaudid, etc.); you should continue drinking 
fluids, you may take stool softeners, and should eat foods that 
are high in fiber. .
.
 Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the LABTEST following: 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s). 2. A large amount of bleeding 
from the incision(s) or drain(s). 3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF 
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication. . 
.
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications. 
 * If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you. 
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you. 
.
****Please call to schedule your Lymphedema 
Treatment Clinic Our lymphedema therapists 
would like to see you 4 weeks after your operation and then at 3 
months after your They will formally follow 
you every 3 months for years.

PATIENT SUMMARY STATEMENT
====================================== 
Ms. is a year-old speaking female with 
history
of HFpEF (EF 58%), CAD, atrial fibrillation (on Xarelto), hypertension,
and CVA while on warfarin (BI-N who presented as a
transfer from the for weight gain, edema, and fatigue
concerning for acute diastolic HF exacerbation. 

# CORONARIES: Unknown 
# PUMP: EF 58% ( )
# RHYTHM: Atrial fibrillation 

ACTIVE ISSUES: 
============== 
# Acute diastolic heart failure exacerbation 
Patient brought in by family due to concern for ~20 lb weight 
gain. Per patient's daughter, her legs, abdomen and face were 
very edematous. Dry weight was reported as 122.6 lbs and weight 
on admission at 154.32 lbs. Her volume exam was inconsistent 
with this weight gain as she did not appear floridly overloaded. 
She had no JVD, no crackles other than lower extremity edema. 
Her pro-BNP on admission was 1594. She diuresed very well with 
40mg Iv Lasix and was transitioned to Po torsemide at 30mg 
daily. 

Etiology of this exacerbation likely secondary to confusion 
regarding fluid restriction as family was reporting that they 
were encouring patient to drink at least 2L of fluids daily. Has 
otherwise been compliant with home diuretic dosing, though 
notably, may have inadequate absorption due to mild abdominal 
edema. There was no evidence of active infection, no chest 
pain/ECG changes to suggest ACS and Atrial fib with 
well-controlled rates. It was ultimately felt that patient may 
have a new dry weight as she appeared euvolemic on discharge. 
Her discharge weight was 139.33 lbs. She was discharged on 
torsemide 30mg QD, metoprolol 50mg XL twice a day, amlodipine 10mg daily 
and losartan 25mg daily.

# RLE rash
Patient had an erythematous patch on the RLE which improved
overnight and now with a 4cm nodular mass in the area. Initially
suspected cellulitis. However given improvement without 
antibiotics, no fever, leukocytosis and development of nodular 
lesion, suspected phlebitis. Lower extremity ultrasound was 
negative. Encouraged patient to apply warm compress to the area 
upon discharge.

# Hyponatremia
Patient presented with hyponatremia to 127. Etiology felt to be 
hypervolemic hyponatremia in setting of volume overload, as 
above. Her sodium improved with diuresis. She was also placed on 
a 2L fluid restriction.

# Coagulopathy
Elevated INR to 3.4 on arrival. Suspect nutritional component 
and
patient also on xarelto which can affect INR. No evidence of active
bleeding. INR was 2.4 on discharge. Patient sent home with 
who will continue to check INR.

======================================

CHADS2-VASc 9
Continued home metoprolol fractionated to 25mg Q6h and home 
rivaroxaban 20mg qpm. 

# Hypertension
Continued home amlodipine 10mg daily and losartan 25mg daily

# CVA
Admitted to BI-N in with CVA while on warfarin.
Transitioned to rivaroxaban at this time and ASA 81mg daily
discontinued. 

# PAD
# CAD
Previously on ASA 81mg daily; discontinued upon initiation of
rivaroxaban in . Continued home atorvastatin 20mg qpm and 
metoprolol as above.

# Medullary thyroid carcinoma status post thyroidectomy
# Post-surgical hypothyroidism
Continued home levothyroxine 112mcg daily.

Dear Ms. ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at 
 .

- You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart 
failure exacerbation. You family noticed that you had gained 
extra weight and your legs and face were puffier.

WHAT HAPPENED WHILE I WAS IN THE HOSPITAL?
- We gave you IV diuretics 
- We discontinued your home Lasix and started you on a 
medication called torsemide.
- We also noticed a rash on your right leg and a lump near the 
rash. We think this is due to inflammation in one of your blood 
vessels. Please put a warm compress to the area for 15 mins 3 
times a day.

WHAT SHOULD I DO WHEN I GO HOME?
- You should continue to take your medications as prescribed. 
- You should attend the appointments listed below. 
- Weigh yourself every morning, call your doctor if your weight 
goes up more than 3 lbs. 
- Please do not take in more than 2L of fluids in a day. 
- Seek medical attention if you have new or concerning symptoms 
or you develop swelling in your legs, abdominal distention, or 
shortness of breath at night. 
- Your discharge LABTEST weight: 139.33 lb. You should use this as your 
baseline after you leave the hospital. 

We wish you the best!
Your Care Team

gain. Per patient's daughter, her legs, abdomen and face were

were encouring patient to drink at least 2L of fluids daily. Has 
otherwise been compliant with home diuretic dosing, though 
notably, may have inadequate absorption due to mild abdominal 
edema. There was no evidence of active infection, no chest 
pain/ECG changes to suggest ACS and Atrial fib with

have a new dry weight as she appeared euvolemic on discharge.

overnight and now with a 4cm nodular mass in the area. Initially

# Acute diastolic heart failure exacerbation 
Patient brought in by family due to concern for ~20 lb weight 
gain. Per patient's daughter, her legs, abdomen and face were 
very edematous. Dry weight was reported as 122.6 lbs and weight 
on admission at 154.32 lbs. Her volume exam was inconsistent 
with this weight gain as she did not appear floridly overloaded. 
She had no JVD, no crackles other than lower extremity edema. 
Her pro-BNP on admission was 1594. She diuresed very well with 
40mg Iv Lasix and was transitioned to Po torsemide at 30mg 
daily. 

Etiology of this exacerbation likely secondary to confusion 
regarding fluid restriction as family was reporting that they 
were encouring patient to drink at least 2L of fluids daily. Has 
otherwise been compliant with home diuretic dosing, though 
notably, may have inadequate absorption due to mild abdominal 
edema. There was no evidence of active infection, no chest 
pain/ECG changes to suggest ACS and Atrial fib with 
well-controlled rates. It was ultimately felt that patient may 
have a new dry weight as she appeared euvolemic on discharge. 
Her discharge weight was 139.33 lbs. She was discharged on 
torsemide 30mg QD, metoprolol 50mg XL twice a day, amlodipine 10mg daily 
and losartan 25mg daily.

# RLE rash
Patient had an erythematous patch on the RLE which improved
overnight and now with a 4cm nodular mass in the area. Initially
suspected cellulitis. However given improvement without 
antibiotics, no fever, leukocytosis and development of nodular 
lesion, suspected phlebitis. Lower extremity ultrasound was 
negative. Encouraged patient to apply warm compress to the area 
upon discharge.

hypervolemic hyponatremia in setting of volume overload, as 
above. Her sodium improved with diuresis. She was also placed on 
a 2L fluid restriction.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at 
 . 

WHY WAS I ADMITTED TO THE HOSPITAL?
- You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart 
failure exacerbation. You family noticed that you had gained 
extra weight and your legs and face were puffier.

WHAT HAPPENED WHILE I WAS IN THE HOSPITAL?
- We gave you IV diuretics 
- We discontinued your home Lasix and started you on a 
medication called torsemide.
- We also noticed a rash on your right leg and a lump near the 
rash. We think this is due to inflammation in one of your blood 
vessels. Please put a warm compress to the area for 15 mins 3 
times a day.

WHAT SHOULD I DO WHEN I GO HOME?
- You should continue to take your medications as prescribed. 
- You should attend the appointments listed below. 
- Weigh yourself every morning, call your doctor if your weight 
goes up more than 3 lbs. 
- Please do not take in more than 2L of fluids in a day. 
- Seek medical attention if you have new or concerning symptoms 
or you develop swelling in your legs, abdominal distention, or 
shortness of breath at night. 
- Your discharge LABTEST weight: 139.33 lb. You should use this as your 
baseline after you leave the hospital. 

We wish you the best!
Your Care Team

Past Medical History:

changes. She underwent a cardiac cath in demonstrated no 
angiographically apparent flow-limiting lesions, mild mitral 
regurgitation, and severe systolic ventricular dysfunction with 
a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20%. Her last echo in 
 demonstrated an ejection fraction of 40-45% with 
mild-to-moderate global left ventricular hypokinesis and 
moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. 
- clean cath

Mg-1. y/o female with hepatic sarcoidosis who is status post TIPS placement 
on who presented with increasing abdominal distension. 
KUB on admission was suspicious for evidence of bowel 
obstruction and encepalopathy. She had not been taking her 
lactulose. 
An abdominal CT was also performed demonstrated:
-Complete occlusion of the left portal vein and distal branches 
of the right portal vein. The proximal right portal vein and 
main portal vein were patent.
-The TIPS was patent and enhanced appropriately.
-Splenomegaly and other signs of cirrhosis including ascites was 
noted.
-Findings were concerning for partial distal small bowel 
obstruction.
She was made NPO and an NGT was placed.
Due to the portal vein thrombus noted on CT, a heparin drip was 
initiated. This was discontinued on due to past history of 
head bleeding with aneurysms, as recommended in conjunction with 
Hepatology.
The NG tube was removed when she started passing flatus. Abdomen 
was soft and she had no abdominal pain. She tolerated a regular 
diet on . Lactulose was resumed. 

Vital signs were stable, she was ambulatory and tolerating a 
regular diet on . A follow up appointment was arranged for 
 .

(Daily).

a day).
Disp:*90 Capsule(s)* Refills:*2*
5. Amitriptyline 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at 
bedtime). 
6. Sucralfate 1 gram Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO four times a day (4 times 
a day). 
7. Diltiazem HCl 30 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day (2 
times a day). 
8. Gabapentin 300 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO Q12H (every 
12 hours). 
9. Metoclopramide 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO three times a day (3 
times a day). 
10. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg (65 mg Iron) Tablet Sig: One (1) 
Tablet PO twice a day (2 times a day). 
11. Albuterol 90 mcg/Actuation Aerosol Sig: Puffs Inhalation 
Q4H (every 4 hours) as needed. 
12. Lactulose 10 gram/15 mL Syrup Sig: Thirty (30) ML PO three times a day (3 
times a day). 
13. Folic Acid 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. 
14. Omeprazole 40 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) 
Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO twice a day.

 w/ PMH allergies, asthma, OSA, hypertension, HFpEF presented with few

eosinophilia vs COPD with possible microaspiration events.

section_text:
year old female with history of OSA, allergies/?asthma who 
presents with worsening shortness of breath/wheezing over the 
last two months. Patient is known to have allergies during the 
warmer months that respond to intra-nasal steroids. However, 
since , the patient has had persistent cough and wheezing. 
Inhaler therapy was uptitrated with no effect. In early , 
she failed an empiric course of azithromycin. A subsequent chest 
x-ray at the end of showed a right basilar opacity 
concerning for pneumonia. She was placed on prednisone and 
augmentin with some effect, but quickly had recurrence of her 
symptoms. A repeat CXR on showed resolution of the 
pneumonia. However, she called her PCP 1 week ago to report 
that her symptoms of shortness of breath had again worsened. 
Today, given lack of improvement on max inhalers, the patient 
was referred to the ED for further management.

In the ED, initial VS were temp 98.2, HR 70, BP 138/74, RR 20, 
92% RA. Exam notable for diffuse inspiratory and expiratory 
wheezes. 
Labs showed serum bicarbonate 35, creatinine 0.9, BNP 1739, 
normal CBC. UA showed few bacteria, moderate Leuk, and 14 WBC
Imaging showed low lung volume with atelectasis but no obvious 
pneumonia. Patient received several duonebs, 125mg 
methylprednisolone, IV magnesium, and oral lorazepam.
Transfer VS were HR 81, BP 143/70, RR- 18, 93% RA. 

Decision was made to admit to medicine for further management. 

On arrival to the floor, patient confirms the above history. On 
the floor, she denies previous history of asthma and states she 
only recently within the last few months began taking inhalers. 
She has a 60 pk year history of smoking but quick many years ago. No 
fevers/chills.

HOSPITAL COURSE
===============
 w/ PMH allergies, asthma, OSA, hypertension, HFpEF presented with few 
month history of worsening shortness of breath, wheezing. Patient has been seen 
by her PCP multiple times for this complaint. She has tried 
multiple antibiotic courses without improvement as well as oral 
steroids with brief response. Patient carries a presumptive 
diagnosis of asthma. On admission, patient was diffusely 
wheezing with coarse breath sounds maintaining sats in low 
on 2L NC - RA. CXR negative for signs of infection. CT chest 
most consistent with asthma/allergy with small airway 
inflammation. Patient was treated for asthma vs COPD 
exacerbation with Duonebs, Advair, oral prednisone with 60mg PO 
QD. Pulmonology was consulted who felt the patient's 
presentation likely represented atypical asthma given 
eosinophilia vs COPD with possible microaspiration events. 
Patient improved on oral steroids and maintained O2 sats on room 
air including ambulatory O2 sat of 94-95% RA. Patient was 
scheduled to follow up as an outpatient with pulmonology clinic 
and obtain formal PFTs. Speech and swallow bedside eval showed 
no evidence of aspiration. 

ACTIVE ISSUES
=============

history of asthma, on Symbicort. She has had little improvement 
with outpatient antibiotics and oral steroids. Differential was 
broad and included atypical infection, refractory obstructive 
lung disease, atypical asthma (eosinophilic asthma given 
increased eosinophils) or other inflammatory process. CXR was 
unremarkable. VBG and ABG consistent with CO2 retention with 
mild hypoxemia. CT chest consistent with asthma/allergy. Patient 
improved on oral prednisone 60mg PO QD. Plan is to tape 
prednisone 10mg Q2 days until done. Antibiotics were not given 
since no evidence of infection on history or imaging. 
Pulmonology was consulted who felt etiology may be atypical 
asthma given eosinophilia vs COPD vs possible eosinophilic 
syndromes. Presentation could also be due to microaspiration, 
however, bedside speech and swallow test normal. No evidence of 
pulmonary edema on CT. Patient was able to maintain O2 sats on 
room air at rest and with ambulation. She was discharged to 
follow up with pulm as an outpatient for formal PFTs.

inpatient.

not moving the right side of her body, eyes midline, 
unresponsive
to voice or noxious stim. They brought her to the ED, where on

arrival
she was moving both of her legs briskly, reportedly had right
facial droop. She was brought directly to CT scanner. In the
scanner, she had tonic extension of her legs followed by a

tox screen, CBC, chem 10, UA, CXR. INR was 1.4. EKG showed AFib.

noxious stim and extending legs weakly but symmetrically to
noxious.

# NEURO: 

Initially, patient called EMS from and then was unable to speak. 
 When they arrived, found her on the ground with right face and 
arm weakness and unresponsive. She was taken to the ED and had a 
GTC in the CT scanner with no focality, terminated after 
lorazepam 2mg IV, admitted to the neuro ICU. She was started on 
a heparin drip given subtherapeutic INR. CT perfusion was 
concerning for possible right temporal/parietal ischemia 
(particularly in the setting of subtherapeutic INR), but an MRI 
brain ruled out ischemic infarct. Echocardiogram demonstrated 
an EF of 55%. Her LDL was 108 and A1c was 5.9. For seizures, 
she was started on Keppra 750mg twice a day. She was transferred to the 
floor on . Lumbar puncture done, CSF without WBCs, protein 
elevated to 79, HSV PCR neg, paraneoplastic panel pending. Ms. 
 had multiple episodes of head deviation to the right, 
then GTC, followed by urnesponsivenessa nd mouth twitching. At 
that time, Keppra was increased to 1500mg twice a day, and she was 
loaded with Dilantin. EEG with focal slowing left hemisphere. 
The next day, she was confused and disoriented. As EEG was till 
acrive, she was started on Zonegran. On , she went into 
afib with RVR as well as focal motor seizures with secondary 
generalization and was transferred back to the ICU. Next day, 
was in nonconvulsive status. She was started also on Depakote 
which helped to control her seizures and she was transferred to 
the floor. Gradually, she was tapered off zonegran and 
dilantin, and remained seizure free. On discharge, she was 
stable on Depakote ER 1000mg twice a day and Keppra 1500mg twice a day. She 
should follow up in epilepsy clinic on discharge. 

 
# CARDS: The patient has known afib and was initially in afib 
with RVR in the ED which was controlled. Patient received an 
ECHO of her heart which revealed no ASD or PFO seen. On 
admission, INR was subtherapeutic and she was on a heparin drip. 
 INR fluctuated throughout the stay given titration of anti 
epileptic drugs, many of which interact with coumadin. Prior to 
discharge, patient was on heparin drip while awaiting INR to 
enter therapeutic range. In rehab, she should be on Lovenox 
70mg twice a day until INR is therapeutic (2 to 5). Coumadin dose was 
increased from 3mg qd to 5mg qd on evening, so expect to 
see effect 48 hours later.

# ID: Ucx with gram neg rods. Completed course of CTX for 
UTI (day 1 = , day = .

# Pulm: Patient had a productive cough for last 2 days of 
hospitalization. She remained afebrile, lungs clear to 
auscultation on exam, and chest xray neg for pneumonia. Most 
likely, she has a viral bronchitis, did not treat with 
antibiotics just supportive methods.

# CODE: Full (confirmed)

TRANSITIONS OF CARE:
- in rehab, will need Lovenox 70mg twice a day until INR 
- paraneoplastic antibodies in CSF pending on discharge
- will follow up in epilepsy clinic

 When they arrived, found her on the ground with right face and 
arm weakness and unresponsive. She was taken to the ED and had a 
GTC in the CT scanner with no focality, terminated after 
lorazepam 2mg IV, admitted to the neuro ICU. She was started on 
a heparin drip given subtherapeutic INR. CT perfusion was 
concerning for possible right temporal/parietal ischemia 
(particularly in the setting of subtherapeutic INR), but an MRI 
brain ruled out ischemic infarct. Echocardiogram demonstrated 
an EF of 55%. Her LDL was 108 and A1c was 5.9. For seizures, 
she was started on Keppra 750mg twice a day. She was transferred to the 
floor on . Lumbar puncture done, CSF without WBCs, protein 
elevated to 79, HSV PCR neg, paraneoplastic panel pending. Ms. 
 had multiple episodes of head deviation to the right, 
then GTC, followed by urnesponsivenessa nd mouth twitching. At 
that time, Keppra was increased to 1500mg twice a day, and she was 
loaded with Dilantin. EEG with focal slowing left hemisphere. 
The next day, she was confused and disoriented. As EEG was till 
acrive, she was started on Zonegran. On , she went into 
afib with RVR as well as focal motor seizures with secondary 
generalization and was transferred back to the ICU. Next day, 
was in nonconvulsive status. She was started also on Depakote 
which helped to control her seizures and she was transferred to 
the floor. Gradually, she was tapered off zonegran and 
dilantin, and remained seizure free. On discharge, she was 
stable on Depakote ER 1000mg twice a day and Keppra 1500mg twice a day. She 
should follow up in epilepsy clinic on discharge. 

 
# CARDS: The patient has known afib and was initially in afib 
with RVR in the ED which was controlled. Patient received an 
ECHO of her heart which revealed no ASD or PFO seen. On 
admission, INR was subtherapeutic and she was on a heparin drip. 
 INR fluctuated throughout the stay given titration of anti 
epileptic drugs, many of which interact with coumadin. Prior to 
discharge, patient was on heparin drip while awaiting INR to 
enter therapeutic range. In rehab, she should be on Lovenox 
70mg twice a day until INR is therapeutic (2 to 5). Coumadin dose was 
increased from 3mg qd to 5mg qd on evening, so expect to 
see effect 48 hours later.

# ID: Ucx with gram neg rods. Completed course of CTX for 
UTI (day 1 = , day = .

# Pulm: Patient had a productive cough for last 2 days of 
hospitalization. She remained afebrile, lungs clear to 
auscultation on exam, and chest xray neg for pneumonia. Most 
likely, she has a viral bronchitis, did not treat with 
antibiotics just supportive methods.

# CODE: Full (confirmed)

TRANSITIONS OF CARE:
- in rehab, will need Lovenox 70mg twice a day until INR 
- paraneoplastic antibodies in CSF pending on discharge
- will follow up in epilepsy clinic

# NEURO: 

Initially, patient called EMS from and then was unable to speak. 
 When they arrived, found her on the ground with right face and 
arm weakness and unresponsive. She was taken to the ED and had a 
GTC in the CT scanner with no focality, terminated after 
lorazepam 2mg IV, admitted to the neuro ICU. She was started on 
a heparin drip given subtherapeutic INR. CT perfusion was 
concerning for possible right temporal/parietal ischemia 
(particularly in the setting of subtherapeutic INR), but an MRI 
brain ruled out ischemic infarct. Echocardiogram demonstrated 
an EF of 55%. Her LDL was 108 and A1c was 5.9. For seizures, 
she was started on Keppra 750mg twice a day. She was transferred to the 
floor on . Lumbar puncture done, CSF without WBCs, protein 
elevated to 79, HSV PCR neg, paraneoplastic panel pending. Ms. 
 had multiple episodes of head deviation to the right, 
then GTC, followed by urnesponsivenessa nd mouth twitching. At 
that time, Keppra was increased to 1500mg twice a day, and she was 
loaded with Dilantin. EEG with focal slowing left hemisphere. 
The next day, she was confused and disoriented. As EEG was till 
acrive, she was started on Zonegran. On , she went into 
afib with RVR as well as focal motor seizures with secondary 
generalization and was transferred back to the ICU. Next day, 
was in nonconvulsive status. She was started also on Depakote 
which helped to control her seizures and she was transferred to 
the floor. Gradually, she was tapered off zonegran and 
dilantin, and remained seizure free. On discharge, she was 
stable on Depakote ER 1000mg twice a day and Keppra 1500mg twice a day. She 
should follow up in epilepsy clinic on discharge. 

 
# CARDS: The patient has known afib and was initially in afib 
with RVR in the ED which was controlled. Patient received an 
ECHO of her heart which revealed no ASD or PFO seen. On 
admission, INR was subtherapeutic and she was on a heparin drip. 
 INR fluctuated throughout the stay given titration of anti 
epileptic drugs, many of which interact with coumadin. Prior to 
discharge, patient was on heparin drip while awaiting INR to 
enter therapeutic range. In rehab, she should be on Lovenox 
70mg twice a day until INR is therapeutic (2 to 5). Coumadin dose was 
increased from 3mg qd to 5mg qd on evening, so expect to 
see effect 48 hours later.

# ID: Ucx with gram neg rods. Completed course of CTX for 
UTI (day 1 = , day = .

# Pulm: Patient had a productive cough for last 2 days of 
hospitalization. She remained afebrile, lungs clear to 
auscultation on exam, and chest xray neg for pneumonia. Most 
likely, she has a viral bronchitis, did not treat with 
antibiotics just supportive methods.

days before admission. CXR with possible interstitial abnormal 
in LLL; no consolidation. Empirically treated with azithromycin 
and ceftriaxone. Rapid improvement with Combivent nebs and oral 
prednisone. He was maintained on albuterol and ipratropium 
nebs. Started on prednisone 40mg, taper over 10 days. He was

reaction to ongoing pulmonary infection. Was treated empirically 
with CTX and azithromycin for two days. On discharge the CTX

.

resting HR is in the mid and has been in 120s on at least 
two outpatient visits. Work up of this sinus tachycardia was 
negative. patient has had numerous TSHs checked, all of which have 
been normal. Continued home diltiazem XR. Tachycardia resolved 
after volume repletion.
.

worked up by PCP. Weight loss has been gradual but is 
significant. He was told that he did not need further f/u of 
colon CA status post resection. The hematocrit drop likely volume 
repletion and WBC count dropped as well. Iron studies had not 
returned at time of discharge. Patient is up to date with age 
appropriate cancer screening.
.

functioning.

stop early.

antibiotics.

lightheadedness, dark or bloody stools or any other symptoms 
that are concerning to you please call your doctor or go to the 
nearest emergency room.

the resting images. Following resting images and two minutes 
following intravenous dipyridamole, approximately three times 
the resting dose of Tc-99m sestamibi was administered 
intravenously. Stress images were obtained approximately 30 
minutes following tracer injection.

the 17-segment myocardial perfusion model.

defects in the inferior, inferolateral, and apical walls.

apical akinesia. The calculated left ventricular ejection 
fraction is 36%.

IMPRESSION: New moderate and partially reversible defects in the 
inferior, 
inferolateral, and apical walls with associated wall motion 
abnormalities. 
Decreased LVEF of 36%.

 year old female with h/o LV systolic dysfunction, hypertension, HL,

asymptomatic. Cardiac markers were negative. Results of P-MIBI 
stress test are noted above, with partially reversible defects 
in lateral/apical distribution. The decision was made to 
optimize medical management during this admission. She was 
discharged on aspirin, statin, Toprol XL, and Imdur. She will

.

exam, although history may have been consistent with diastolic 
or systolic function, more likely diastolic given h/o hypertension.

continued, was continued to be held in setting of renal 
insufficiency, as creatinine remained elevated between 2.6-2.9.
. 
# RHYTHM: h/o complete heart block status post dual chamber PPM. 
currently v-paced with new lateral changes noted above. There 
were no events on telemetry. Toprol XL was continued.
.
# CKD: SCr elevated but stable at 2.8, 2.6 at discharge. 
Continued to hold at time of discharge. 
.

continue home weekly methotrexate dose
.

INTERPRETATION: This year old woman with PMH of LV 
dysfucntion, 
status post pacemaker was referred to the lab for evaluation of 
shortness of 
breath. The patient was infused with 0.142 mg/kg/min of 
dipyridamole 
over 4 minutes. No arm, neck, back or chest discomfort was 
reported by 
the patient throughout the study. The ST segments are 
uninterpretable 
for ischemia in the setting of the ventricular paced rhythm. The 
rhythm 
was sinus with ventricular pacing and rare apb and vpb. 
Appropriate 
hemodynamic response to the infusion. The dipyridamole was 
reversed 
with 125 mg of aminophylline IV.

for 
ischemia. Nuclear results reported separately.

SUMMARY FROM THE EXERCISE LAB: 
Dipyridamole was infused intravenously for 4 minutes at a dose 
of 0.142 
mg/kg/min. No anginal symptoms. EKG was uninterpretable for 
ischemic changes, due to ventricular pacing. 
 
IMAGING METHOD: 
Resting perfusion images were obtained with Tc-99m sestamibi. 
Tracer was injected approximately 45 minutes prior to obtaining 
the resting images. Following resting images and two minutes 
following intravenous dipyridamole, approximately three times 
the resting dose of Tc-99m sestamibi was administered 
intravenously. Stress images were obtained approximately 30 
minutes following tracer injection. 
Imaging protocol: Gated SPECT. This study was interpreted using 
the 17-segment myocardial perfusion model. 
 
INTERPRETATION: 
Left ventricular cavity size is normal. Rest and stress 
perfusion images reveal moderate and partially reversible 
defects in the inferior, inferolateral, and apical walls. 
 
Gated images reveal hypokinesis in this region, with complete 
apical akinesia. The calculated left ventricular ejection 
fraction is 36%. 
 
Compared with the study of , these changes are new. 
 
IMPRESSION: New moderate and partially reversible defects in the 
inferior, 
inferolateral, and apical walls with associated wall motion 
abnormalities. 
Decreased LVEF of 36%.

.

.

Continued to hold at time of discharge. 
. 
# h/o rheumatoid arthritis- continued prednisone, patient will 
continue home weekly methotrexate dose
.

.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

 underwent a successful pipeline embolization for your left 
unruptured paraopthalmic artery aneurysm

 have to be on Aspirin and Plavix daily

Instructions follow:

Activity
 may gradually return to your normal activities, but we 
recommend take it easy for the next hours to avoid 
bleeding from your groin.
•Heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise 
should be avoided for ten (10) days. This is to prevent bleeding 
from your groin.
 make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace. try to do too much all at once.
•Do not go swimming or submerge yourself in water for five (5) 
days after your procedure.
 make take a shower.
Medications
•Resume your normal medications and begin new medications as 
directed.
•It is very important to take the medication your doctor 
prescribe for to keep your blood thin and slippery. This 
will prevent clots from developing and sticking to the stent.
 may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
 are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.
•If take Metformin (Glucophage) may start it again 
three (3) days after your procedure.
Care of the Puncture Site
 will have a small bandage over the site.
•Remove the bandage in 24 hours by soaking it with water and 
gently peeling it off.
•Keep the site clean with soap and water and dry it carefully.
 may use a band-aid if wish.

What Experience:
•Mild tenderness and bruising at the puncture site (groin).
•Soreness in your arms from the intravenous lines.
•The medication may make bleed or bruise easily.
•Fatigue is very normal.
When to Call Your Doctor at for:
•Severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the puncture 
site. 
•Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit
•Constipation
•Blood in your stool or urine
•Nausea and/or vomiting

Call and go to the nearest Emergency Room if experience 
any of the following:
•Sudden numbness or weakness in the face, arm, or leg
•Sudden confusion or trouble speaking or understanding
•Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, or loss of balance or 
coordination
•Sudden severe headaches with no known reason

•Resume your normal medications and begin new medications as 
directed.
•It is very important to take the medication your doctor 
prescribe for to keep your blood thin and slippery. This 
will prevent clots from developing and sticking to the stent.
 may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor discomfort if 
 are not otherwise restricted from taking this medication.
•If take Metformin (Glucophage) may start it again 
three (3) days after your procedure.

 will have a small bandage over the site.
•Remove the bandage in 24 hours by soaking it with water and 
gently peeling it off.
•Keep the site clean with soap and water and dry it carefully.
 may use a band-aid if wish.

What Experience:
•Mild tenderness and bruising at the puncture site (groin).
•Soreness in your arms from the intravenous lines.
•The medication may make bleed or bruise easily.
•Fatigue is very normal.
When to Call Your Doctor at for:
•Severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the puncture 
site. 
•Fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit
•Constipation
•Blood in your stool or urine
•Nausea and/or vomiting

Call and go to the nearest Emergency Room if experience 
any of the following:
•Sudden numbness or weakness in the face, arm, or leg
•Sudden confusion or trouble speaking or understanding
•Sudden trouble walking, dizziness, or loss of balance or 
coordination
•Sudden severe headaches with no known reason

Gi Bleed

History of Present Illness:
The patient is a year old female with medical history 
pertinent for CLL, ITP status post splenectomy on chronic steroids, and 
history of presumed small bowel angioectasia with multiple 
admissions between and for GI bleeding. 
The patient was most recently admitted to from to 
 for GI bleeding. During that hospital course the patient 
underwent Spirus enteroscopy on which revealed a medium 
sized angioectasia in the ileum which was successfully 
cauterised using a BICAP probe. The patient was seen by 
Hematology that admission with recommendation to increase 
patient's steroids from Prednisone 10mg daily to 40mg IV 
methyleprednisolone daily with increase in platelet count from 
17,000 to 177,000. The patient was discharged on Prednisone 60mg 
daily. The patient was readmitted from to with 
symptoms of lightheadedness and fatigue with Hct of 22 and 
platelet count of 17,000. The patient was again seen by 
Hematology with recommendation to increase Prednisone from 20mg 
three times a day to 40 mg Solumedrol q12h. A repeat Spirus scope was intended 
but appropriate components were not present. 
The patient now presents from after presenting 
on with symptoms of maroon to bright rectal bleeding. 
The patient received in total 4 units RBCs and 6 units 
platelets. At time of transfer the patient is reported to have 
Hct 31 and platelet count 40,000. The patient is transferred 
from to as patient has received therapy here prior, 
consideration to be given to further therapy. Of note, the 
patient has received > 100U PRBC and platelets over the last few 
years. 
On arrival to floor patient reports her last BM was yesterday, 
reported to be dark brown/black with some maroon stool as well. 
The patient currently denies any lightheadedness, chest pain, 
dyspnea, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting. 
.

Brief Hospital Course:
. 
Assessment/Plan: The patient is a year old female with 
history of prior NHL, now with CLL and secondary ITP on chronic 
steroids with recurrent GI bleeding presumed to be secondary to 
small bowel angioectasia after extensive evaluation to date. 
. 
#. GI Bleeding: Patient has recurrent bleeding in setting of 
presumed predisposing lesions of angioectasia and chronic 
thrombocytopenia. Patient clearly needs optimal management of 
her thrombocytopenia in the long run given treatment options of 
angioectasia may be limited. The patient was seen by the GI 
service who thought interventional management was limited at 
this time, and that ongoing GI bleeding would only be optimally 
treated with better control of her thrombocytopenia. During this 
admission, she displayed no evidence of ongoign GI bleeding, 
though she had a stable platelet count and a slowly downtrending 
hematocrit. She was continued on carafate at time of discharge 
given known gastric angioectasias and prednison use. 
. 
#. Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura: Patient still with 
significant low platelet count contributing to ongoing bleeding. 
Prior Heme notes reviewed, patient has previously received 
Rituxan, now status post splenectomy. Impression per prior Hem 
consulting team was that the patient would qualify for N-plate 
as well as immuran or cytoxan plus steroids although this 
decision was deferred to the patient's outpatient Hematologist. 
Of, patient has received innumerable blood transfusions to date 
related to chronic bleeding and more definitive therapy seems 
warranted at this time. At admission, she was initiated on IV 
Solumedrol and displaued no evidence of bleeding. Hematology was 
consulted for ongoing evaluation and management. Per reports of 
smear, platelets do appear atypical for ITP and her outpatient 
hematologist was contacted. The patient's own doctor was on 
vacation, but the Heme fellow and attending spoke with Dr. 
 covering physician and discussed their 
 for one of thrombopoietin mimetics, either 
romiplostim (administered SC weekly) or eltrombopag 
(administered daily by mouth); these
agents would be used as temporizing measures to hopefully get 
her
platelet count up to ~50K and allow tapering of the 
corticosteroids. The covering hematologist was agreeable to 
starting these medications as an outpatient. The patient was 
discharged home on prednisone 30 twice a day and was to contact Dr. 
 on day of discahrge.

#. CLL: Active issue related to ITP as above, otherwise no acute 
issues were addressed in house

day).

Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO once a day.
Disp:*30 Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.)(s)* Refills:*2*

Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
1) GI Bleed
2) ITP
3) CLL

 
Discharge Condition:
Stable; without evidence of bleeding

 
Discharge Instructions:
You were transferred from with a recurrent GI 
Bleed.
.
We made adjustments to your prednisone dosage. You should resume 
your regular medications. Given your long term use of steroids, 
you should also take calcium supplements and IU of 
vitamin D daily.
.
Please call your doctor if you develop dark stools, blood in 
your stool, or bright red blood per rectum. You should have him 
continue to follow your blood counts.

history of prior NHL, now with CLL and secondary ITP on chronic 
steroids with recurrent GI bleeding presumed to be secondary to 
small bowel angioectasia after extensive evaluation to date. 
. 
#. GI Bleeding: Patient has recurrent bleeding in setting of 
presumed predisposing lesions of angioectasia and chronic 
thrombocytopenia. Patient clearly needs optimal management of 
her thrombocytopenia in the long run given treatment options of 
angioectasia may be limited. The patient was seen by the GI 
service who thought interventional management was limited at 
this time, and that ongoing GI bleeding would only be optimally 
treated with better control of her thrombocytopenia. During this 
admission, she displayed no evidence of ongoign GI bleeding, 
though she had a stable platelet count and a slowly downtrending 
hematocrit. She was continued on carafate at time of discharge 
given known gastric angioectasias and prednison use. 
. 
#. Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura: Patient still with 
significant low platelet count contributing to ongoing bleeding. 
Prior Heme notes reviewed, patient has previously received 
Rituxan, now status post splenectomy. Impression per prior Hem 
consulting team was that the patient would qualify for N-plate 
as well as immuran or cytoxan plus steroids although this 
decision was deferred to the patient's outpatient Hematologist. 
Of, patient has received innumerable blood transfusions to date 
related to chronic bleeding and more definitive therapy seems 
warranted at this time. At admission, she was initiated on IV 
Solumedrol and displaued no evidence of bleeding. Hematology was 
consulted for ongoing evaluation and management. Per reports of 
smear, platelets do appear atypical for ITP and her outpatient 
hematologist was contacted. The patient's own doctor was on 
vacation, but the Heme fellow and attending spoke with Dr. 
 covering physician and discussed their 
 for one of thrombopoietin mimetics, either

agents would be used as temporizing measures to hopefully get 
her
platelet count up to ~50K and allow tapering of the 
corticosteroids. The covering hematologist was agreeable to 
starting these medications as an outpatient. The patient was

 on day of discahrge.

section_text:
The patient is a year old female with medical history 
pertinent for CLL, ITP status post splenectomy on chronic steroids, and 
history of presumed small bowel angioectasia with multiple 
admissions between and for GI bleeding. 
The patient was most recently admitted to from to 
 for GI bleeding. During that hospital course the patient 
underwent Spirus enteroscopy on which revealed a medium 
sized angioectasia in the ileum which was successfully 
cauterised using a BICAP probe. The patient was seen by 
Hematology that admission with recommendation to increase 
patient's steroids from Prednisone 10mg daily to 40mg IV 
methyleprednisolone daily with increase in platelet count from 
17,000 to 177,000. The patient was discharged on Prednisone 60mg 
daily. The patient was readmitted from to with 
symptoms of lightheadedness and fatigue with Hct of 22 and 
platelet count of 17,000. The patient was again seen by 
Hematology with recommendation to increase Prednisone from 20mg 
three times a day to 40 mg Solumedrol q12h. A repeat Spirus scope was intended 
but appropriate components were not present. 
The patient now presents from after presenting 
on with symptoms of maroon to bright rectal bleeding. 
The patient received in total 4 units RBCs and 6 units 
platelets. At time of transfer the patient is reported to have 
Hct 31 and platelet count 40,000. The patient is transferred 
from to as patient has received therapy here prior, 
consideration to be given to further therapy. Of note, the 
patient has received > 100U PRBC and platelets over the last few 
years. 
On arrival to floor patient reports her last BM was yesterday, 
reported to be dark brown/black with some maroon stool as well. 
The patient currently denies any lightheadedness, chest pain, 
dyspnea, abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting. 
.
