Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. was admitted to the hospital and taken to the 
Operating Room where he underwent a minimally invasive 
 esophagectomy and jejunostomy tube placement with 
pericardial fat pad buttress. He tolerated the procedure well 
and returned to the PACU in stable condition. He maintained 
stable hemodynamics and his pain was controlled with an 
epidural. He underwent vigorous pulmonary toilet and was able to 
use his incentive spirometer effectively. He began J tube 
feedings on post op day #1 and his blood sugars were followed 
closely as he advanced.

Following transfer to the Surgical floor he developed some 
confusion and removed his nasogastric tube inadvertently. It 
remained out as serial chest xrays showed no dilatation of his 
gastric conduit. The Geriatric service followed him as well to 
recommend appropriate treatment for some post op confusion. 
Within 24 hours he improved with additional sleep and family 
support.

He remained NPO until when he underwent a barium 
swallow. The swallow study showed no leak and he began modest 
amounts of clear liquids which he tolerated well. He developed a 
temperature of 101 on that day with a WBC of 11.2. Urine and 
blood cultures were done and levaquin was started. His urine 
culture was positive for >100K Klebsiella Oxytoca and his 
antibiotic was switched to Ciprofloxicin. He remained afebrile 
and his WBC remained 11K. He had no urinary symptoms once his 
catheter was removed. 

He was tolerating a full liquid diet and will advance to soft 
foods tomorrow. His J tube feedings were cycled over 14 hours 
(Glucerna 1.5 @ 95 cc's/hr). He had some trouble with 
constipation which resolved with Dulcolax tabs.

His blood sugars were elevated and covered with sliding scale 
regular insulin but his pre op Levimir will be resumed today. 

The Physical Therapy service was consulted but he really had no 
needs as he was up and walking independently and steady. His 
right chest incisions were healing well and his J tube site was 
clean. services were set up to help with home tube feedings 
and education and he was discharged to home on and will 
follow up with Dr. in 2 weeks.

Operating Room where he underwent a minimally invasive 
 esophagectomy and jejunostomy tube placement with

stable hemodynamics and his pain was controlled with an 
epidural. He underwent vigorous pulmonary toilet and was able to 
use his incentive spirometer effectively. He began J tube 
feedings on post op day #1 and his blood sugars were followed 
closely as he advanced.

confusion and removed his nasogastric tube inadvertently. It 
remained out as serial chest xrays showed no dilatation of his 
gastric conduit. The Geriatric service followed him as well to 
recommend appropriate treatment for some post op confusion.

support.

catheter was removed.

constipation which resolved with Dulcolax tabs.

regular insulin but his pre op Levimir will be resumed today.

right chest incisions were healing well and his J tube site was

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. was admitted to the hospital and taken to the 
Operating Room where he underwent a minimally invasive 
 esophagectomy and jejunostomy tube placement with 
pericardial fat pad buttress. He tolerated the procedure well 
and returned to the PACU in stable condition. He maintained 
stable hemodynamics and his pain was controlled with an 
epidural. He underwent vigorous pulmonary toilet and was able to 
use his incentive spirometer effectively. He began J tube 
feedings on post op day #1 and his blood sugars were followed 
closely as he advanced.

Following transfer to the Surgical floor he developed some 
confusion and removed his nasogastric tube inadvertently. It 
remained out as serial chest xrays showed no dilatation of his 
gastric conduit. The Geriatric service followed him as well to 
recommend appropriate treatment for some post op confusion. 
Within 24 hours he improved with additional sleep and family 
support.

He remained NPO until when he underwent a barium 
swallow. The swallow study showed no leak and he began modest 
amounts of clear liquids which he tolerated well. He developed a 
temperature of 101 on that day with a WBC of 11.2. Urine and 
blood cultures were done and levaquin was started. His urine 
culture was positive for >100K Klebsiella Oxytoca and his 
antibiotic was switched to Ciprofloxicin. He remained afebrile 
and his WBC remained 11K. He had no urinary symptoms once his 
catheter was removed. 

He was tolerating a full liquid diet and will advance to soft 
foods tomorrow. His J tube feedings were cycled over 14 hours 
(Glucerna 1.5 @ 95 cc's/hr). He had some trouble with 
constipation which resolved with Dulcolax tabs.

His blood sugars were elevated and covered with sliding scale 
regular insulin but his pre op Levimir will be resumed today. 

The Physical Therapy service was consulted but he really had no 
needs as he was up and walking independently and steady. His 
right chest incisions were healing well and his J tube site was 
clean. services were set up to help with home tube feedings 
and education and he was discharged to home on and will 
follow up with Dr. in 2 weeks.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. was admitted to the hospital and taken to the 
Operating Room where he underwent a minimally invasive 
 esophagectomy and jejunostomy tube placement with 
pericardial fat pad buttress. He tolerated the procedure well 
and returned to the PACU in stable condition. He maintained 
stable hemodynamics and his pain was controlled with an 
epidural. He underwent vigorous pulmonary toilet and was able to 
use his incentive spirometer effectively. He began J tube 
feedings on post op day #1 and his blood sugars were followed 
closely as he advanced.

Following transfer to the Surgical floor he developed some 
confusion and removed his nasogastric tube inadvertently. It 
remained out as serial chest xrays showed no dilatation of his 
gastric conduit. The Geriatric service followed him as well to 
recommend appropriate treatment for some post op confusion. 
Within 24 hours he improved with additional sleep and family 
support.

He remained NPO until when he underwent a barium 
swallow. The swallow study showed no leak and he began modest 
amounts of clear liquids which he tolerated well. He developed a 
temperature of 101 on that day with a WBC of 11.2. Urine and 
blood cultures were done and levaquin was started. His urine 
culture was positive for >100K Klebsiella Oxytoca and his 
antibiotic was switched to Ciprofloxicin. He remained afebrile 
and his WBC remained 11K. He had no urinary symptoms once his 
catheter was removed. 

He was tolerating a full liquid diet and will advance to soft 
foods tomorrow. His J tube feedings were cycled over 14 hours 
(Glucerna 1.5 @ 95 cc's/hr). He had some trouble with 
constipation which resolved with Dulcolax tabs.

His blood sugars were elevated and covered with sliding scale 
regular insulin but his pre op Levimir will be resumed today. 

The Physical Therapy service was consulted but he really had no 
needs as he was up and walking independently and steady. His 
right chest incisions were healing well and his J tube site was 
clean. services were set up to help with home tube feedings 
and education and he was discharged to home on and will 
follow up with Dr. in 2 weeks.

Name: Unit No: 
 
Admission Date: Discharge Date: 
 
Date of Birth: Sex: F
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
Sulfa (Sulfonamide Antibiotics) / sotalol
 
Attending: .
 
Chief Complaint:
Hemothorax
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
 - Attempted Left Subclavian Vein Port-A-Cath Insertion
 - Thoracentesis
 - Right Internal Jugular Vein Single Lumen Chest Power 
Port-A-Cath Placement by Interventional Radiology
 
History of Present Illness:
Ms. is a with PMH of leiomyosarcoma and metastatic 
melanoma who went for a left subclavian port placement today 
which was complicated by placement difficulty leading to 
termination prior to port placement.

Per surgery signout, she was placed under conscious sedation for 
L subclavian port placement for metastatic melanoma. The wire 
wouldn't travel down the vein properly, and the patient 
experienced fluctuating heart rates throughout the procedure. 
Given failure to advance the wire despite several attempts and 
the heart rate variability, the procedure was terminated. She 
maintained adequate O2 sats and was HD stable during the 
procedure. Post-procedure, there was concern for possible venous 
perforation with new R hemothorax on CXR. A CT chest w/o 
contrast showed a new right moderate pleural effusion containing 
high-density material since , concerning for acute 
blood products. She was asymptomatic but given concern about an 
intrathoracic bleed she is being transferred to the FICU for 
further management. 
 
Per surgery, interventional pulmonology would like to place a 
pigtail drain on morning. They state that they will 
contact IP in the morning and there is nothing for us to do.

On arrival to the MICU, she states that she has right lower back 
pain which is new since her surgery and is worse with movement 
and with breathing. She also reports a chronic cough which has 
worsened since receiving nivolumab as well as new fatigue since 
that same time. She otherwise has no symptoms and denies any CP, 
shortness of breath, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, N/V, constipation, diarrhea.
 
Past Medical History:
Atrial Fibrillation ablation , no longer on anticoag
CAD: Positive stress test fall , (cardiol Giasuddin)
cervical cancer
Hypertension
OSA
GERD
cough
bruit
 
PSH

 . You were admitted to the hospital due

 30 minutes before your appointment with Interventional

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at the 
 . You were admitted to the hospital due 
to a complication after your port placement in which you had 
some bleeding into your right lung.

Due to the concern for bleeding you were monitored closely in 
the ICU. You had a chest tube placed that helped to drain out 
some of the blood. Your blood counts were monitored closely and 
you had no evidence of continued bleeding.

You likely had fluid buildup due to your Lasix being held. You 
were given intravenous Lasix with improvement in your breathing 
and fluid. However, despite this you continued to need 
supplemental oxygen particularly when ambulating. You had a 
repeat chest CT scan which did not show any evidence of blood 
clots but did note persistent fluid in the base of your right 
lung which likely represents old blood. After discussion with 
the pulmonary and thoracic surgery teams it was recommended to 
hold off on any further procedures or surgery at this time given 
your need for minimal supplemental oxygen, the small size of the 
effusion, and not feeling short of breath. Hopefully the fluid 
will diminish and you came come off oxygen in a short time.

You were scheduled to follow-up in the Multidisciplinary 
Thoracic Surgery/Interventional Pulmonary Clinic. You should go 
to the Radiology department on floor of the 
 30 minutes before your appointment with Interventional 
Pulmonary on for a chest x-ray and check in at the 
desk (please have the x-ray done around 3PM).

You will also follow-up with your Oncologist on to resume 
your immunotherapy.

Please resume your home medications.

Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.

All the best,
Your Team

Brief Hospital Course:
 with PMH of leiomyosarcoma and metastatic melanoma who 
presents to the FICU after a failed port placement on c/b 
likely right hemothorax

# Hemothorax: Patient developed right pleural effusion 
concerning for hemothorax following placement of port on as 
demonsrated on post-procedure chest X-ray. Per procedure report, 
team had difficulty advancing wire. Hemothorax possibly 
developed to venous perforation in the setting of multiple 
port placement attempts. Of interest, the port was attempted to 
be placed on the left side of the chest with development of a 
right-sided pleural effusion. CT chest showed right pleural 
effusion with density consistent with blood. Interventional 
pulmonology was consulted who placed a right sided chest pigtail 
drain at the bedside with drainage of 300-400cc of bright red 
blood over the course of hospital day 1. Patient had CBC checks 
Q4Hs with stabilization of post-procedure hemoglobin of 
approximately 10. A second CT chest on showed decrease in 
size of the patient's hemothorax. She otherwise remained 
hemodynamically stable and was transferred to the floor. Chest 
tube pulled . HD and H/H stable. Given persistent O2 
requirement a CTA chest was done which was negative for PE but 
did show persistent small to moderate right pleueral effusion. 
IP was re-consulted and assessed effusion at bedside via 
ultrasound but given size and location was not safe to drain at 
bedside. After discussion with IP and Thoracic Surgery, they 
recommended no further interventions at this time with hope of 
fluid reabsorption and recommended follow-up in 
 clinic.

# Acute Hypoxic Respiratory Failure: Patient requiring 
supplement O2 since admission. Desaturation yesterday to mid-80s 
on RA while ambulating. Requiring 1L supplemental O2 while at 
rest or exertion however not symptomatic. Likely secondary to 
hemothorax and volume overload in the setting of baseline poor 
pulmonary status given untreated sleep apnea, previous left lung 
lobectomy, and large body habitus. She was diuressed with IV 
lasix and given nebs. As she had not been on DVT prophlyaxis in 
the setting of bleed, a CTA chest was done which was negative 
for PE but did show persistent right hemorrhagic pleural 
effusion. She was discharged with home oxygen and will follow-up 
with Pulmonary/Thoracics.

# Atrial Fibrillation with RVR: On diltiazem ER and metoprolol 
tartrate for rate control and warfarin for anticoagulation. Per 
patient report, she has had elevated heart rates over the past 
month and is supposed to see her Afib specialist within a week 
or two. She is currently asymptomatic. Diltiazem dose was 
fractionated and reduced to 30mg Q6H given concern for bleed. 
Metoprolol 25mg Q6H was staggered with diltiazem for addtional 
rate control. Anticoagulation held given bleed. She was 
discharged on her home metoprolol and diltizazem with good rate 
control. Her warfarin was restarted prior to discharge.

# Acute on Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure. Patient with EF >60% 
in . Weight increased since admission while Lasix on hold 
during ICU stay. On transfer from ICU to floor patient weight 
294.1 lb. Per patient, baseline weight about 185 lbs. Patient 
diuressed with several doses of IV lasix to her dry weight. She 
was discharged on her home lasix.

# hypertension: Pressures currently well-controlled. Held home valsartan 
due to concern for bleed which was restarted at time of 
discharge.

# Metastatic Melanoma: As above, diagnosed in 
predominantly in the RLL. Now being treated with single-agent 
nivolumab q2 weeks. Followed by Dr. . Single lumen 
port placed by on . She will follow-up in clinic on 
 .

# Leiomyosarcoma: Previously resected. Dr. and 
felt that adjuvant therapy was not indicated. She does have a 
RUL lung lesion which may be a metastatic focus of 
leiomyosarcoma, however it was felt that biopsy was not 
indicated at this time unless it grows in size.

# GERD: Continued ranitidine.

# Delayed Gastric Emptying: Continued metoclopramide 10 mg 
QACHS.

====================
Transitional Issues:
====================
- Please discharged on supplemental O2 likely due to right 
hemorrhagic pleural effusion. Patient with follow-up with 
Interventional Pulmonary and Thoracic Surgery with repeat chest 
x-ray for further management of effusion. Please continue to 
monitor oxygen saturation and titrate O2 as needed.
- Patient discharged with oxycodone for pain related to her port 
placement procedure.
- Please ensure follow-up with Oncology for immunotherapy.
- Patient had right IJ Port-A-Cath placed by Interventional 
Radiology on .

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at the 
 . You were admitted to the hospital due 
to a complication after your port placement in which you had 
some bleeding into your right lung.

Due to the concern for bleeding you were monitored closely in 
the ICU. You had a chest tube placed that helped to drain out 
some of the blood. Your blood counts were monitored closely and 
you had no evidence of continued bleeding.

You likely had fluid buildup due to your Lasix being held. You 
were given intravenous Lasix with improvement in your breathing 
and fluid. However, despite this you continued to need 
supplemental oxygen particularly when ambulating. You had a 
repeat chest CT scan which did not show any evidence of blood 
clots but did note persistent fluid in the base of your right 
lung which likely represents old blood. After discussion with 
the pulmonary and thoracic surgery teams it was recommended to 
hold off on any further procedures or surgery at this time given 
your need for minimal supplemental oxygen, the small size of the 
effusion, and not feeling short of breath. Hopefully the fluid 
will diminish and you came come off oxygen in a short time.

You were scheduled to follow-up in the Multidisciplinary 
Thoracic Surgery/Interventional Pulmonary Clinic. You should go 
to the Radiology department on floor of the 
 30 minutes before your appointment with Interventional 
Pulmonary on for a chest x-ray and check in at the 
desk (please have the x-ray done around 3PM).

You will also follow-up with your Oncologist on to resume 
your immunotherapy.

Please resume your home medications.

Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.

All the best,
Your Team

hemithorax is due to substantial increasein or new right pleural

chronic left mediastinal shift. Heart is not enlarged. Volume 
of left pleural effusion is indeterminate but it is not large. 
There is no pneumothorax.

CT Chest w/o Contrast 
1. Moderate, layering hemorrhagic right pleural effusion, new 
since corresponds to findings on conventional chest 
radiograph earlier this afternoon.
2. Minimal edema or bleeding from recent instrumentation, left 
subclavian region.
3. Expected postsurgical changes are noted following left 
thoracotomy and left lower lobectomy.
4. Metastatic lung and pleural nodules are unchanged since prior 
exam in .

CT Chest w/o Contrast 
Impression: Interval decrease in hemorrhagic right pleural 
effusion. Stable lung nodules, pleural nodules and lymph nodes 
about gastroesophageal junction.

CTA Chest 
1. No evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic abnormality.
2. Bilateral pleural effusions, small on the right and trace on 
the left.
3. Right lower lobe consolidation which could reflect 
atelectasis due to the adjacent pleural effusion, though an 
infectious process cannot be excluded in the right clinical 
setting. Additional tiny focus of consolidation in the right 
upper lobe which may reflect an infectious or inflammatory 
process.
4. Expected postsurgical anatomy and volume loss after left 
lower lobectomy. Unchanged metastatic disease. No new nodule or 
mass identified.
 
Brief Hospital Course:
 with PMH of leiomyosarcoma and metastatic melanoma who 
presents to the FICU after a failed port placement on c/b 
likely right hemothorax

# Hemothorax: Patient developed right pleural effusion 
concerning for hemothorax following placement of port on as 
demonsrated on post-procedure chest X-ray. Per procedure report, 
team had difficulty advancing wire. Hemothorax possibly 
developed to venous perforation in the setting of multiple 
port placement attempts. Of interest, the port was attempted to 
be placed on the left side of the chest with development of a 
right-sided pleural effusion. CT chest showed right pleural 
effusion with density consistent with blood. Interventional 
pulmonology was consulted who placed a right sided chest pigtail 
drain at the bedside with drainage of 300-400cc of bright red 
blood over the course of hospital day 1. Patient had CBC checks 
Q4Hs with stabilization of post-procedure hemoglobin of 
approximately 10. A second CT chest on showed decrease in 
size of the patient's hemothorax. She otherwise remained 
hemodynamically stable and was transferred to the floor. Chest 
tube pulled . HD and H/H stable. Given persistent O2 
requirement a CTA chest was done which was negative for PE but 
did show persistent small to moderate right pleueral effusion. 
IP was re-consulted and assessed effusion at bedside via 
ultrasound but given size and location was not safe to drain at 
bedside. After discussion with IP and Thoracic Surgery, they 
recommended no further interventions at this time with hope of 
fluid reabsorption and recommended follow-up in 
 clinic.

# Acute Hypoxic Respiratory Failure: Patient requiring 
supplement O2 since admission. Desaturation yesterday to mid-80s 
on RA while ambulating. Requiring 1L supplemental O2 while at 
rest or exertion however not symptomatic. Likely secondary to 
hemothorax and volume overload in the setting of baseline poor 
pulmonary status given untreated sleep apnea, previous left lung 
lobectomy, and large body habitus. She was diuressed with IV 
lasix and given nebs. As she had not been on DVT prophlyaxis in 
the setting of bleed, a CTA chest was done which was negative 
for PE but did show persistent right hemorrhagic pleural 
effusion. She was discharged with home oxygen and will follow-up 
with Pulmonary/Thoracics.

# Atrial Fibrillation with RVR: On diltiazem ER and metoprolol 
tartrate for rate control and warfarin for anticoagulation. Per 
patient report, she has had elevated heart rates over the past 
month and is supposed to see her Afib specialist within a week 
or two. She is currently asymptomatic. Diltiazem dose was 
fractionated and reduced to 30mg Q6H given concern for bleed. 
Metoprolol 25mg Q6H was staggered with diltiazem for addtional 
rate control. Anticoagulation held given bleed. She was 
discharged on her home metoprolol and diltizazem with good rate 
control. Her warfarin was restarted prior to discharge.

# Acute on Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure. Patient with EF >60% 
in . Weight increased since admission while Lasix on hold 
during ICU stay. On transfer from ICU to floor patient weight 
294.1 lb. Per patient, baseline weight about 185 lbs. Patient 
diuressed with several doses of IV lasix to her dry weight. She 
was discharged on her home lasix.

# hypertension: Pressures currently well-controlled. Held home valsartan 
due to concern for bleed which was restarted at time of 
discharge.

# Metastatic Melanoma: As above, diagnosed in 
predominantly in the RLL. Now being treated with single-agent 
nivolumab q2 weeks. Followed by Dr. . Single lumen 
port placed by on . She will follow-up in clinic on 
 .

# Leiomyosarcoma: Previously resected. Dr. and 
felt that adjuvant therapy was not indicated. She does have a 
RUL lung lesion which may be a metastatic focus of 
leiomyosarcoma, however it was felt that biopsy was not 
indicated at this time unless it grows in size.

# GERD: Continued ranitidine.

# Delayed Gastric Emptying: Continued metoclopramide 10 mg 
QACHS.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

It was a pleasure taking care of you at the 
 . You were admitted to the hospital due 
to a complication after your port placement in which you had 
some bleeding into your right lung.

Due to the concern for bleeding you were monitored closely in 
the ICU. You had a chest tube placed that helped to drain out 
some of the blood. Your blood counts were monitored closely and 
you had no evidence of continued bleeding.

You likely had fluid buildup due to your Lasix being held. You 
were given intravenous Lasix with improvement in your breathing 
and fluid. However, despite this you continued to need 
supplemental oxygen particularly when ambulating. You had a 
repeat chest CT scan which did not show any evidence of blood 
clots but did note persistent fluid in the base of your right 
lung which likely represents old blood. After discussion with 
the pulmonary and thoracic surgery teams it was recommended to 
hold off on any further procedures or surgery at this time given 
your need for minimal supplemental oxygen, the small size of the 
effusion, and not feeling short of breath. Hopefully the fluid 
will diminish and you came come off oxygen in a short time.

You were scheduled to follow-up in the Multidisciplinary 
Thoracic Surgery/Interventional Pulmonary Clinic. You should go 
to the Radiology department on floor of the 
 30 minutes before your appointment with Interventional 
Pulmonary on for a chest x-ray and check in at the 
desk (please have the x-ray done around 3PM).

You will also follow-up with your Oncologist on to resume 
your immunotherapy.

Please resume your home medications.

Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.

All the best,
Your Team

Major Surgical or Invasive LABTEST Procedure: 1. Irrigation debridement left wrist.
2. Left wrist arthrotomy with synovectomy

 
History of Present Illness:
 w/PMHx spondyloarthropathy and 1 prior episode septic joint, 
reports L wrist pain of sudden onset x 2 days. He reports that 
the pain is similar to a prior wrist septic arthritis in . 
Denies trauma. The onset of pain was accompanied by redness and 
swelling. Per Rheumatology referral, an arthrocentesis attempted 
in clinic yesterday, but no fluid was aspirated; the patient was 
referred to the ED. In the ED. the patient reports significant 
anxiety. Denies systemic symptoms, no n/v/d/f/c. Denies chest 
pain, abdominal pain, shortness of breath. 

 
Past Medical History:
spondyloarthropathy on Methotrexate and sulfsalazine 
Hypertension 
glucose intolerance 
osteopenia 
low back pain 
PSH 
 - Arthroscopic lavage right wrist for septic joint 
 - Excision of right medial elbow varicose vein 

 
Social History:
 
Family History:
Multiple family members with RA. Mother - breast cancer, no 
heart dz.

HEENT: EOMI, PERRL, Mouth and Mucous Membranes Dry

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the plastic surgery service on 
 and had a left L wrist arthrocentesis and washout. The 
patient tolerated the procedure well. 

Neuro: Post-operatively, the patient received Dilaudid IV/PCA 
with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral 
intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications. 

CV: The patient was stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.

Pulmonary: The patient was stable from a pulmonary standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.

GI/GU: Post-operatively, the patient was given IV fluids until 
tolerating oral intake. His diet was advanced when appropriate, 
which was tolerated well. He was also started on a bowel regimen 
to encourage bowel movement. Intake and output were closely 
monitored. 
 
ID: Post-operatively, the patient was started on IV vancomycin 
and Ciprofloxacin PO, then switched to PO Bactrim on POD#2. The 
patient's temperature was closely watched for signs of 
infection. 

Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin during 
this stay, and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as 
possible. 
 
At the time of discharge on POD#2, the patient was doing well, 
afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, 
ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled.

Mr. was admitted to the service of Dr. for a 
thoracolumbar fusion for kyphoscoliosis. He was informed and 
consented and elected to proceed. Please see Operative Note for 
procedure in detail.

volume maintenance and neuro checks. He was given antibiotics 
and pain medication. A hemovac drain was placed

procedure in detail.

volume maintenance and neuro checks. He was given antibiotics

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ,

You were directly admitted to from home after you 
developed worsening shortness of breath and weight gain. We 
started you on IV diuretic medications, but you quickly 
developed kidney damage with low urine output. We stopped giving 
you diuretics for a few days and your kidney function recovered. 
We did a right heart cath which showed that the right side of 
your heart wasn't working properly, making you very sensitive to 
the presence and absence of fluids in your body. We were a 
little concerned several times during your admission that you 
appeared a little confused. We think this might have been due to 
your opiate use, and we recommend that you continue to use less 
amounts of these medications for your back pain at home and that 
you continue to discuss these doses with your PCP. We are also 
sending you home with a new medication called Narcan which you 
should use in the setting of an emergency if you breathing rate 
slows from using too many narcotics. 

We think that your "dry weight" LABTEST is: 123.5 kg
Please weigh yourself every morning, and call your PCP or 
 if your weight goes up more than 3 lbs in a 24 hour 
period or 5lbs in a 72 hour period.

Please have your labs checked at a lab that is convenient for 
you on . These should be sent to Dr. 
review. 

It was a pleasure taking care of you!

Your Cardiology Team

before being transitioned to Torsemide 100mg daily on discharge.

allergy.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ,

You were directly admitted to from home after you 
developed worsening shortness of breath and weight gain. We 
started you on IV diuretic medications, but you quickly 
developed kidney damage with low urine output. We stopped giving 
you diuretics for a few days and your kidney function recovered. 
We did a right heart cath which showed that the right side of 
your heart wasn't working properly, making you very sensitive to 
the presence and absence of fluids in your body. We were a 
little concerned several times during your admission that you 
appeared a little confused. We think this might have been due to 
your opiate use, and we recommend that you continue to use less 
amounts of these medications for your back pain at home and that

sending you home with a new medication called Narcan which you

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ,

You were directly admitted to from home after you 
developed worsening shortness of breath and weight gain. We 
started you on IV diuretic medications, but you quickly 
developed kidney damage with low urine output. We stopped giving 
you diuretics for a few days and your kidney function recovered. 
We did a right heart cath which showed that the right side of 
your heart wasn't working properly, making you very sensitive to 
the presence and absence of fluids in your body. We were a 
little concerned several times during your admission that you 
appeared a little confused. We think this might have been due to 
your opiate use, and we recommend that you continue to use less 
amounts of these medications for your back pain at home and that 
you continue to discuss these doses with your PCP. We are also 
sending you home with a new medication called Narcan which you 
should use in the setting of an emergency if you breathing rate 
slows from using too many narcotics.

Dear Mr. ,

You were directly admitted to from home after you 
developed worsening shortness of breath and weight gain. We 
started you on IV diuretic medications, but you quickly 
developed kidney damage with low urine output. We stopped giving

your heart wasn't working properly, making you very sensitive to 
the presence and absence of fluids in your body. We were a 
little concerned several times during your admission that you 
appeared a little confused. We think this might have been due to 
your opiate use, and we recommend that you continue to use less 
amounts of these medications for your back pain at home and that 
you continue to discuss these doses with your PCP. We are also 
sending you home with a new medication called Narcan which you 
should use in the setting of an emergency if you breathing rate 
slows from using too many narcotics.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ,

You were directly admitted to from home after you 
developed worsening shortness of breath and weight gain. We 
started you on IV diuretic medications, but you quickly 
developed kidney damage with low urine output. We stopped giving 
you diuretics for a few days and your kidney function recovered. 
We did a right heart cath which showed that the right side of 
your heart wasn't working properly, making you very sensitive to 
the presence and absence of fluids in your body. We were a 
little concerned several times during your admission that you 
appeared a little confused. We think this might have been due to 
your opiate use, and we recommend that you continue to use less 
amounts of these medications for your back pain at home and that 
you continue to discuss these doses with your PCP. We are also 
sending you home with a new medication called Narcan which you 
should use in the setting of an emergency if you breathing rate 
slows from using too many narcotics. 

We think that your "dry weight" LABTEST is: 123.5 kg
Please weigh yourself every morning, and call your PCP or 
 if your weight goes up more than 3 lbs in a 24 hour 
period or 5lbs in a 72 hour period.

you on . These should be sent to Dr. 
review.

Your Cardiology Team

History of Present Illness:
 year old female with HCV cirrhosis complicated by HE and 
ascites, chronic abdominal pain, and recent admission for new 
right sided colitis brought in by her daughter for disorganized 
speech, auditory hallucinations, and anxiety for 3 days. Though 
she does have some confusion at baseline, her daughter reports 
that she has never seen the patient this severe before. The 
voices are telling her to repeat herself and she is not making 
sense. She denies SI/HI and any substance use. She states she 
has been compliant with her lactulose, but her daughter reports 
otherwise. She continues to have her chronic abdominal pain, 
which she has had for decades, but denies vomiting, fever, 
chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations. Her last BM was this 
morning. She has been "racing" for the past three days and has 
been unable to sleep. She reports increased anxiety and 
depression, and felt as if she was "about to freak out". She 
reports not acting like her normal self. Daughter reports that 
patient has been seeking to spend time with and talk with all of 
her children because voices in her head are telling her to do 
so.
She was previously hospitalized here just a few weeks ago 
( ) with acute on chronic abdominal pain, fever, and 
altered
mental status with radiographic evidence of right-sided colitis. 
She was treated with ceftriaxone and flagyl until HOD 2 when 
they
were discontinued, with subsequent improvement in her abdominal 
pain. Colonoscopy showed normal mucosa throughout the TI and 
colon. Biopsies were taken and additional polyp was biopsied, 
showing normal colonic mucosa without colitis and fragments of 
an adenoma. However, given concern for IBD, the patient was 
started on mesalamine prior to discharge. Her altered mental 
status was felt to be secondary to hepatic encephalopathy, 
although she continued to report compliance. She was treated 
with rifaximin and lactulose, with negative infectious work-up, 
and mental status improved by discharge.
Of note, she was on triple therapy for her chronic hepatitis C 
with excellent virologic response. She unfortunately developed 
severe anemia and colitis requiring admission. Her therapy was 
stopped and her virus relapsed. Given an elevated MELD at the 
time of her last discharge, plan was to initiate the transplant 
evaluation at her last outpatient appointment.
In the ED, initial vitals were 98.6 97 186/68 18 100% on rA. She 
repeated "liver acceptance, liver acceptance, liver acceptance. 
need to slow down" throughout the ED assessment. She was 
initially very anxious and agitated, yelling at times,

at 82. CXR without acute process. RUQ u/s with patent veins. ED 
resident reported that they did not see good pocket for 
paracentesis on u/s. She was placed on a 1:1 sitter for racing 
thoughts and auditory hallucinations. Most recent vitals prior 
to transfer: 82 172/78 14 98% on RA.

depression

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted due to confusion, which possibly represented 
"hepatic encephalopathy," or confusion due to liver disease. 
Your exam and vital signs were reassuring, and a medical workup 
showed that you do not have an infection. You slept well 
overnight and are much more oriented so you are being discharged 
home. 

We increased your Lactulose dose to prevent confusion; you 
should increase or decrease the frequency of the medication to 
ensure that you have bowel movements daily. Please do not 
take Loperamide unless you are having >5 bowel movements in a 
day.

(Zolpidem) to be used in the case of severe insomnia. You can 
try tab and if that doesn't work you can take the other 
tab.

right sided colitis brought in by her daughter for disorganized

been unable to sleep. She reports increased anxiety and

reports not acting like her normal self. Daughter reports that

pain. Colonoscopy showed normal mucosa throughout the TI and

and mental status improved by discharge.

time of her last discharge, plan was to initiate the transplant 
evaluation at her last outpatient appointment.

 year old female with HCV cirrhosis complicated by HE and 
ascites, chronic abdominal pain, and recent admission for new 
right sided colitis brought in by her daughter for disorganized 
speech, auditory hallucinations, and anxiety for 3 days. Though 
she does have some confusion at baseline, her daughter reports 
that she has never seen the patient this severe before. The 
voices are telling her to repeat herself and she is not making 
sense. She denies SI/HI and any substance use. She states she 
has been compliant with her lactulose, but her daughter reports 
otherwise. She continues to have her chronic abdominal pain, 
which she has had for decades, but denies vomiting, fever, 
chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations. Her last BM was this 
morning. She has been "racing" for the past three days and has 
been unable to sleep. She reports increased anxiety and 
depression, and felt as if she was "about to freak out". She 
reports not acting like her normal self. Daughter reports that 
patient has been seeking to spend time with and talk with all of 
her children because voices in her head are telling her to do 
so.
She was previously hospitalized here just a few weeks ago 
( ) with acute on chronic abdominal pain, fever, and 
altered
mental status with radiographic evidence of right-sided colitis. 
She was treated with ceftriaxone and flagyl until HOD 2 when 
they
were discontinued, with subsequent improvement in her abdominal 
pain. Colonoscopy showed normal mucosa throughout the TI and 
colon. Biopsies were taken and additional polyp was biopsied, 
showing normal colonic mucosa without colitis and fragments of 
an adenoma. However, given concern for IBD, the patient was 
started on mesalamine prior to discharge. Her altered mental 
status was felt to be secondary to hepatic encephalopathy, 
although she continued to report compliance. She was treated 
with rifaximin and lactulose, with negative infectious work-up, 
and mental status improved by discharge.
Of note, she was on triple therapy for her chronic hepatitis C 
with excellent virologic response. She unfortunately developed 
severe anemia and colitis requiring admission. Her therapy was 
stopped and her virus relapsed. Given an elevated MELD at the 
time of her last discharge, plan was to initiate the transplant 
evaluation at her last outpatient appointment.

resident reported that they did not see good pocket for 
paracentesis on u/s. She was placed on a 1:1 sitter for racing 
thoughts and auditory hallucinations. Most recent vitals prior 
to transfer: 82 172/78 14 98% on RA.

 year old female with decompensated HCV cirrhosis complicated 
by encephalopathy and ascites and chronic abdominal pain, 
brought in by her daughter for disorganized speech, auditory 
hallucinations, and anxiety for 3 days

# Altered mental status (Delirium): Resolved overnight. Given 
her recent admission, liver failure, and particularly the 
insomnia and that she's never had psych symptoms like these 
before, our highest suspicion was that this is was mild hepatic 
encephalopathy, with secondary possibility of an early 
adjustment-type episode on underlying depression and anxiety 
about her diagnosis. Patient also having severe incomnia. Other 
toxic metabolic workup has been negative (including infectious). 
Time course too short for mania. Patient no longer symptomatic, 
and no SI/HI. Treated with lactulose/rifaximin for 
encephalopathy. After discussion with patient and attending, 
Ambien was chosen as sleep aid as only an occasional, as needed 
medication if she truly cannot sleep by 1 or 2 AM. Continued 
fluoxetine. She was back at baseline by discharge after close 
monitoring.

# HCV cirrhosis: Previously c/b ascites, encephalopathy. EGD 
without varices. Now with bilateral edema. Currently 
decompensated. MELD 17. Continued lasix/spironolactone, 
lactulose and rifaximin.
# Right sided colitis: biopsies without evidence of colitis, CT 
findings only. Per Dr. knows this patient, she 
had done well whenever mesalamine has been started, and has 
colitis type symptoms when it is stoppped, so continued it.
# Chronic abdominal pain: Treated with as needed tylenol, less than 2 
grams max per day
# h/o esophagus: continued home PPI
# Hypertension: continued home atenolol

2. Fluoxetine 60 mg PO DAILY 
3. Furosemide 20 mg PO DAILY 
4. Lactulose 30 mL PO three times a day 
titrate to bowel movements a day 
5. Mesalamine 500 mg PO three times a day 
6. Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H

ONLY USE THIS IF YOU ARE HAVING MORE THAN 5 BOWEL MOVEMENTS 
DAILY.

DO NOT DRIVE OR OPERATE MACHINERY WHILE ON THIS.

Home

depression

You were admitted due to confusion, which possibly represented 
"hepatic encephalopathy," or confusion due to liver disease. 
Your exam and vital signs were reassuring, and a medical workup 
showed that you do not have an infection. You slept well 
overnight and are much more oriented so you are being discharged 
home. 

We increased your Lactulose dose to prevent confusion; you 
should increase or decrease the frequency of the medication to 
ensure that you have bowel movements daily. Please do not 
take Loperamide unless you are having >5 bowel movements in a 
day.

In addition, we are giving you a small supply of Ambien 
(Zolpidem) to be used in the case of severe insomnia. You can 
try tab and if that doesn't work you can take the other 
tab.

Your exam and vital signs were reassuring, and a medical workup 
showed that you do not have an infection. You slept well 
overnight and are much more oriented so you are being discharged 
home.

should increase or decrease the frequency of the medication to 
ensure that you have bowel movements daily. Please do not 
take Loperamide unless you are having >5 bowel movements in a 
day.

try tab and if that doesn't work you can take the other 
tab.

Ms. is a year old woman with a history notable for HCV 
and EtOH cirrhosis (complicated by portal hypertension & hepatic
encephalopathy, Childs A), unspecified colitis, and non-small 
cell lung cancer who presented to the ED with 2 days of 
progressive abdominal pain and was found on CT to have 
uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for perforation, 
abscess or fistula. She was treated with antibiotics as detailed 
below. 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
==================

ACTIVE/ACUTE ISSUES: 
==================== 
#Abdominal pain
#Diverticulitis 
CT scan shows uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for 
abscess, perforation or fistula. Diverticulitis is most likely 
the source of her abdominal pain. Other work-up has included US 
showing no portal vein thrombosis, non elevated lipase or 
amylase. Patient was initially started on Meropenem for empiric 
coverage of GI flora due to allergy to metronidazole and 
penicillins, but was switched to IV Unasyn on . She was 
transitioned to PO Augmentin on as she was clinically 
stable and was discharged on a total 14-day course of 
antibiotics (end date . Serial abdominal exams were 
performed to assess treatment response. Blood cultures and urine 
cultures showed no growth to date. Patient received ondansetron 
for nausea and her diet was advanced as tolerated. She received 
IV morphine while inpatient, but was noted to be somnolent with 
low respiratory rate and hypoxemia to the on which 
ultimately required Narcan. After that, she was continued on 
acetaminophen and tramadol only for pain control. Patient should 
have colonoscopy 6-weeks post discharge (around . 

#Diarrhea
Patient developed watery diarrhea on , despite skipping 
several doses of lactulose. She also reported subjective fevers 
and chills, worsening RLQ pain and decreased appetite. She did 
not have objective fevers and she was leukopenic as below. 
Differential included antibiotic-induced diarrhea and C 
difficile infection. Stool was tested for C-diff and was 
negative. As such, seems most likely to be antibiotic-induced 
diarrhea. Patient was advised to eat yogurt and other sources of 
probiotics while on Augmentin.

#Constipation
CT scan also showing constipation. Patient normally has 4 BMs 
taking lactulose but noted decreased quantity over past few days 
prior to admission. Her home lactulose dose was increased, and 
miralax, bisacodyl, suppositories and enemas were added to her 
bowel regimen with resolution of constipation prior to 
discharge. Unfortunately she developed diarrhea as detailed 
above.

#Cirrhosis, Childs A 
Her LFTs were at baseline and on exam she was cognitively intact 
and without signs of encephalopathy or ascites. Concern for SBP 
was low given no ascites on CT or exam and she was with a clear 
source of her symptoms (diverticulitis). No concern for 
decompensated cirrhosis during her hospitalization. No ascites 
to tap on CT and therefore did not undergo paracentesis. Her 
LFTs and CBC were trended daily and remained stable. She was 
continued on home lactulose, rifaximin, furosemide and 
spironolactone. 

#Colitis, NOS
In the past patient carries the diagnosis of presumed colitis. 
However there is no prior pathology studies showing evidence of 
colitis. She had been intermittently on and off Mesalamine. Per 
patient her MD recently took her off mesalamine and therefore 
this was not continued on discharge. Her colitis had been under 
control. There was no evidence of surgical abdomen, perforation 
or peritonitis during her hospitalization. Patient should 
discuss with PCP whether or not mesalamine should be restarted.

===============

Appears chronically low and at about baseline during admission. 
At discharge, she had a WBC of 1.4.

currently on any chemotherapy or immunotherapy. She has been

 .

#Abdominal pain
#Diverticulitis 
CT scan shows uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for 
abscess, perforation or fistula. Diverticulitis is most likely 
the source of her abdominal pain. Other work-up has included US 
showing no portal vein thrombosis, non elevated lipase or 
amylase. Patient was initially started on Meropenem for empiric 
coverage of GI flora due to allergy to metronidazole and 
penicillins, but was switched to IV Unasyn on . She was 
transitioned to PO Augmentin on as she was clinically 
stable and was discharged on a total 14-day course of 
antibiotics (end date . Serial abdominal exams were 
performed to assess treatment response. Blood cultures and urine 
cultures showed no growth to date. Patient received ondansetron 
for nausea and her diet was advanced as tolerated. She received 
IV morphine while inpatient, but was noted to be somnolent with 
low respiratory rate and hypoxemia to the on which 
ultimately required Narcan. After that, she was continued on 
acetaminophen and tramadol only for pain control. Patient should 
have colonoscopy 6-weeks post discharge (around . 

#Diarrhea
Patient developed watery diarrhea on , despite skipping 
several doses of lactulose. She also reported subjective fevers 
and chills, worsening RLQ pain and decreased appetite. She did 
not have objective fevers and she was leukopenic as below. 
Differential included antibiotic-induced diarrhea and C 
difficile infection. Stool was tested for C-diff and was 
negative. As such, seems most likely to be antibiotic-induced 
diarrhea. Patient was advised to eat yogurt and other sources of 
probiotics while on Augmentin.

CT scan also showing constipation. Patient normally has 4 BMs 
taking lactulose but noted decreased quantity over past few days 
prior to admission. Her home lactulose dose was increased, and 
miralax, bisacodyl, suppositories and enemas were added to her 
bowel regimen with resolution of constipation prior to 
discharge. Unfortunately she developed diarrhea as detailed 
above.

Her LFTs were at baseline and on exam she was cognitively intact 
and without signs of encephalopathy or ascites. Concern for SBP 
was low given no ascites on CT or exam and she was with a clear 
source of her symptoms (diverticulitis). No concern for 
decompensated cirrhosis during her hospitalization. No ascites 
to tap on CT and therefore did not undergo paracentesis. Her 
LFTs and CBC were trended daily and remained stable. She was 
continued on home lactulose, rifaximin, furosemide and 
spironolactone.

In the past patient carries the diagnosis of presumed colitis. 
However there is no prior pathology studies showing evidence of 
colitis. She had been intermittently on and off Mesalamine. Per 
patient her MD recently took her off mesalamine and therefore 
this was not continued on discharge. Her colitis had been under 
control. There was no evidence of surgical abdomen, perforation 
or peritonitis during her hospitalization. Patient should 
discuss with PCP whether or not mesalamine should be restarted.

Ms. is a year old woman with a history notable for HCV
and EtOH cirrhosis (complicated by portal hypertension & hepatic
encephalopathy), unspecified colitis, and non-small cell lung
cancer who presented to the ED with 2 days of progressive
abdominal pain. She also reports fever last night ( ) up
to . The pain is primarily located in the right lower
quadrant. She rates the pain as . She has not tried anything
for the pain. Nothing seems to make it worse. Described as achy
but intermittenly sharp. There is no association with eating.
There is associated nausea but no vomiting. She is chronically 
on
lactulose. She reports peripheral edema at baseline, as well as
chronic dyspnea on exertion. She also notes increased confusion
over the past 2 days. 
On arrival to the ED, she got a CT abdomen showing uncomplicated
diverticulitis without concern for abscess, perforation or
fistula. Liver US showed no portal vein thrombosis. CXR showed 
no
acute cardiopulmonary process. Her LFTs and CBC were at 
baseline.
She got one dose of IV morphine for pain control. 
On arrival to the floor, the patient reports continued abdominal
pain, nausea, bloating and light-headedness. She denies 
anorexia,
chest pain, shortness of breath and dysuria.

=====================
Ms. is a year old woman with a history notable for HCV 
and EtOH cirrhosis (complicated by portal hypertension & hepatic
encephalopathy, Childs A), unspecified colitis, and non-small 
cell lung cancer who presented to the ED with 2 days of 
progressive abdominal pain and was found on CT to have 
uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for perforation, 
abscess or fistula. She was treated with antibiotics as detailed 
below. 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
==================
[ ] Schedule colonoscopy for 6-weeks post discharge (last 
colonoscopy was should occur around 
[ ] Mesalamine- discuss whether patient should be restarted on 
medication (was recently discontinued)
[ ] Please note that patient is sensitive to opioids
[ ] Follow up constipation and ensure having bowel movements 
daily
[ ] Will need repeat CBC/BMP/LFTs/INR at next appointment to 
monitor leukopenia and cirrhosis
[ ] Patient to end 14-day course of Augmentin on . 

ACTIVE/ACUTE ISSUES: 
==================== 
#Abdominal pain
#Diverticulitis 
CT scan shows uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for 
abscess, perforation or fistula. Diverticulitis is most likely 
the source of her abdominal pain. Other work-up has included US 
showing no portal vein thrombosis, non elevated lipase or 
amylase. Patient was initially started on Meropenem for empiric 
coverage of GI flora due to allergy to metronidazole and 
penicillins, but was switched to IV Unasyn on . She was 
transitioned to PO Augmentin on as she was clinically 
stable and was discharged on a total 14-day course of 
antibiotics (end date . Serial abdominal exams were 
performed to assess treatment response. Blood cultures and urine 
cultures showed no growth to date. Patient received ondansetron 
for nausea and her diet was advanced as tolerated. She received 
IV morphine while inpatient, but was noted to be somnolent with 
low respiratory rate and hypoxemia to the on which 
ultimately required Narcan. After that, she was continued on 
acetaminophen and tramadol only for pain control. Patient should 
have colonoscopy 6-weeks post discharge (around . 

#Diarrhea
Patient developed watery diarrhea on , despite skipping 
several doses of lactulose. She also reported subjective fevers 
and chills, worsening RLQ pain and decreased appetite. She did 
not have objective fevers and she was leukopenic as below. 
Differential included antibiotic-induced diarrhea and C 
difficile infection. Stool was tested for C-diff and was 
negative. As such, seems most likely to be antibiotic-induced 
diarrhea. Patient was advised to eat yogurt and other sources of 
probiotics while on Augmentin.

#Constipation
CT scan also showing constipation. Patient normally has 4 BMs 
taking lactulose but noted decreased quantity over past few days 
prior to admission. Her home lactulose dose was increased, and 
miralax, bisacodyl, suppositories and enemas were added to her 
bowel regimen with resolution of constipation prior to 
discharge. Unfortunately she developed diarrhea as detailed 
above.

#Cirrhosis, Childs A 
Her LFTs were at baseline and on exam she was cognitively intact 
and without signs of encephalopathy or ascites. Concern for SBP 
was low given no ascites on CT or exam and she was with a clear 
source of her symptoms (diverticulitis). No concern for 
decompensated cirrhosis during her hospitalization. No ascites 
to tap on CT and therefore did not undergo paracentesis. Her 
LFTs and CBC were trended daily and remained stable. She was 
continued on home lactulose, rifaximin, furosemide and 
spironolactone. 

#Colitis, NOS
In the past patient carries the diagnosis of presumed colitis. 
However there is no prior pathology studies showing evidence of 
colitis. She had been intermittently on and off Mesalamine. Per 
patient her MD recently took her off mesalamine and therefore 
this was not continued on discharge. Her colitis had been under 
control. There was no evidence of surgical abdomen, perforation 
or peritonitis during her hospitalization. Patient should 
discuss with PCP whether or not mesalamine should be restarted. 

CHRONIC ISSUES: 
=============== 
#Leukopenia: DDx includes infection vs medication-induced. 
Appears chronically low and at about baseline during admission. 
At discharge, she had a WBC of 1.4.

#Depression: Continued home paroxetine.

#H/o Non-small cell Lung cancer, status post resection: She is not 
currently on any chemotherapy or immunotherapy. She has been 
following up with radiation oncology at .

=====================
Ms. is a year old woman with a history notable for HCV 
and EtOH cirrhosis (complicated by portal hypertension & hepatic
encephalopathy, Childs A), unspecified colitis, and non-small 
cell lung cancer who presented to the ED with 2 days of 
progressive abdominal pain and was found on CT to have 
uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for perforation, 
abscess or fistula. She was treated with antibiotics as detailed 
below. 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
==================
[ ] Schedule colonoscopy for 6-weeks post discharge (last 
colonoscopy was should occur around 
[ ] Mesalamine- discuss whether patient should be restarted on 
medication (was recently discontinued)
[ ] Please note that patient is sensitive to opioids
[ ] Follow up constipation and ensure having bowel movements 
daily
[ ] Will need repeat CBC/BMP/LFTs/INR at next appointment to 
monitor leukopenia and cirrhosis
[ ] Patient to end 14-day course of Augmentin on . 

ACTIVE/ACUTE ISSUES: 
==================== 
#Abdominal pain
#Diverticulitis 
CT scan shows uncomplicated diverticulitis without concern for 
abscess, perforation or fistula. Diverticulitis is most likely 
the source of her abdominal pain. Other work-up has included US 
showing no portal vein thrombosis, non elevated lipase or 
amylase. Patient was initially started on Meropenem for empiric 
coverage of GI flora due to allergy to metronidazole and 
penicillins, but was switched to IV Unasyn on . She was 
transitioned to PO Augmentin on as she was clinically 
stable and was discharged on a total 14-day course of 
antibiotics (end date . Serial abdominal exams were 
performed to assess treatment response. Blood cultures and urine 
cultures showed no growth to date. Patient received ondansetron 
for nausea and her diet was advanced as tolerated. She received 
IV morphine while inpatient, but was noted to be somnolent with 
low respiratory rate and hypoxemia to the on which 
ultimately required Narcan. After that, she was continued on 
acetaminophen and tramadol only for pain control. Patient should 
have colonoscopy 6-weeks post discharge (around . 

#Diarrhea
Patient developed watery diarrhea on , despite skipping 
several doses of lactulose. She also reported subjective fevers 
and chills, worsening RLQ pain and decreased appetite. She did 
not have objective fevers and she was leukopenic as below. 
Differential included antibiotic-induced diarrhea and C 
difficile infection. Stool was tested for C-diff and was 
negative. As such, seems most likely to be antibiotic-induced 
diarrhea. Patient was advised to eat yogurt and other sources of 
probiotics while on Augmentin.

#Constipation
CT scan also showing constipation. Patient normally has 4 BMs 
taking lactulose but noted decreased quantity over past few days 
prior to admission. Her home lactulose dose was increased, and 
miralax, bisacodyl, suppositories and enemas were added to her 
bowel regimen with resolution of constipation prior to 
discharge. Unfortunately she developed diarrhea as detailed 
above.

#Cirrhosis, Childs A 
Her LFTs were at baseline and on exam she was cognitively intact 
and without signs of encephalopathy or ascites. Concern for SBP 
was low given no ascites on CT or exam and she was with a clear 
source of her symptoms (diverticulitis). No concern for 
decompensated cirrhosis during her hospitalization. No ascites 
to tap on CT and therefore did not undergo paracentesis. Her 
LFTs and CBC were trended daily and remained stable. She was 
continued on home lactulose, rifaximin, furosemide and 
spironolactone. 

#Colitis, NOS
In the past patient carries the diagnosis of presumed colitis. 
However there is no prior pathology studies showing evidence of 
colitis. She had been intermittently on and off Mesalamine. Per 
patient her MD recently took her off mesalamine and therefore 
this was not continued on discharge. Her colitis had been under 
control. There was no evidence of surgical abdomen, perforation 
or peritonitis during her hospitalization. Patient should 
discuss with PCP whether or not mesalamine should be restarted. 

CHRONIC ISSUES: 
=============== 
#Leukopenia: DDx includes infection vs medication-induced. 
Appears chronically low and at about baseline during admission. 
At discharge, she had a WBC of 1.4.

#Depression: Continued home paroxetine.

#H/o Non-small cell Lung cancer, status post resection: She is not 
currently on any chemotherapy or immunotherapy. She has been 
following up with radiation oncology at .

Dear Ms. , 

It was a pleasure to care for you at the 
 . 

Why did you come to the hospital? 
- You came to the hospital because you had abdominal pain and 
fevers. 

What did you receive in the hospital? 
- You had imaging of your abdomen which showed an infection of 
your colon, called diverticulitis.
- You received antibiotics to treat the infection in your 
abdomen.
- You were found to be very sensitive to Morphine, and we had to 
use a reversal medication because you became confused
- You also received medications to help relieve your 
constipation.
- You developed diarrhea and we tested you for a bacterial 
infection in your stool. The test was negative. Please take some 
probiotics (or yogurt) while taking the antibiotic. 

What should you do once you leave the hospital? 
- You should attend all your scheduled follow-up appointments.
- You should take your medications as prescribed, especially the 
antibiotics to treat the infection.

We wish you the best! 
Your Care Team

Brief Hospital Course:
 yo F presents with AMS and CT found SAH. 

#aSAH
#Strokes 
Patient had coil embolization of basilar apex aneurysm. The 
procedure was uncomplicated, please see OMR for detailed 
operative report. She was kept intubated after the OR and 
transferred to PACU for post-anesthesia monitoring. A Right EVD 
was placed in PACU and leveled at 15. She remained 
hemodynamically and neurologically stable on post-operative 
check. A foley was placed intraoperatively, and was initially 
removed, however given patients declining neurological status 
was replaced for strict I&O monitoring. Patient went to for 
management after the OR pending a Neuro ICU bed. On POD 1 
patient was doing well. She remained intubated but was able to 
participate in a full neurologic exam with sedation held. She 
was euvolemic. On Nimodipine and Keppra. Patient was extubated 
 during the evening. Early morning , patient self 
removed her EVD. She was given 1 dose of Ancef. STAT CT showed 
stable ventricle size with no hematoma and mild pneumocephalus. 
Repeat head CT for increased lethargy and new upgaze restriction 
was stable. Keppra was discontinued . Additional STAT CT on 
 in the setting of lethargy was stable. Repeat CT on 
was essentially stable. She was started on phenylephrine on 
to maximize CPP with goal SBP 140-200. CTA on showed 
vasospasm in the P1 and P2, superior division of right M2, and 
left M1, left M2, stable to improved from prior imaging. SBP 
goal was narrowed to 160-200. Levophed, milrinone, and 
vasopressin were additionally used to achieve this SBP goal. 
This SBP goal was later relaxed back to 140-200. She went for 
angio on , which showed mild vasospasm. She received IA 
nitro and verapamil during the procedure. SBP goal was further 
relaxed to > 120 and medications were weaned. Repeat CT on 
showed new bilateral occipital strokes and slight increase in 
ventricular size. EVD was replaced given concern for hydro 
as cause of strokes. Post placement CT stable. CT for 
plegia x4 extremities, showed MCA/PCA watershed infarcts with 
left caudate infarct. EVD was clamped on and removed later 
that night given high need for anticoagulation for cardiac. 
Repeat head CT stable. MRI brain for prognostication 
showed diffuse areas of ischemia involving the bilateral 
parietooccipital lobes, left insular region, right cerebellar 
lobe and cerebellar vermis. CT head with contrast was obtained 
without signs of infection. Repeat MRI brain with thin cuts was 
ordered on per neuroradiology. MRI performed and revealed 
bilateral cerebral punctate infarcts otherwise stable imaging. 
TCD on was negative for vasospasm. Patients exam remained 
poor and was ordered for an MRI Cspine which was performed on 
 and showed no evidence of compression to explain plegia. 
Vitamin B12 level was slightly elevated but not enough to 
contribute to patients exam status. She was put on cEEG 
overnight on to evaluate for seizures and showed diffuse 
slowing/burst of GRDA, but no seizures.

#Afib with RVR
The patient was put on metoprolol for her afib with RVR. She 
required an esmolol drip for more strict management. LENIs were 
ordered given tachycardia, and were negative. She was 
subsequently loaded with amiodarone and esmolol was weaned off. 
She was later loaded with amiodarone again as well as digoxin. 
Metoprolol and digoxin with discontinued on later per Cards. She 
was continued on amiodarone per Cards. TEE was ordered. 
Amiodarone was stopped on , and the patient was started on a 
diltiazem drip. She was also started on a heparin drip for afib. 
Heparin gtt discontinued for placement of EVD given 
patient's mental status. TTE on revealed EF of 70%, 
tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary hypertension but no 
thrombus. Heparin gtt was restarted . Patient taken for a 
cardioversion on x2 and was unsuccessful. Patient remained 
in rapid AFib status post the procedure on . She was placed on 
amiodarione drip which was transitioned to PO on . Repeat 
cardioversion was discussed with cardiology and planned for 
 .

#Pulmonary edema/Respiratory failure
The patient was found to be hypoxic with flash pulmonary edema 
on chest x-ray on . She received Lasix. She was reintubated 
on given continued respiratory compromise. She continued to 
receive Lasix as needed. Repeat chest x-ray on was improved. 
Chest xray on with decreased amount of pulmonary edema. On 
 , she was noted to have increased use of accessory muscles; 
ABG showed respiratory acidosis. Vent settings were adjusted 
and she was diuresed. Sedation was adjusted due to vent 
dysynchrony. Patient continued to require intermittent as needed Lasix 
due to pulmonary effusions and congestion/edema, most recently 
shown on CXR from . 

#Fevers, leukocytosis
The patient was febrile and leukocytic while admitted. She was 
pancultured and put on empiric antibiotics. She was 
repancultured on in the setting of fever. She continued to 
have fevers intermittently. She was started on empiric 
Vancomycin. Febrile up to 103 on , cooling blanket and ice 
packs applied as needed. CSF sent , which was negative for 
growth. C.diff negative. Flagyl was started for ongoing 
fever. All cultures negative. Vanc/Flagyl were discontinued on 
 . BAL repeated . Cefepime was restarted for 
ongoing fevers and leukocytosis. She was febrile again . BAL 
grew coag positive staph. Cdiff was sent and she was started on 
PO Vanco pending result. Cdiff resulted as negative and PO 
vanco was discontinued. She was febrile again on and was 
pancultured.

#DVT
Patient's RUE was noted to be edematous on and a duplex was 
performed. Duplex revealed a partial non-occlusive thrombus in 
distal right axillary, full occlusion thrombus in right basilic, 
partial thrombus in right cephalic. 

#Hypernatremia
The patient became hypernatremic as a result of diuresis. FWF 
were increased as needed. D51/2NS was started on . 

#Loose stools
Flexiseal was placed on for loose stools. C. diff negative 
on .

#Dispo
 were consulted and recommended long term nursing facility. 
Patients family was involved in multiple family meetings to 
discuss patient diagnosis, prognosis and goals of care. Another 
family meeting was held on and family ultimately decided to 
transition to CMO. She was terminally extubated on at 5pm 
and expired at 04:59 on .

Brief Hospital Course:
 yo F presents with AMS and CT found SAH. 

#aSAH
#Strokes 
Patient had coil embolization of basilar apex aneurysm. The 
procedure was uncomplicated, please see OMR for detailed 
operative report. She was kept intubated after the OR and 
transferred to PACU for post-anesthesia monitoring. A Right EVD 
was placed in PACU and leveled at 15. She remained 
hemodynamically and neurologically stable on post-operative 
check. A foley was placed intraoperatively, and was initially 
removed, however given patients declining neurological status 
was replaced for strict I&O monitoring. Patient went to for 
management after the OR pending a Neuro ICU bed. On POD 1 
patient was doing well. She remained intubated but was able to 
participate in a full neurologic exam with sedation held. She 
was euvolemic. On Nimodipine and Keppra. Patient was extubated 
 during the evening. Early morning , patient self 
removed her EVD. She was given 1 dose of Ancef. STAT CT showed 
stable ventricle size with no hematoma and mild pneumocephalus. 
Repeat head CT for increased lethargy and new upgaze restriction 
was stable. Keppra was discontinued . Additional STAT CT on 
 in the setting of lethargy was stable. Repeat CT on 
was essentially stable. She was started on phenylephrine on 
to maximize CPP with goal SBP 140-200. CTA on showed 
vasospasm in the P1 and P2, superior division of right M2, and 
left M1, left M2, stable to improved from prior imaging. SBP 
goal was narrowed to 160-200. Levophed, milrinone, and 
vasopressin were additionally used to achieve this SBP goal. 
This SBP goal was later relaxed back to 140-200. She went for 
angio on , which showed mild vasospasm. She received IA 
nitro and verapamil during the procedure. SBP goal was further 
relaxed to > 120 and medications were weaned. Repeat CT on 
showed new bilateral occipital strokes and slight increase in 
ventricular size. EVD was replaced given concern for hydro 
as cause of strokes. Post placement CT stable. CT for 
plegia x4 extremities, showed MCA/PCA watershed infarcts with 
left caudate infarct. EVD was clamped on and removed later 
that night given high need for anticoagulation for cardiac. 
Repeat head CT stable. MRI brain for prognostication 
showed diffuse areas of ischemia involving the bilateral 
parietooccipital lobes, left insular region, right cerebellar 
lobe and cerebellar vermis. CT head with contrast was obtained 
without signs of infection. Repeat MRI brain with thin cuts was 
ordered on per neuroradiology. MRI performed and revealed 
bilateral cerebral punctate infarcts otherwise stable imaging. 
TCD on was negative for vasospasm. Patients exam remained 
poor and was ordered for an MRI Cspine which was performed on 
 and showed no evidence of compression to explain plegia. 
Vitamin B12 level was slightly elevated but not enough to 
contribute to patients exam status. She was put on cEEG 
overnight on to evaluate for seizures and showed diffuse 
slowing/burst of GRDA, but no seizures.

#Afib with RVR
The patient was put on metoprolol for her afib with RVR. She 
required an esmolol drip for more strict management. LENIs were 
ordered given tachycardia, and were negative. She was 
subsequently loaded with amiodarone and esmolol was weaned off. 
She was later loaded with amiodarone again as well as digoxin. 
Metoprolol and digoxin with discontinued on later per Cards. She 
was continued on amiodarone per Cards. TEE was ordered. 
Amiodarone was stopped on , and the patient was started on a 
diltiazem drip. She was also started on a heparin drip for afib. 
Heparin gtt discontinued for placement of EVD given 
patient's mental status. TTE on revealed EF of 70%, 
tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary hypertension but no 
thrombus. Heparin gtt was restarted . Patient taken for a 
cardioversion on x2 and was unsuccessful. Patient remained 
in rapid AFib status post the procedure on . She was placed on 
amiodarione drip which was transitioned to PO on . Repeat 
cardioversion was discussed with cardiology and planned for 
 .

#Pulmonary edema/Respiratory failure
The patient was found to be hypoxic with flash pulmonary edema 
on chest x-ray on . She received Lasix. She was reintubated 
on given continued respiratory compromise. She continued to 
receive Lasix as needed. Repeat chest x-ray on was improved. 
Chest xray on with decreased amount of pulmonary edema. On 
 , she was noted to have increased use of accessory muscles; 
ABG showed respiratory acidosis. Vent settings were adjusted 
and she was diuresed. Sedation was adjusted due to vent 
dysynchrony. Patient continued to require intermittent as needed Lasix 
due to pulmonary effusions and congestion/edema, most recently 
shown on CXR from . 

#Fevers, leukocytosis
The patient was febrile and leukocytic while admitted. She was 
pancultured and put on empiric antibiotics. She was 
repancultured on in the setting of fever. She continued to 
have fevers intermittently. She was started on empiric 
Vancomycin. Febrile up to 103 on , cooling blanket and ice 
packs applied as needed. CSF sent , which was negative for 
growth. C.diff negative. Flagyl was started for ongoing 
fever. All cultures negative. Vanc/Flagyl were discontinued on 
 . BAL repeated . Cefepime was restarted for 
ongoing fevers and leukocytosis. She was febrile again . BAL 
grew coag positive staph. Cdiff was sent and she was started on 
PO Vanco pending result. Cdiff resulted as negative and PO 
vanco was discontinued. She was febrile again on and was 
pancultured.

#DVT
Patient's RUE was noted to be edematous on and a duplex was 
performed. Duplex revealed a partial non-occlusive thrombus in 
distal right axillary, full occlusion thrombus in right basilic, 
partial thrombus in right cephalic. 

#Hypernatremia
The patient became hypernatremic as a result of diuresis. FWF 
were increased as needed. D51/2NS was started on . 

#Loose stools
Flexiseal was placed on for loose stools. C. diff negative 
on .

#Dispo
 were consulted and recommended long term nursing facility. 
Patients family was involved in multiple family meetings to 
discuss patient diagnosis, prognosis and goals of care. Another 
family meeting was held on and family ultimately decided to 
transition to CMO. She was terminally extubated on at 5pm 
and expired at 04:59 on .

5. Losartan Potassium 25 mg PO DAILY 
6. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO QPM

patient was doing well. She remained intubated but was able to 
participate in a full neurologic exam with sedation held. She 
was euvolemic. On Nimodipine and Keppra. Patient was extubated 
 during the evening. Early morning , patient self 
removed her EVD. She was given 1 dose of Ancef. STAT CT showed 
stable ventricle size with no hematoma and mild pneumocephalus. 
Repeat head CT for increased lethargy and new upgaze restriction 
was stable. Keppra was discontinued . Additional STAT CT on 
 in the setting of lethargy was stable. Repeat CT on 
was essentially stable. She was started on phenylephrine on 
to maximize CPP with goal SBP 140-200. CTA on showed 
vasospasm in the P1 and P2, superior division of right M2, and 
left M1, left M2, stable to improved from prior imaging. SBP 
goal was narrowed to 160-200. Levophed, milrinone, and 
vasopressin were additionally used to achieve this SBP goal. 
This SBP goal was later relaxed back to 140-200. She went for 
angio on , which showed mild vasospasm. She received IA 
nitro and verapamil during the procedure. SBP goal was further 
relaxed to > 120 and medications were weaned. Repeat CT on 
showed new bilateral occipital strokes and slight increase in 
ventricular size. EVD was replaced given concern for hydro 
as cause of strokes. Post placement CT stable. CT for 
plegia x4 extremities, showed MCA/PCA watershed infarcts with 
left caudate infarct. EVD was clamped on and removed later 
that night given high need for anticoagulation for cardiac. 
Repeat head CT stable. MRI brain for prognostication 
showed diffuse areas of ischemia involving the bilateral 
parietooccipital lobes, left insular region, right cerebellar 
lobe and cerebellar vermis. CT head with contrast was obtained 
without signs of infection. Repeat MRI brain with thin cuts was 
ordered on per neuroradiology. MRI performed and revealed 
bilateral cerebral punctate infarcts otherwise stable imaging. 
TCD on was negative for vasospasm. Patients exam remained 
poor and was ordered for an MRI Cspine which was performed on 
 and showed no evidence of compression to explain plegia. 
Vitamin B12 level was slightly elevated but not enough to 
contribute to patients exam status. She was put on cEEG 
overnight on to evaluate for seizures and showed diffuse 
slowing/burst of GRDA, but no seizures.

required an esmolol drip for more strict management. LENIs were 
ordered given tachycardia, and were negative. She was 
subsequently loaded with amiodarone and esmolol was weaned off.

patient's mental status. TTE on revealed EF of 70%, 
tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary hypertension but no 
thrombus. Heparin gtt was restarted . Patient taken for a 
cardioversion on x2 and was unsuccessful. Patient remained 
in rapid AFib status post the procedure on . She was placed on 
amiodarione drip which was transitioned to PO on . Repeat 
cardioversion was discussed with cardiology and planned for 
 .

The patient was found to be hypoxic with flash pulmonary edema 
on chest x-ray on . She received Lasix. She was reintubated 
on given continued respiratory compromise. She continued to 
receive Lasix as needed. Repeat chest x-ray on was improved. 
Chest xray on with decreased amount of pulmonary edema. On 
 , she was noted to have increased use of accessory muscles; 
ABG showed respiratory acidosis. Vent settings were adjusted 
and she was diuresed. Sedation was adjusted due to vent 
dysynchrony. Patient continued to require intermittent as needed Lasix 
due to pulmonary effusions and congestion/edema, most recently 
shown on CXR from . 

#Fevers, leukocytosis
The patient was febrile and leukocytic while admitted. She was 
pancultured and put on empiric antibiotics. She was 
repancultured on in the setting of fever. She continued to 
have fevers intermittently. She was started on empiric 
Vancomycin. Febrile up to 103 on , cooling blanket and ice 
packs applied as needed. CSF sent , which was negative for 
growth. C.diff negative. Flagyl was started for ongoing 
fever. All cultures negative. Vanc/Flagyl were discontinued on 
 . BAL repeated . Cefepime was restarted for 
ongoing fevers and leukocytosis. She was febrile again . BAL 
grew coag positive staph. Cdiff was sent and she was started on 
PO Vanco pending result. Cdiff resulted as negative and PO 
vanco was discontinued. She was febrile again on and was 
pancultured.

#DVT
Patient's RUE was noted to be edematous on and a duplex was 
performed. Duplex revealed a partial non-occlusive thrombus in 
distal right axillary, full occlusion thrombus in right basilic, 
partial thrombus in right cephalic. 

#Hypernatremia
The patient became hypernatremic as a result of diuresis. FWF 
were increased as needed. D51/2NS was started on . 

#Loose stools
Flexiseal was placed on for loose stools. C. diff negative 
on .

 were consulted and recommended long term nursing facility. 
Patients family was involved in multiple family meetings to 
discuss patient diagnosis, prognosis and goals of care. Another 
family meeting was held on and family ultimately decided to 
transition to CMO. She was terminally extubated on at 5pm 
and expired at 04:59 on .
