Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the General Surgical Service for 
evaluation and treatment. On the patient underwent 
ileostomy takedown and reversal, which went well without 
complication (reader referred to the Operative Note for 
details). After a brief, uneventful stay in the PACU, the 
patient arrived on the floor NPO, on IV fluids and antibiotics, 
with a foley catheter, and IV dilaudid for pain control. The 
patient was hemodynamically stable. She received fluid boluses 
in the PACU for oliguria. 
Neuro: The patient received IV dilaudid with good effect and 
adequate pain control. When tolerating oral intake, the patient 
was transitioned to oral tylenol and oxycodone for pain.
CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. She was 
discharged on half of her home atenolol (12.5 instead of 25) 
given her well controlled rate and normal blood pressure. 
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.
GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced when appropriate, which was well 
tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored, 
and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. Electrolytes were 
routinely followed, and repleted when necessary. By time of 
discharge she had been advanced to a regular diet. She was given 
a PPI. Her incision was loosely approximated in the operating 
room to allow drainage and prevent infection. Daily wick changes 
were provided using either telfa, sterile gauze, or inch 
packing. She did have loose stools starting on POD 4 which were 
somewhat bloody which would be expected after this surgery. Her 
hematocrit remained stable. 
ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection. 
Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly. Her blood 
sugars were often elevated despite glargine and sliding scale. 
When tolerating PO she was transitioned back to her home regimen 
for discharge including nightly and AM glargine, scheduled 
regular insulin, and regular insulin sliding scale. 
Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required. Her hematocrit was 
stable at 30 from 34 on discharge. 
Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin initially 
and venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to 
get up and ambulate as early as possible. She was transitioned 
to home therapeutic lovenox on POD2. 
At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and 
follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and 
agreement with the discharge plan. Social work and case 
management assisted in disposition planning and facilitating 
patient's safe return to living with her daughter and husband. 
All important conversations including discussion of surgical 
course and discharge instructions were conducted with the 
assistance of an interpreter.

weights greater than lbs until you follow-up with your 
surgeon, who will instruct you further regarding activity 
restrictions.

medications.

increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision 
site.

appointment.

water.

prevent pulling or dislocation.

the sutures. A dry dressing can then be placed overlying the

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the General Surgical Service for 
evaluation and treatment. On the patient underwent 
ileostomy takedown and reversal, which went well without 
complication (reader referred to the Operative Note for 
details). After a brief, uneventful stay in the PACU, the 
patient arrived on the floor NPO, on IV fluids and antibiotics, 
with a foley catheter, and IV dilaudid for pain control. The 
patient was hemodynamically stable. She received fluid boluses 
in the PACU for oliguria. 
Neuro: The patient received IV dilaudid with good effect and 
adequate pain control. When tolerating oral intake, the patient 
was transitioned to oral tylenol and oxycodone for pain.
CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. She was 
discharged on half of her home atenolol (12.5 instead of 25) 
given her well controlled rate and normal blood pressure. 
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.
GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced when appropriate, which was well 
tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored, 
and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. Electrolytes were 
routinely followed, and repleted when necessary. By time of 
discharge she had been advanced to a regular diet. She was given 
a PPI. Her incision was loosely approximated in the operating 
room to allow drainage and prevent infection. Daily wick changes 
were provided using either telfa, sterile gauze, or inch 
packing. She did have loose stools starting on POD 4 which were 
somewhat bloody which would be expected after this surgery. Her 
hematocrit remained stable. 
ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection. 
Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly. Her blood 
sugars were often elevated despite glargine and sliding scale. 
When tolerating PO she was transitioned back to her home regimen 
for discharge including nightly and AM glargine, scheduled 
regular insulin, and regular insulin sliding scale. 
Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required. Her hematocrit was 
stable at 30 from 34 on discharge. 
Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin initially 
and venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to 
get up and ambulate as early as possible. She was transitioned 
to home therapeutic lovenox on POD2. 
At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and 
follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and 
agreement with the discharge plan. Social work and case 
management assisted in disposition planning and facilitating 
patient's safe return to living with her daughter and husband. 
All important conversations including discussion of surgical 
course and discharge instructions were conducted with the 
assistance of an interpreter.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the General Surgical Service for 
evaluation and treatment. On the patient underwent 
ileostomy takedown and reversal, which went well without 
complication (reader referred to the Operative Note for 
details). After a brief, uneventful stay in the PACU, the 
patient arrived on the floor NPO, on IV fluids and antibiotics, 
with a foley catheter, and IV dilaudid for pain control. The 
patient was hemodynamically stable. She received fluid boluses 
in the PACU for oliguria. 
Neuro: The patient received IV dilaudid with good effect and 
adequate pain control. When tolerating oral intake, the patient 
was transitioned to oral tylenol and oxycodone for pain.
CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. She was 
discharged on half of her home atenolol (12.5 instead of 25) 
given her well controlled rate and normal blood pressure. 
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary 
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary 
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.
GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV 
fluids. Diet was advanced when appropriate, which was well 
tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored, 
and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. Electrolytes were 
routinely followed, and repleted when necessary. By time of 
discharge she had been advanced to a regular diet. She was given 
a PPI. Her incision was loosely approximated in the operating 
room to allow drainage and prevent infection. Daily wick changes 
were provided using either telfa, sterile gauze, or inch 
packing. She did have loose stools starting on POD 4 which were 
somewhat bloody which would be expected after this surgery. Her 
hematocrit remained stable. 
ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were 
closely watched for signs of infection. 
Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout 
his stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly. Her blood 
sugars were often elevated despite glargine and sliding scale. 
When tolerating PO she was transitioned back to her home regimen 
for discharge including nightly and AM glargine, scheduled 
regular insulin, and regular insulin sliding scale. 
Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined 
routinely; no transfusions were required. Her hematocrit was 
stable at 30 from 34 on discharge. 
Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin initially 
and venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to 
get up and ambulate as early as possible. She was transitioned 
to home therapeutic lovenox on POD2. 
At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and 
follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and 
agreement with the discharge plan. Social work and case 
management assisted in disposition planning and facilitating 
patient's safe return to living with her daughter and husband. 
All important conversations including discussion of surgical 
course and discharge instructions were conducted with the 
assistance of an interpreter.

5. Lovenox 80mg SC

ileostomy reversal

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
General Discharge Instructions:
 Please resume all regular home medications , unless 
specifically advised not to take a particular medication. Also, 
please take any new medications as prescribed.
 Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times 
per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids. Avoid lifting 
weights greater than lbs until you follow-up with your 
surgeon, who will instruct you further regarding activity 
restrictions.
 Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain 
medications.
 Please follow-up with your surgeon and Primary Care Provider 
(PCP) as advised.
 Incision Care:
 *Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner if you have 
increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision 
site.
 *Avoid swimming and baths until your follow-up appointment.
 *You may shower, and wash surgical incisions with a mild soap 
and warm water. Gently pat the area dry.
 *If you have staples, they will be removed at your follow-up 
appointment.
*You may shower; wash the area gently with warm, soapy water.
 *Keep the insertion site clean and dry otherwise.
 *Avoid swimming, baths, hot tubs; do not submerge yourself in 
water.
 *Make sure to keep the drain attached securely to your body to 
prevent pulling or dislocation.

 Instructions: Please change packing to incision daily. 
Individual strips of wet to dry gauze should be applied between 
the sutures. A dry dressing can then be placed overlying the 
incision for comfort.

Discharge Instructions:
 Activity

· We recommend that you avoid heavy lifting, running, 
climbing, or other strenuous exercise until your follow-up 
appointment.

· You make take leisurely walks and slowly increase your 
activity at your own pace. try to do too much all at once.

· No driving while taking any narcotic or sedating 
medication. 

· No contact sports until cleared by your neurosurgeon. 

 

Medications

· You may take Ibuprofen/ Motrin for pain.

· You may use Acetaminophen (Tylenol) for minor 
discomfort if you are not otherwise restricted from taking this 
medication.

· It is important to increase fluid intake while taking 
pain medications. We also recommend a stool softener like 
Colace. Pain medications can cause constipation. 

 

 

When to Call Your Doctor at for:

· Severe pain, worsening numbness, New weakness or 
changes in sensation in your arms or legs.

 yo male with h/o metastatic RRC presenting with increased 
right hip pain, decreased po intake and increased fatigue. 
Initially treated with a left nephrectomy in with neg lymph 
nodes. In he had a traumatic fall at work and trauma imaging 
with CT showed new pulmonary nodules and mediastinal LAD. FNA 
was consistent with metastatic clear cell carcinoma. He was 
started on IL-2 but had progression of disease with bony 
lesions. He was started on avastin in and cyberknife in 
 . He progressed on avastin and with worsening right hip 
pain has been recieving palliative XRT for the past week. He 
says that his pain has progressed which is limiting his 
movement. He has has had decreased appetite and decreased 
energy over the past week and generalized pain in his abd/chest 
and back. Reports one episode of non bloody/non bilious emesis. 
 No bladder or bowel incontinence and no loss sensation. 
His mobility is limited by pain, not new weakness. Has been 
feeling warm at home, but no objective fevers, cough, diarrhea 
or dysuria and no recent sick contacts.
.
Pertinent ROS noted above rest of review wnl.

PAST ONCOLOGIC HISTORY: 
- presented to in complains of abdominal pain and gross
hematuria. CT scan performed and showed a 14-cm tumor on his
left kidney. 
- : underwent a radical left nephrectomy which showed a
14 x 14 x 10 cm tumor that was of clear cell type, firm and
nuclear grade . There was evidence of tumor thrombus 
extending
into a large muscular vein at the hilum of the kidney. His left
adrenal gland was removed and was negative for tumor. hilar
lymph nodes, paraaortic lymph nodes and a small bowel lymph
node obtained was negative for malignancy.
- : suffered a traumatic work-related fall (fell 25 
feet
off a ladder). Standard trauma x-rays and a nonenhanced CT,
showed the presence of new pulmonary nodules. 
- CT TORSO: innumerable pulmonary metastases, bulky
mediastinal lymphadenopathy. 
- : FNA right upper lobe lung nodules showed malignant
cells consistent with metastatic clear cell carcinoma of the
kidney
 : Started on IL-2; received 10 out of 14 doses, first
week was complicated by encephalopathy and the second week was
complicated by renal failure, transaminitis and Staph 
epidermitis
bacteremia status post Vancomycin
- chest CT, no evidence of progression of metastatic
disease
- CT TORSO: progression of disease
- : Started Avastin 10mg/kg q2 weeks; CT showed
stable disease
- : Cyberknife to subcarinal mass; 2400 cGy in 3
fractions. 
- C3D1 of Avastin after staging CT showed disease 
progression
.

GERD

Brief Hospital Course:
 man with metastatic renal cell CA admitted for worsening 
right hip, back, shoulder, right chest pain, fatigue and 
decreased PO intake. CT scan showed progressive bony lesions.
.
# Hip/back/chest/neck pain: Due to bone mets. MRI spine and CT 
torso showed progressive bone mets, but no cord compression or 
fracture. Used additional 32g PO hydromorphone as needed yesterday. 
Clonazepam dose decreased for somnolence. He refused gabapentin 
stating it did not work in the past. Palliative care consulted. 
 XRT to right scapula , single fraction. OxyContin 
changed to methadone due to over-sedation. Finished 
dexamethasone taper . Continued methadone 10mg PO three times a day 
and hydromorphone PO as needed with modest pain control (very slow to 
improve). EKG verified for QTc.
.
# Abnormal LFTs: New, no liver involvement by CT. Hep B and C 
Ab negative. Lipase normal. Elevated GGT. Possibly due to 
paraneoplastic effect.
.
# Hypercalcemia: Mild, improved after IV fluids.
.
# Weakness: Due to progressive disease, poor PO intake, sedation 
from narcotic analgesia, pain, and depression. Pain control as 
above and physical therapy. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Somnolence: Due to over sedation from narcotic pain meds, 
benzos, and severe depression. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Renal cell CA: status post IL-2 and bevacizumab. Planning to start 
sunitinib this week, but will discuss with primary oncologist 
regarding role of sunitinib in setting of new LFT abnormalities. 
 Palliative XRT to hip finished . XRT to scapula 
 . Separate family meeting one with Mr. , 
wife, and Dr. the other with Mr. brother and 
friend regarding questions about prognosis revealed 
family dysfunction. His brother felt that living 
environement may not be safe for him. However, did not 
want to live with his brother, preferring to eventually go home 
after rehab.
.
# Dysphagia/hoarseness: Onset last month. Normal swallow eval. 
Possible recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement from mediastinal 
mets. ENT referral scheduled as outpatient.
.
# Anemia: Stable, microcytic, but iron studies reflect anemia of 
inflammation.
.
# Depression/Anxiety/Delirium: Likely contributing to inability 
to mobilize in addition to pain. Consulted Psychiatry. 
Increased sertraline from 50 to 75mg daily. Improved mood day 
of discharge. Weaned clonazepam. Started methylphenidate 
initially at 5mg daily , changing to 2.5 qAM and qNOON 
per Psychiatry.
.
# Hypothyroidism: TSH elevated; increased dose of levothyroxine 
from 150 to 175mcg.
.
# GERD: Continue PPI.
.
# GI PPx: PPI and bowel regimen.
.
# DVT PPx: Heparin SC.
.
# FEN: Regular diet, replete lytes as needed.
.
# Precautions: history of positive MRSA screen.
.
# Code status: FULL.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital for uncontrolled pain due to 
increased bony metastases from your kidney cancer. An MRI was 
done to exclude spinal compression and brain metastasis, which 
you do not have. With the help of the Palliative Care team, 
your pain regimen was titrated up for control of the pain and 
you started an oral chemotherapy sunitinb (Sutent) to treat the 
cancer as an outpatient. You also received radiation therapy to 
the right scapula to help control the pain in that location.

your thyroid was still underactive. For your mood, depression, 
and lack of energy, you were evaluated by Psychiatry and started 
on methylphenidate (Ritalin). Sertraline (Zoloft) dose was 
increased and clonazepam (Klonapin) was stopped.
.

5. Stop clonazepam (Klonapin).
6. Methylphenidate (Ritalin) 2.5mg every morning and every noon.
7. Increase sertraline (Zoloft) to 75mg daily.

Brief Hospital Course:
 man with metastatic renal cell CA admitted for worsening 
right hip, back, shoulder, right chest pain, fatigue and 
decreased PO intake. CT scan showed progressive bony lesions.
.
# Hip/back/chest/neck pain: Due to bone mets. MRI spine and CT 
torso showed progressive bone mets, but no cord compression or 
fracture. Used additional 32g PO hydromorphone as needed yesterday. 
Clonazepam dose decreased for somnolence. He refused gabapentin 
stating it did not work in the past. Palliative care consulted. 
 XRT to right scapula , single fraction. OxyContin 
changed to methadone due to over-sedation. Finished 
dexamethasone taper . Continued methadone 10mg PO three times a day 
and hydromorphone PO as needed with modest pain control (very slow to 
improve). EKG verified for QTc.
.
# Abnormal LFTs: New, no liver involvement by CT. Hep B and C 
Ab negative. Lipase normal. Elevated GGT. Possibly due to 
paraneoplastic effect.
.
# Hypercalcemia: Mild, improved after IV fluids.
.
# Weakness: Due to progressive disease, poor PO intake, sedation 
from narcotic analgesia, pain, and depression. Pain control as 
above and physical therapy. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Somnolence: Due to over sedation from narcotic pain meds, 
benzos, and severe depression. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Renal cell CA: status post IL-2 and bevacizumab. Planning to start 
sunitinib this week, but will discuss with primary oncologist 
regarding role of sunitinib in setting of new LFT abnormalities. 
 Palliative XRT to hip finished . XRT to scapula 
 . Separate family meeting one with Mr. , 
wife, and Dr. the other with Mr. brother and 
friend regarding questions about prognosis revealed 
family dysfunction. His brother felt that living 
environement may not be safe for him. However, did not 
want to live with his brother, preferring to eventually go home 
after rehab.
.
# Dysphagia/hoarseness: Onset last month. Normal swallow eval. 
Possible recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement from mediastinal 
mets. ENT referral scheduled as outpatient.
.
# Anemia: Stable, microcytic, but iron studies reflect anemia of 
inflammation.
.
# Depression/Anxiety/Delirium: Likely contributing to inability 
to mobilize in addition to pain. Consulted Psychiatry. 
Increased sertraline from 50 to 75mg daily. Improved mood day 
of discharge. Weaned clonazepam. Started methylphenidate 
initially at 5mg daily , changing to 2.5 qAM and qNOON 
per Psychiatry.
.
# Hypothyroidism: TSH elevated; increased dose of levothyroxine 
from 150 to 175mcg.
.
# GERD: Continue PPI.
.
# GI PPx: PPI and bowel regimen.
.
# DVT PPx: Heparin SC.
.
# FEN: Regular diet, replete lytes as needed.
.
# Precautions: history of positive MRSA screen.
.

You were admitted to the hospital for uncontrolled pain due to 
increased bony metastases from your kidney cancer. An MRI was 
done to exclude spinal compression and brain metastasis, which 
you do not have. With the help of the Palliative Care team, 
your pain regimen was titrated up for control of the pain and 
you started an oral chemotherapy sunitinb (Sutent) to treat the 
cancer as an outpatient. You also received radiation therapy to 
the right scapula to help control the pain in that location.

your thyroid was still underactive. For your mood, depression, 
and lack of energy, you were evaluated by Psychiatry and started

increased and clonazepam (Klonapin) was stopped.
.

7. Increase sertraline (Zoloft) to 75mg daily.

section_text:
yo male with h/o metastatic RRC presenting with increased 
right hip pain, decreased po intake and increased fatigue. 
Initially treated with a left nephrectomy in with neg lymph 
nodes. In he had a traumatic fall at work and trauma imaging 
with CT showed new pulmonary nodules and mediastinal LAD. FNA 
was consistent with metastatic clear cell carcinoma. He was 
started on IL-2 but had progression of disease with bony 
lesions. He was started on avastin in and cyberknife in 
 . He progressed on avastin and with worsening right hip 
pain has been recieving palliative XRT for the past week. He 
says that his pain has progressed which is limiting his 
movement. He has has had decreased appetite and decreased 
energy over the past week and generalized pain in his abd/chest 
and back. Reports one episode of non bloody/non bilious emesis. 
 No bladder or bowel incontinence and no loss sensation. 
His mobility is limited by pain, not new weakness. Has been 
feeling warm at home, but no objective fevers, cough, diarrhea 
or dysuria and no recent sick contacts.
.
Pertinent ROS noted above rest of review wnl.

Brief Hospital Course:
 man with metastatic renal cell CA admitted for worsening 
right hip, back, shoulder, right chest pain, fatigue and 
decreased PO intake. CT scan showed progressive bony lesions.
.
# Hip/back/chest/neck pain: Due to bone mets. MRI spine and CT 
torso showed progressive bone mets, but no cord compression or 
fracture. Used additional 32g PO hydromorphone as needed yesterday. 
Clonazepam dose decreased for somnolence. He refused gabapentin 
stating it did not work in the past. Palliative care consulted. 
 XRT to right scapula , single fraction. OxyContin 
changed to methadone due to over-sedation. Finished 
dexamethasone taper . Continued methadone 10mg PO three times a day 
and hydromorphone PO as needed with modest pain control (very slow to 
improve). EKG verified for QTc.
.
# Abnormal LFTs: New, no liver involvement by CT. Hep B and C 
Ab negative. Lipase normal. Elevated GGT. Possibly due to 
paraneoplastic effect.
.
# Hypercalcemia: Mild, improved after IV fluids.
.
# Weakness: Due to progressive disease, poor PO intake, sedation 
from narcotic analgesia, pain, and depression. Pain control as 
above and physical therapy. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Somnolence: Due to over sedation from narcotic pain meds, 
benzos, and severe depression. Rapid wean off clonazepam.
.
# Renal cell CA: status post IL-2 and bevacizumab. Planning to start 
sunitinib this week, but will discuss with primary oncologist 
regarding role of sunitinib in setting of new LFT abnormalities. 
 Palliative XRT to hip finished . XRT to scapula 
 . Separate family meeting one with Mr. , 
wife, and Dr. the other with Mr. brother and 
friend regarding questions about prognosis revealed 
family dysfunction. His brother felt that living 
environement may not be safe for him. However, did not 
want to live with his brother, preferring to eventually go home 
after rehab.
.
# Dysphagia/hoarseness: Onset last month. Normal swallow eval. 
Possible recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement from mediastinal 
mets. ENT referral scheduled as outpatient.
.
# Anemia: Stable, microcytic, but iron studies reflect anemia of 
inflammation.
.
# Depression/Anxiety/Delirium: Likely contributing to inability 
to mobilize in addition to pain. Consulted Psychiatry. 
Increased sertraline from 50 to 75mg daily. Improved mood day 
of discharge. Weaned clonazepam. Started methylphenidate 
initially at 5mg daily , changing to 2.5 qAM and qNOON 
per Psychiatry.
.
# Hypothyroidism: TSH elevated; increased dose of levothyroxine 
from 150 to 175mcg.
.
# GERD: Continue PPI.
.
# GI PPx: PPI and bowel regimen.
.
# DVT PPx: Heparin SC.
.
# FEN: Regular diet, replete lytes as needed.
.
# Precautions: history of positive MRSA screen.
.
# Code status: FULL.
 
Medications on Admission:
synthroid mcg daily
zoloft daily(dose unknown)
trazadone 100 mg qhs
oxycontin 30 md twice a day
oxycodone 15 mg q4h
protonix 40mg daily
 
Discharge LABTEST Medications: 1. sunitinib 12.5 mg Capsule LABTEST Sig: 3 Capsules PO LABTEST DAILY: 4 weeks 
on, 2 weeks off.
2. levothyroxine 175 mcg PO DAILY.
3. sertraline 75 mg PO DAILY.
4. trazodone 150 mg PO Q 9 .
5. pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H.
6. methadone 10 mg PO three times a day.
Disp:*90 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
7. hydromorphone mg PO Q2H as needed pain.
Disp:*250 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
8. acetaminophen 325-650mg PO Q4H as needed Pain.
9. docusate sodium 100 mg PO twice a day.
10. senna 8.6 mg PO twice a day.
11. methylphenidate 2.5 mg PO 8:00AM.
Disp:*15 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
12. methylphenidate 2.5 mg PO NOON.
Disp:*15 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
 
Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care
 
Facility:
 
 
 Diagnosis:
Pain related to bony metastatic disease
Metastatic renal cell carcinoma (kidney cancer)
Depression
Anxiety
Weakness
Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid)
 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Out of Bed with assistance to chair or 
wheelchair.
 
Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital for uncontrolled pain due to 
increased bony metastases from your kidney cancer. An MRI was 
done to exclude spinal compression and brain metastasis, which 
you do not have. With the help of the Palliative Care team, 
your pain regimen was titrated up for control of the pain and 
you started an oral chemotherapy sunitinb (Sutent) to treat the 
cancer as an outpatient. You also received radiation therapy to 
the right scapula to help control the pain in that location. 
Also your levothyroxine (Synthroid) dose was increased because 
your thyroid was still underactive. For your mood, depression, 
and lack of energy, you were evaluated by Psychiatry and started 
on methylphenidate (Ritalin). Sertraline (Zoloft) dose was 
increased and clonazepam (Klonapin) was stopped.
.
MEDICATION LABTEST CHANGES: 1. Methadone 10mg 3x a day.
2. Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) as needed for breakthrough pain.
3. Sunitinib (Sutent) daily x4 weeks, then 2 weeks off.
4. Increase levothyroxine (Synthroid) to 175mcg daily.
5. Stop clonazepam (Klonapin).
6. Methylphenidate (Ritalin) 2.5mg every morning and every noon.
7. Increase sertraline (Zoloft) to 75mg daily.
 
Followup Instructions:

Facility:
 
 
 Diagnosis:
Pain related to bony metastatic disease
Metastatic renal cell carcinoma (kidney cancer)
Depression
Anxiety
Weakness
Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid)
 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Out of Bed with assistance to chair or 
wheelchair.
 
Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital for uncontrolled pain due to 
increased bony metastases from your kidney cancer. An MRI was 
done to exclude spinal compression and brain metastasis, which 
you do not have. With the help of the Palliative Care team, 
your pain regimen was titrated up for control of the pain and 
you started an oral chemotherapy sunitinb (Sutent) to treat the 
cancer as an outpatient. You also received radiation therapy to 
the right scapula to help control the pain in that location. 
Also your levothyroxine (Synthroid) dose was increased because 
your thyroid was still underactive. For your mood, depression, 
and lack of energy, you were evaluated by Psychiatry and started 
on methylphenidate (Ritalin). Sertraline (Zoloft) dose was 
increased and clonazepam (Klonapin) was stopped.
.
MEDICATION LABTEST CHANGES: 1. Methadone 10mg 3x a day.
2. Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) as needed for breakthrough pain.
3. Sunitinib (Sutent) daily x4 weeks, then 2 weeks off.
4. Increase levothyroxine (Synthroid) to 175mcg daily.
5. Stop clonazepam (Klonapin).
6. Methylphenidate (Ritalin) 2.5mg every morning and every noon.
7. Increase sertraline (Zoloft) to 75mg daily.

a 17-gauge coaxial needle was then advanced to the right hepatic 
dome lesion in segment VII. Two biopsy samples were obtained 
through this lesion and were given to the pathologist for 
analysis. Following this, the radiofrequency ablation probe was 
advanced into the lesion and using a pulsed algorithm, 
radiofrequency ablation was carried out for 12 minutes. The 
temperature within the lesion is approximately 65 degrees 
Celsius at the end of the procedure.
 
CT with and without contrast with arterial, portal venous and 
delayed phase
through the liver was then carried out.

Post-RFA CT demonstrated no residual arterial phase enhancement 
in the region of the segments VI and VII lesions. Some reactive 
hyperemia in the posterior right hepatic lobe was demonstrated 
on the arterial phase. In the region of radiofrequency ablation 
in the segment VII lesion, there is a high dense area on the 
portal venous and delayed phase measuring 3.5 x 2.2 cm. The 
 area treated with RFA was hyperdense on the 
pre-contrast examination compatible with treatment effect. The 
lesion in segment VI was replaced by an area of ablation 
measuring 3.6 x 2.5 cm (series 6, image 85) and this area was 
also hyperdense on the pre-contrast CT, compatible with ablation 
defect.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Successful radiofrequency ablation of lesions located in 
segments VI and
VII.
2. Right adrenal nodule, unchanged from CT in .
3. Splenomegaly.
 
Brief Hospital Course:
 male with recently diagnosed hepatitis-C cirrhosis 
and two lesions in the right lobe of the liver who comes in 
today for radiofrequency ablation therapy.
.

liver seen on CT-scan on which are highly suspicious 
for malignancy. Lessions appear to be superficial and amendable 
to RFA treatment. Pre-Procedure: EKG done, lab work done: Plts 
55, INR 1.5 didn't need any transfusion. Patient returned 
from the procedure late in the afternoon. was somnolent and 
complaining of referred pain on right shoulder which resolved on 
the following day. was feeling well in the morning after the 
procedure. was tolerating a regular diet and denied having 
any complaints. will need f/u with Dr. in 1 month and 
repeat CT- scan.
.
.
# HEP C cirrhosis: This is a new diagnosis complicated by 
ascitis. Patient was started on lasix and spirolactone about 1 
month ago. stated to be feeling well and to have decreased 
abdominal distention and lower extremety edema. LFTs sl. 
elevated ALT , AST , AP 160s-170s, T.bili 1.2 at time of 
discharge
-continue lasix 40mg PO Qday
-spirolactone 100mg PO QDAY
-Monitor LFTs
. 
# PULMONARY/ADRENAL NODULE: Right upper lobe pulmonary nodule. 
Suggest followup CT in six months to check for stability. Right 
adrenal nodule suspicious for metastases. MRI recommended for 
better characterization, scheduled for .
.
#LOW BACK PAIN: Patient with history of chronic low back pain. 
As per patient this is related to work injury and 
herniated lumbar disc. sees a pain management doctor for the 
past few years and has monthly epidural injections.
-Ms contin 30mg three times a day
-Oxycodone 15mg four times a day
-Lyrica 200mg 
.
#Smoking: Nicotine patch, discussed the importance of quitting 
.

(Daily).

day) as needed for pain . 
5. Morphine 30 mg Tablet Sustained Release Sig: One (1) Tablet 
Sustained Release PO three times a day (3 times a day).

Home

Vitals stable, no complaints

treatment of two lesions on your liver. The procedure went well 
with no complications. Please follow-up as outlined below.

If you develop severe abdominal pain, dizziness,

immediate medical attention.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

 was a pleasure taking care of you during your admission to 
 . You were admitted for removal of hardware in your back at 
L4-L5. The surgery was successful and your surgeon was Dr. 
 . 

You were found to have low blood counts requiring blood 
transfusions. This was likely because of your infection (low 
production of blood) and also very small amount of blood loss in 
your stool (maybe because of your diarrhea). Your counts are 
stable, but you should have your blood checked after discharge.

There was briefly a concern for a heart attack, but our 
cardiologists felt this was NOT the case. 

Please follow up with your primary care doctor and other 
appointments. Please continue to take your home medications but 
avoid alprazalam and cyclobenzaprine, especially if you feel 
confused.

Sincerely,
Your team at 

¨ You have staples, keep your wound clean and dry until they are 
removed. 
¨ You should wear your brace when out of bed or when your head 
of bed is above 30 degrees.
¨ You may put the brace on at the edge of your bed.
¨ You may use a shower chair to bathe without the brace on.
¨ No tub baths or pool swimming for two weeks from your date of 
surgery.
¨ Do not smoke.
¨ No pulling up, lifting more than 10 lbs., or excessive bending 
or twisting.
¨ Limit your use of stairs to times per day.
¨ Have a friend or family member check your incision daily for 
signs of infection.
¨ Take your pain medication as instructed; you may find it best 
if taken in the morning when you wake-up for morning stiffness, 
and before bed for sleeping discomfort. Pain medication should 
be used as needed when you have pain. You do not need to take it 
if you do not have pain.
¨ Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc. for two weeks.
¨ Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as pain medicine 
(narcotics) can cause constipation. We recommend taking an over 
the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace) while 
taking narcotic pain medication.
¨ Clearance to drive and return to work will be addressed at 
your post-operative office visit.

ALL YOUR SURGEON IMMEDIATELY IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE 
FOLLOWING
¨ Pain that is continually increasing or not relieved by pain 
medicine.
¨ Any weakness, numbness, tingling in your extremities.
¨ Any signs of infection at the wound site: redness, swelling, 
tenderness, and drainage
¨ Fever greater than or equal to 101.5° F. 
¨ Loss of control of bowel or bladder functioning

 was a pleasure taking care of you during your admission to 
 . You were admitted for removal of hardware in your back at 
L4-L5. The surgery was successful and your surgeon was Dr. 
 . 

You were found to have low blood counts requiring blood 
transfusions. This was likely because of your infection (low 
production of blood) and also very small amount of blood loss in 
your stool (maybe because of your diarrhea). Your counts are 
stable, but you should have your blood checked after discharge.

¨ You have staples, keep your wound clean and dry until they are 
removed. 
¨ You should wear your brace when out of bed or when your head 
of bed is above 30 degrees.
¨ You may put the brace on at the edge of your bed.
¨ You may use a shower chair to bathe without the brace on.
¨ No tub baths or pool swimming for two weeks from your date of 
surgery.
¨ Do not smoke.
¨ No pulling up, lifting more than 10 lbs., or excessive bending 
or twisting.
¨ Limit your use of stairs to times per day.
¨ Have a friend or family member check your incision daily for 
signs of infection.
¨ Take your pain medication as instructed; you may find it best 
if taken in the morning when you wake-up for morning stiffness, 
and before bed for sleeping discomfort. Pain medication should 
be used as needed when you have pain. You do not need to take it 
if you do not have pain.
¨ Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc. for two weeks.
¨ Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as pain medicine 
(narcotics) can cause constipation. We recommend taking an over 
the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace) while 
taking narcotic pain medication.
¨ Clearance to drive and return to work will be addressed at 
your post-operative office visit.

ALL YOUR SURGEON IMMEDIATELY IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE 
FOLLOWING
¨ Pain that is continually increasing or not relieved by pain 
medicine.
¨ Any weakness, numbness, tingling in your extremities.
¨ Any signs of infection at the wound site: redness, swelling, 
tenderness, and drainage
¨ Fever greater than or equal to 101.5° F. 
¨ Loss of control of bowel or bladder functioning

failure with reduced ejection fraction (last EF 40% in ,

and MSSA hardware infection, who was admitted to the 
neurosurgical service on for hardware removal after CT 
showed loosening.

This patient's complicated course began last when a 
stroke left her with a residual field deficit. Then, in she sustained a fall with a back injury that caused her to 
be bed bound for several months. On , she went to the OR 
for an L4/L5 discectomy and fusion. Post-operatively, she 
develped drainage from her lumbar wound and was taken back to 
the OR on . An I&D was performed and there was pustular 
and sanguinous fluid that was drained. MSSA grew in bottles. 
Cultures were suggestive of deep wound infection with bone 
involvement of MSSA and pseudomonas, both pansensitive. She was 
started on zosyn but transitioned to meropenem after 
experiencing a rash to zosyn. She was on meropenem for 3 months 
until when in lieu of decreasing inflammatory marker, 
she was transitioned to oral levofloxacin for suppressive 
therapy. 

Two weeks later, on , she presented for follow-up to the 
 clinic complaining of 1 month of worsening back pain, but no 
fevers, n/v/d. A CT scan was performed after that clinic visit 
on which revealed hardware loosening at L4 and L5 
pedicle screws. She was admitted for same-day hardware 
extrusion.

and recommendations were made to discontinue the vancomycin and 
cefipime and start meropenum with a planned course of 6 weeks.

that was the same as pre operative leg pain and neurontin. The

had one episode of hypotension with a systolic pressure in the 
high . She was given a fluid bolus of 500cc, for which she 
initially responded well to. Then, she was given her home 
carvedilol and torsemide which caused her again to become 
hypotensive. She was then transfused 1U pRBCs given her post-op 
anemia and transferred to medicine for further management. On 
transfer, ACS was ruled out with negative troponins. The 
etiology of her hypotension likely was a combination of anemia 
and medication effect. She was later hypertensive, and home 
medications were restarted. Stable at discharge

falling to or below ~7 and ~22 respectively, on and . 
Each time, she received 1 unit pRBC transfusion with appropriate 
response. She did have guiac positive stools this admission, but 
in the setting of diarrhea. She did not have any melena, bright 
red blood per rectum, or bleed at any other sites. Patient has 
low reticulocyte count, and iron studies consistent with anemia 
of chronic disease. Suspect bone marrow suppression and chronic 
disease, plus or minus microscopic blood losses. GI workup was 
deferred during inpatient stay, given overall clinical picture 
and discussion with patient and family, but can be further 
addressed as outpatient.

#Acute kidney injury: Also on , the patient's creatinine 
increased from 1.2 to 1.4 in the context of hypotension. She 
was continued on IV fluids while her oral intake was 
insufficient. She did develop pulmonary edema and increased O2 
requirement after receiving fluids on , thus though her 
creatinine remained slightly elevated, she was not given 
additional exogenous fluid due to concern for congestive heart failure. Her 
creatinine was monitored closely. Improved by discharge.

#New ECG changes, slight CK/CK-MB rise: There was concern that 
hypotension on was secondary to ACS given ECG changes and 
slight elevation in CK/CK-MB. Enzymes downtrended rapidly, 
suggesting alternate cause for rise. patient started on heparin drip, 
full dose aspirin, high dose statin and continued on home 
metoprolol. She received a repeat ECG that showed nonspecific T 
wave inversions, with plans for echo to further evaluate for 
wall motion abnormalities. She is already on cardioprotective 
medications for home medications. Heparin gtt was stopped after 
several hours when her troponins failed to elevate. Cardiology 
was consulted who recommended continuing her home cardiac 
medications. 

#Acute on Chronic systolic heart failure: On , patient 
developed increased O2 requirement to 6L but after a dose of IV 
lasix and her home torsemide, her O2 requirement is downtrending 
and she is now satting mid on RA. Also had wheezing 
that improved with neb. Her respiratory status was monitored 
closely on this admission through vital signs. She did require 
extra diuresis given IVF resusciation and blood transfusion 
causing pulmonary edema. A Foley was temporarily placed to track 
I/O's. She was continued on her home carvedilol and losartan. 
Discharge weight was ***. 

#Delirium: Developed fluctuating hyper and hypo-active delirium 
on . Likely multifactorial etiology from prolonged 
hospitalization and rehabilitation stay, recent surgery and 
acute illness. Narcotics and benzos were d/ced and she improved 
steadily. Delirium precautions were maintained. She often was 
A+Ox3 and at the time of discharge was back to baseline. 

# Guaiac positive stool during admission. Hemodynamically stable 
without melena or overt blood. Family and team opted to plan for 
outpatient followup. Discharged on a daily PPI.

transufion , and persistent lower extremity pain that 
originates in her back and shoots down both legs.

#Hardware extrusion, possible osteomyelitis: The patient 
tolerated the procedure well but developed post-op delerium and 
hypotension. The patient's hardware was sent for culture as well 
as superficial and deep interoperative tissue cultures. There 
was no evidence of infection during the surgery. The patient was 
recovered in the PACU. The patients diet was advanced. On 
dressing saturated and replaced at 1000. Drainage was expected 
from the wound. The patient was evaluated by infectious disease 
and recommendations were made to discontinue the vancomycin and 
cefipime and start meropenum with a planned course of 6 weeks. 
Blood cultures and LFTs were sent at the recommendation of 
infectious disease. The patient was measured at the bedside for 
a lumbar brace and the patient was maintained on bedrest until 
the brace was obtained. The patient reported ongoing leg pain 
that was the same as pre operative leg pain and neurontin. The 
patients serum albumin was noted to be 3.1 and ensure three times a day 
ordered. On , the antibiotic regimen was clarified with ID

replacement. A joint aspiration was performed, which was not 
consistent with infection (WBC 230). A joint culture fluid is 
pending. X-ray of the right knee did not demonstrate any 
hardware complications. 

#Hypotension: Overnight, between and , Mrs. 
had one episode of hypotension with a systolic pressure in the 
high . She was given a fluid bolus of 500cc, for which she 
initially responded well to. Then, she was given her home 
carvedilol and torsemide which caused her again to become 
hypotensive. She was then transfused 1U pRBCs given her post-op 
anemia and transferred to medicine for further management. On 
transfer, ACS was ruled out with negative troponins. The 
etiology of her hypotension likely was a combination of anemia 
and medication effect. She was later hypertensive, and home 
medications were restarted. Stable at discharge

#Anemia: Patient had two periods of hemoglobin and hematocrit 
falling to or below ~7 and ~22 respectively, on and . 
Each time, she received 1 unit pRBC transfusion with appropriate 
response. She did have guiac positive stools this admission, but 
in the setting of diarrhea. She did not have any melena, bright 
red blood per rectum, or bleed at any other sites. Patient has 
low reticulocyte count, and iron studies consistent with anemia 
of chronic disease. Suspect bone marrow suppression and chronic 
disease, plus or minus microscopic blood losses. GI workup was 
deferred during inpatient stay, given overall clinical picture 
and discussion with patient and family, but can be further 
addressed as outpatient.

increased from 1.2 to 1.4 in the context of hypotension. She 
was continued on IV fluids while her oral intake was 
insufficient. She did develop pulmonary edema and increased O2 
requirement after receiving fluids on , thus though her 
creatinine remained slightly elevated, she was not given 
additional exogenous fluid due to concern for congestive heart failure. Her 
creatinine was monitored closely. Improved by discharge.

#New ECG changes, slight CK/CK-MB rise: There was concern that 
hypotension on was secondary to ACS given ECG changes and 
slight elevation in CK/CK-MB. Enzymes downtrended rapidly, 
suggesting alternate cause for rise. patient started on heparin drip, 
full dose aspirin, high dose statin and continued on home 
metoprolol. She received a repeat ECG that showed nonspecific T 
wave inversions, with plans for echo to further evaluate for 
wall motion abnormalities. She is already on cardioprotective 
medications for home medications. Heparin gtt was stopped after 
several hours when her troponins failed to elevate. Cardiology 
was consulted who recommended continuing her home cardiac 
medications. 

#Acute on Chronic systolic heart failure: On , patient 
developed increased O2 requirement to 6L but after a dose of IV 
lasix and her home torsemide, her O2 requirement is downtrending 
and she is now satting mid on RA. Also had wheezing 
that improved with neb. Her respiratory status was monitored 
closely on this admission through vital signs. She did require 
extra diuresis given IVF resusciation and blood transfusion 
causing pulmonary edema. A Foley was temporarily placed to track 
I/O's. She was continued on her home carvedilol and losartan. 
Discharge weight was ***. 

#Delirium: Developed fluctuating hyper and hypo-active delirium 
on . Likely multifactorial etiology from prolonged 
hospitalization and rehabilitation stay, recent surgery and 
acute illness. Narcotics and benzos were d/ced and she improved 
steadily. Delirium precautions were maintained. She often was 
A+Ox3 and at the time of discharge was back to baseline. 

# Guaiac positive stool during admission. Hemodynamically stable 
without melena or overt blood. Family and team opted to plan for 
outpatient followup. Discharged on a daily PPI.

# Atrial fibrillation: patient was kept on telemonitoring and her 
home amiodarone was continued. She has been off warfarin giving 
history of bleeding complications.

# Hyperlipidemia: patient was continued on her home atorvastatin.

hydroxychloroquine.

# Hypothyroid: patient was continued on her home levothyroxine.

# GERD: patient was continued on her home pantoprazole.

degrees

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

 was a pleasure taking care of you during your admission to 
 . You were admitted for removal of hardware in your back at 
L4-L5. The surgery was successful and your surgeon was Dr. 
 . 

You were found to have low blood counts requiring blood 
transfusions. This was likely because of your infection (low 
production of blood) and also very small amount of blood loss in 
your stool (maybe because of your diarrhea). Your counts are 
stable, but you should have your blood checked after discharge.

There was briefly a concern for a heart attack, but our 
cardiologists felt this was NOT the case. 

Please follow up with your primary care doctor and other 
appointments. Please continue to take your home medications but 
avoid alprazalam and cyclobenzaprine, especially if you feel 
confused.

Sincerely,
Your team at 

¨ You have staples, keep your wound clean and dry until they are 
removed. 
¨ You should wear your brace when out of bed or when your head 
of bed is above 30 degrees.
¨ You may put the brace on at the edge of your bed.
¨ You may use a shower chair to bathe without the brace on.
¨ No tub baths or pool swimming for two weeks from your date of 
surgery.
¨ Do not smoke.
¨ No pulling up, lifting more than 10 lbs., or excessive bending 
or twisting.
¨ Limit your use of stairs to times per day.
¨ Have a friend or family member check your incision daily for 
signs of infection.
¨ Take your pain medication as instructed; you may find it best 
if taken in the morning when you wake-up for morning stiffness, 
and before bed for sleeping discomfort. Pain medication should 
be used as needed when you have pain. You do not need to take it 
if you do not have pain.
¨ Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc. for two weeks.
¨ Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as pain medicine 
(narcotics) can cause constipation. We recommend taking an over 
the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace) while 
taking narcotic pain medication.
¨ Clearance to drive and return to work will be addressed at 
your post-operative office visit.

ALL YOUR SURGEON IMMEDIATELY IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE 
FOLLOWING
¨ Pain that is continually increasing or not relieved by pain 
medicine.
¨ Any weakness, numbness, tingling in your extremities.
¨ Any signs of infection at the wound site: redness, swelling, 
tenderness, and drainage
¨ Fever greater than or equal to 101.5° F. 
¨ Loss of control of bowel or bladder functioning

#Hardware extrusion, possible osteomyelitis: The patient 
tolerated the procedure well but developed post-op delerium and 
hypotension. The patient's hardware was sent for culture as well 
as superficial and deep interoperative tissue cultures. There 
was no evidence of infection during the surgery. The patient was 
recovered in the PACU. The patients diet was advanced. On 
dressing saturated and replaced at 1000. Drainage was expected 
from the wound. The patient was evaluated by infectious disease 
and recommendations were made to discontinue the vancomycin and 
cefipime and start meropenum with a planned course of 6 weeks. 
Blood cultures and LFTs were sent at the recommendation of 
infectious disease. The patient was measured at the bedside for 
a lumbar brace and the patient was maintained on bedrest until 
the brace was obtained. The patient reported ongoing leg pain 
that was the same as pre operative leg pain and neurontin. The 
patients serum albumin was noted to be 3.1 and ensure three times a day

surgery for right knee pain given history of R total knee 
replacement. A joint aspiration was performed, which was not 
consistent with infection (WBC 230). A joint culture fluid is 
pending. X-ray of the right knee did not demonstrate any 
hardware complications. 

#Hypotension: Overnight, between and , Mrs. 
had one episode of hypotension with a systolic pressure in the 
high . She was given a fluid bolus of 500cc, for which she 
initially responded well to. Then, she was given her home 
carvedilol and torsemide which caused her again to become 
hypotensive. She was then transfused 1U pRBCs given her post-op 
anemia and transferred to medicine for further management. On 
transfer, ACS was ruled out with negative troponins. The 
etiology of her hypotension likely was a combination of anemia 
and medication effect. She was later hypertensive, and home 
medications were restarted. Stable at discharge

#Anemia: Patient had two periods of hemoglobin and hematocrit 
falling to or below ~7 and ~22 respectively, on and . 
Each time, she received 1 unit pRBC transfusion with appropriate 
response. She did have guiac positive stools this admission, but 
in the setting of diarrhea. She did not have any melena, bright 
red blood per rectum, or bleed at any other sites. Patient has 
low reticulocyte count, and iron studies consistent with anemia 
of chronic disease. Suspect bone marrow suppression and chronic 
disease, plus or minus microscopic blood losses. GI workup was 
deferred during inpatient stay, given overall clinical picture 
and discussion with patient and family, but can be further 
addressed as outpatient.

#Acute kidney injury: Also on , the patient's creatinine 
increased from 1.2 to 1.4 in the context of hypotension. She 
was continued on IV fluids while her oral intake was 
insufficient. She did develop pulmonary edema and increased O2 
requirement after receiving fluids on , thus though her 
creatinine remained slightly elevated, she was not given 
additional exogenous fluid due to concern for congestive heart failure. Her 
creatinine was monitored closely. Improved by discharge.

#New ECG changes, slight CK/CK-MB rise: There was concern that 
hypotension on was secondary to ACS given ECG changes and 
slight elevation in CK/CK-MB. Enzymes downtrended rapidly, 
suggesting alternate cause for rise. patient started on heparin drip, 
full dose aspirin, high dose statin and continued on home 
metoprolol. She received a repeat ECG that showed nonspecific T 
wave inversions, with plans for echo to further evaluate for 
wall motion abnormalities. She is already on cardioprotective 
medications for home medications. Heparin gtt was stopped after 
several hours when her troponins failed to elevate. Cardiology 
was consulted who recommended continuing her home cardiac 
medications. 

#Acute on Chronic systolic heart failure: On , patient 
developed increased O2 requirement to 6L but after a dose of IV 
lasix and her home torsemide, her O2 requirement is downtrending 
and she is now satting mid on RA. Also had wheezing 
that improved with neb. Her respiratory status was monitored 
closely on this admission through vital signs. She did require 
extra diuresis given IVF resusciation and blood transfusion 
causing pulmonary edema. A Foley was temporarily placed to track 
I/O's. She was continued on her home carvedilol and losartan. 
Discharge weight was ***. 

#Delirium: Developed fluctuating hyper and hypo-active delirium 
on . Likely multifactorial etiology from prolonged 
hospitalization and rehabilitation stay, recent surgery and 
acute illness. Narcotics and benzos were d/ced and she improved 
steadily. Delirium precautions were maintained. She often was 
A+Ox3 and at the time of discharge was back to baseline. 

# Guaiac positive stool during admission. Hemodynamically stable 
without melena or overt blood. Family and team opted to plan for 
outpatient followup. Discharged on a daily PPI.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,

 was a pleasure taking care of you during your admission to 
 . You were admitted for removal of hardware in your back at 
L4-L5. The surgery was successful and your surgeon was Dr. 
 . 

You were found to have low blood counts requiring blood 
transfusions. This was likely because of your infection (low 
production of blood) and also very small amount of blood loss in 
your stool (maybe because of your diarrhea). Your counts are 
stable, but you should have your blood checked after discharge.

There was briefly a concern for a heart attack, but our 
cardiologists felt this was NOT the case. 

Please follow up with your primary care doctor and other 
appointments. Please continue to take your home medications but 
avoid alprazalam and cyclobenzaprine, especially if you feel 
confused.

Sincerely,
Your team at 

¨ You have staples, keep your wound clean and dry until they are 
removed. 
¨ You should wear your brace when out of bed or when your head 
of bed is above 30 degrees.
¨ You may put the brace on at the edge of your bed.
¨ You may use a shower chair to bathe without the brace on.
¨ No tub baths or pool swimming for two weeks from your date of 
surgery.
¨ Do not smoke.
¨ No pulling up, lifting more than 10 lbs., or excessive bending 
or twisting.
¨ Limit your use of stairs to times per day.
¨ Have a friend or family member check your incision daily for 
signs of infection.
¨ Take your pain medication as instructed; you may find it best 
if taken in the morning when you wake-up for morning stiffness, 
and before bed for sleeping discomfort. Pain medication should 
be used as needed when you have pain. You do not need to take it 
if you do not have pain.
¨ Do not take any anti-inflammatory medications such as Motrin, 
Advil, Aspirin, and Ibuprofen etc. for two weeks.
¨ Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as pain medicine 
(narcotics) can cause constipation. We recommend taking an over 
the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace) while 
taking narcotic pain medication.
¨ Clearance to drive and return to work will be addressed at 
your post-operative office visit.

ALL YOUR SURGEON IMMEDIATELY IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE 
FOLLOWING
¨ Pain that is continually increasing or not relieved by pain 
medicine.
¨ Any weakness, numbness, tingling in your extremities.
¨ Any signs of infection at the wound site: redness, swelling, 
tenderness, and drainage
¨ Fever greater than or equal to 101.5° F. 
¨ Loss of control of bowel or bladder functioning

Post Operative Instructions

•Go home and rest today.

decision for 24 hours.
•You need to avoid strenuous activity for 2 weeks
•Do NOT lift any weight greater than 10 Lbs for 2 weeks.

FLUIDS & DIET
•Start with liquids and, if tolerated, you can eat normal 
foods. A soft diet is recommended.

MEDICATIONS
•Take pain medication as prescribed.
•Resume other medications as prescribed except any aspirin or 
aspirin containing products unless cleared by your surgeon.

•You had biopsies taken from inside your mouth, throat and/or 
voicebox which means you may have a sore throat for weeks 
where the biopsies were taken.
•Try to avoid hard foods with sharp edges such as potato chips 
or steak as this may be more difficult to swallow. Hot foods 
(temperature) and spicy foods will irritate your throat so try 
to avoid these.
•A soft diet is recommended and be sure to stay well hydrated 
with water and liquids.

•Fever greater than 101 degrees Fahrenheit
•Bleeding from your mouth or throat
•Difficulty swallowing

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Otolaryngology – Head and Neck Surgery
Post Operative Instructions 
Panendoscopy with biopsy

ACTIVITY
•Go home and rest today.
•If you have received general anesthesia, you may feel tired 
and drowsy for a few hours. You should not drink alcoholic 
beverages, drive a car, operate any machinery, or make legal 
decision for 24 hours.
•You need to avoid strenuous activity for 2 weeks
•Do NOT lift any weight greater than 10 Lbs for 2 weeks.

FLUIDS & DIET
•Start with liquids and, if tolerated, you can eat normal 
foods. A soft diet is recommended.

MEDICATIONS
•Take pain medication as prescribed.
•Resume other medications as prescribed except any aspirin or 
aspirin containing products unless cleared by your surgeon.

CARE OF PROCEDURE SITE
•You had biopsies taken from inside your mouth, throat and/or 
voicebox which means you may have a sore throat for weeks 
where the biopsies were taken.
•Try to avoid hard foods with sharp edges such as potato chips 
or steak as this may be more difficult to swallow. Hot foods 
(temperature) and spicy foods will irritate your throat so try 
to avoid these.
•A soft diet is recommended and be sure to stay well hydrated 
with water and liquids.

WHEN TO CALL YOUR DOCTOR
•Fever greater than 101 degrees Fahrenheit
•Bleeding from your mouth or throat
•Difficulty swallowing

For questions or problems, please call and ask to 
speak to the nurse during clinic hours.

operator to page the Otolaryngology resident on-call, pager

who presents with fall and SDH. She was evaluated by 
neurosurgery in the ED. patient is awake and per her daughter this is 
baseline mental status. She had repeat head CT in AM. This was 
read by neurosurgery as no change in regards to the extent of 
her bleed. She received dilantin for seizure prophylaxis, 700 mg 
loading dose then 100 mg three times a day for 7 days only. We had 2 major 
family meeting with the daughter and they wanted her to go home 
despite our advice. We recommended longer hospital stay and 
transfer back to rehab, thereafter. We were concerned about her 
decubitus ulcers and her nutrition and aspiration risk. We used 
the translator all the time. They understand the risk of 
progression of her ulcer and her malnutrition. SW will file a 
report concerning her care at home ( the are leaving with out 
nurses help at home as services have refused to deal with 
them because of the way the daughter manages the wounds with 
natural treatments ignoring their recommendations). Our wound 
care nurses recommended the following: 
- Aquacel Hydrofiber Dressing 4 X 4 Every 1 to 2 days: Please 
apply to right and left buttock skin wounds. 
- Allevyn 2 X 2 Bandage: apply to right and left buttock wounds 
every 1 to 2 days. 
- Bacitracin 500 unit/g Ointment. Please apply to shoulder skin 
wound daily after cleaning. 
She is leaving AMA. Neurosurgery recommended follow up with 
repeat CT of head in 4 weeks. I gave her prescription to do so 
with a phone number to call for appointment. We recommended the 
following for diet: 
- pureed diet with honey thickened liquids 
- aspiration precautions 
- crush medications for easy swallowing 
- Ensure supplement.
.
.
.
total discharge time 38 minutes.

days.

Chewable PO twice a day. 
4. Zinc Sulfate 220 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. 
 
5. Multi-Vitamin HP/Minerals Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO 
once a day. 
6. CT non contrast of head

weeks ( Please fax the results to Dr. office 
with neurosurgery. ( office number #

Home

fair. The daughter and the husband decided to take her home

Discharge Instructions:
You underwent surgery to remove the sarcoma tumor in your 
anterior abdominal wall. You have been recovering well, and the 
health of your abdominal wall is improving. The following is a 
summary of discharge instructions. 

MEDICATIONS 
1. Please resume all home medications, unless specifically 
advised not to take a particular medication. Please take any new 
medications as prescribed.
2. Please take all pain medications as prescribed, as needed. 
You may not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking 
narcotic pain medications. You may also take acetaminophen 
(Tylenol) as directed, but do not exceed 4000 mg in one day. 
Please note that Percocet and Vicodin have Tylenol as an active 
ingredient, so do not take these medications with additional 
Tylenol. 
3. An over-the-counter stool softener such as Colace (100mg 
twice daily) is recommended to prevent constipation while on 
narcotic pain medication.

WOUND CARE
1. Monitor the wounds for signs of infection, including redness 
that is spreading or increased drainage from wounds. Please call 
Dr. if you experience any of these symptoms.

ACTIVITY
1. No strenuous activity until cleared by Dr. .
2. No showering until cleared by Dr. sponge baths only.
 
Call the office IMMEDIATELY if you have any of the LABTEST following: 1. Signs of infection: fever with chills, increased redness, 
swelling, warmth or tenderness at the surgical site, or unusual 
drainage from the incision(s). 
2. A large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s). 

3. Fever greater than 101.5 oF 
4. Severe pain NOT relieved by your medication. 
 
Return to the ER if: 
* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your 
medications. 
* If you have shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) 
degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or 
discharge from incision, chest pain, shortness of breath, or 
anything else that is troubling you. 
* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that 
concern you.

 : Diagnostic left catheterization
 
History of Present Illness:
Mr. is a year old gentleman with a PMH of 
hypertension, and coronary artery disease status post inferior wall 
STEMI in treated with BMS to RCA and circumflex. His 
STEMI was complicated by cardiogenic shock necessitating IABP 
placement and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. After his STEMI 
a TTE demonstrated an EF of prompting initiation of 
warfarin for LV thrombus prophylaxis. His symptoms since the 
infarct have been consistent with low flow, low EF 
failure. He was admitted on for an elective myocardial 
viability study for consideration of revascularization versus 
ICD placement. A dobutamine stress echocardiogram revealed lack 
of viable myocardium; a subsequent cardiopulmonary stress test 
revealed very poor exercise tolerance without anginal symptoms 
or ST segment changes, and a flat blood pressure response to 
exercise. Due to the lack of viable myocardium, he was not felt 
to be a candidate for revascularization and underwent successful 
ICD placement. 
 
He was most recently hospitalized in the CCU from 
 for elective RHC as part of a pre-cardiac 
transplant work-up. RHC at the time showed severe pulmonary 
arterial hypertension, elevated PCW c/w severe left ventricular 
diastolic HF. He was diuresed effectively and sent home on 
torsemide; during the admission his warfarin was stopped as it 
had been in place for greater than 3 months.

He presents this admission with dyspnea and malaise. He will be 
admitted to the CCU for right catheterization and Swan 
placement for optimization of failure management. He 
reports that since his discharge on , he has been 
progressively more short of breath, especially with activity. He 
notes anorexia as well. He specifically denies orthopnea and 
PND. He feels his pedal edema is decreased. His weight is 
decreased from his discharge weight (208->205 lbs). He reports 
excellent medication compliance. He denies excessive food or 
salt intake over the .

Right cath today demonstrated elevated pressures 
throughout: RV 68/14, RVEDP 24, PA 68/38 with mean 49, PCW 35, 
CO 2.7, CI 1.25.

 was referred to the lab for viability evaluation. The 
patient was infused with 5 and 10 mcg/kg/min of dobutamine over 

11 minutes. No arm, neck, back or chest discomfort was reported 

by the patient throughout the study. There were no significant 
ST segment changes during the infusion or in recovery. The 
rhythm was sinus with rare isolated vpbs. Appropriate 
hemodynamic response to low dose dobutamine infusion. 
IMPRESSION: No anginal type symptoms or significant ST segment 
changes.

function with dobutamine, suggesting lack of viability of 
myocardium.

INTERPRETATION: yo man with hypertension and severe CAD with ischemic 

cardiomyopathy and LVEF of ~ , recent inferior STEMI in 
 with PCI to RCA and LCx c/b cardiogenic shock 
necessitating IABP followed by pulseless VT, recent Dobutamine 
ECHO revealing no viability was referred for evaluation. The 
patient completed 6 minutes of a Gervino protocol which was 
stopped at the patient's request secondary to marked fatigue. No 
 
chest, back, neck or arm discomforts were reported during the 
procedure. No lightheadedness of pre-syncope was reported. No 
significant ST segment changes were noted. The rhythm was sinus 

with rare isolated VPBs and one ventricular couplet. The blood 
pressure response to exercise was flat with a subtle drop in 
systolic blood pressure from baseline. Blunted rate 
response.

in treated with BMS to RCA and circumflex. His STEMI

placement for an EF of . He presents for RHC/Swan

shock and pulseless VT. Now status post ICD implant on for an 
EF of . He presented this admission for RHC/Swan placement 
and optimization of HF management. RHC revealed PCW of 35, CO 
2.7 and CI 1.25. He was aggressively diuresed with a furosemide 
drip and started on dobumatine and milrinone. He tripled his CI 
on inotropes which was thought to represent the existence of of 
viable myocardium. This was in contradiction to the dobatamine 
stess performed on which showed no significant 
augmentation of left ventricular systolic function. FDG PET 
showed minimal tracer uptake in the infero-lateral wall and 
moderately decreased tracer uptake in the anteroseptal wall 
extending to the apex. Repeat echo showed LVEF 15% and 2+ MR. 
cardiac cath was performed on to attempt 
revascularization of the LAD territory, however the proximal 
lesion could not be crossed. Milrinone was discontinued as the 
patient was doing very well clinically. He was started on 
digoxin and PO torsemide. The patient's urine output and 
hemodynamics were preserved the first day after discontinuation 
of milrinone, but quickly deterioriated. He was restarted on 
milrinone c/b hypotension and . He was given gentle IVFs, 
restarted on dobutamine and supported with Levophed overnight. 
The decision was made to bridge to an LVAD with an outpatient 
dobutamine infusion. Transplant evaluation continues. Colonscopy 
on w/o signs of malignancy. Vaccinations were 
reconciled. 

# CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: status post inferior STEMI in w/ 
cardiac cath demonstrating 100% proximal LAD, 95% ramus, 90% 
proximal circumflex, 100% OM1/OM2, and 100% RCA occlusions, 
prompting bare metal stenting of the RCA and LCx. ECG on 
admission was without new ischemic changes. Troponin slightly 
elevated at 0.16, CKMB 5. Repeat cardiac catheterization this 
admission showed no change in coronary anatomy and a failed 
attempt to revascularize the LAD territory. LIMA to LAD was 
considered however ultimately determined to be low yield due to 
the patient's severely depressed EF. The patient was continued 
on atorvastatin, Plavix and aspirin.

# VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA: status post ICD placement. The patient was 
monitored on telemetry and had no events. Electrolytes were 
aggressively repleted.

# HYPERTENSION: The patient was not hypertensive during this 
admission. 

# TRANSAMINITIS: Secondary to congestive hepatopathy. 
 negative. Iron studies within normal limits. Abd 
ultrasound: the liver is normal in size, 
echogenicity and architecture. LFTs trended down on inotropes 
and w/ aggressive diuresis. 

# URINARY RETENTION: Foley catheter removal resulted in 
significant urinary retention with PVR as high as 800mL. Urology 
was consulted and recommended tamsulosin. The patient refused to 
straight catheterizer himself at home. He was therefore 
discharged with an indwelling Foley and urology followup.

# URINARY TRACT INFECTION: UTI two weeks prior to admission 
treated with ciprofloxacin. Repeat urinalysis w/o evidence of 
UTI. The patient complained of urgency and frequency likely from 
BPH. Urine culture w/o growth at discharge.

You were admitted to the hosital this time for acute 
decompensated failure meaning that the pumping fucnction 
of your was decreased and you were unable to move fluid 
forward so it back up within your lungs, ankles and belly. Over 
the course of your hositalization we placed you on medication 
called Dobutamine to help your pump more effectively. We 
also made medication changes to help you get rid of the extra 
fluid you had accumulated. Initiatlly while we did this with the 
help of a right catheter to directly measure the pressures 
in the chambers of your and to see how you responded to 
the medciation we then removed the when you were stable 
enough and we knew the medications were working well. 

It is of the utmost importance that you continue to weigh 
yourself every morning, call Dr. your weight goes up 
more than 3 lbs in two days or more than 5 lbs in 3 days. Also 
try to avoid a diet high in salt this will help you to not 
retain as much fluid.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. is a year old gentleman with a PMH of 
hypertension and coronary artery disease status post inferior wall STEMI 
in treated with BMS to RCA and circumflex. His STEMI 
was complicated by cardiogenic shock necessitating IABP 
placement and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. He is status post ICD 
placement for an EF of . He presents for RHC/Swan 
placement and optimization of HF management.

# ACUTE ON CHRONIC SYSTOLIC HF: status post large MI c/b cardiogenic 
shock and pulseless VT. Now status post ICD implant on for an 
EF of . He presented this admission for RHC/Swan placement 
and optimization of HF management. RHC revealed PCW of 35, CO 
2.7 and CI 1.25. He was aggressively diuresed with a furosemide 
drip and started on dobumatine and milrinone. He tripled his CI 
on inotropes which was thought to represent the existence of of 
viable myocardium. This was in contradiction to the dobatamine 
stess performed on which showed no significant 
augmentation of left ventricular systolic function. FDG PET 
showed minimal tracer uptake in the infero-lateral wall and 
moderately decreased tracer uptake in the anteroseptal wall 
extending to the apex. Repeat echo showed LVEF 15% and 2+ MR. 
cardiac cath was performed on to attempt 
revascularization of the LAD territory, however the proximal 
lesion could not be crossed. Milrinone was discontinued as the 
patient was doing very well clinically. He was started on 
digoxin and PO torsemide. The patient's urine output and 
hemodynamics were preserved the first day after discontinuation 
of milrinone, but quickly deterioriated. He was restarted on 
milrinone c/b hypotension and . He was given gentle IVFs, 
restarted on dobutamine and supported with Levophed overnight. 
The decision was made to bridge to an LVAD with an outpatient 
dobutamine infusion. Transplant evaluation continues. Colonscopy 
on w/o signs of malignancy. Vaccinations were 
reconciled. 

# CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: status post inferior STEMI in w/ 
cardiac cath demonstrating 100% proximal LAD, 95% ramus, 90% 
proximal circumflex, 100% OM1/OM2, and 100% RCA occlusions, 
prompting bare metal stenting of the RCA and LCx. ECG on 
admission was without new ischemic changes. Troponin slightly 
elevated at 0.16, CKMB 5. Repeat cardiac catheterization this 
admission showed no change in coronary anatomy and a failed 
attempt to revascularize the LAD territory. LIMA to LAD was 
considered however ultimately determined to be low yield due to 
the patient's severely depressed EF. The patient was continued 
on atorvastatin, Plavix and aspirin.

# VENTRICULAR TACHYCARDIA: status post ICD placement. The patient was 
monitored on telemetry and had no events. Electrolytes were 
aggressively repleted.

# HYPERTENSION: The patient was not hypertensive during this 
admission. 

# TRANSAMINITIS: Secondary to congestive hepatopathy. 
 negative. Iron studies within normal limits. Abd 
ultrasound: the liver is normal in size, 
echogenicity and architecture. LFTs trended down on inotropes 
and w/ aggressive diuresis. 

# URINARY RETENTION: Foley catheter removal resulted in 
significant urinary retention with PVR as high as 800mL. Urology 
was consulted and recommended tamsulosin. The patient refused to 
straight catheterizer himself at home. He was therefore 
discharged with an indwelling Foley and urology followup.

# URINARY TRACT INFECTION: UTI two weeks prior to admission 
treated with ciprofloxacin. Repeat urinalysis w/o evidence of 
UTI. The patient complained of urgency and frequency likely from 
BPH. Urine culture w/o growth at discharge.

# THE GOUT: complains of left ankle pain/gout flare. Received colchicine 
x 1 on .

Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hosital this time for acute 
decompensated failure meaning that the pumping fucnction 
of your was decreased and you were unable to move fluid 
forward so it back up within your lungs, ankles and belly. Over 
the course of your hositalization we placed you on medication 
called Dobutamine to help your pump more effectively. We 
also made medication changes to help you get rid of the extra 
fluid you had accumulated. Initiatlly while we did this with the 
help of a right catheter to directly measure the pressures 
in the chambers of your and to see how you responded to 
the medciation we then removed the when you were stable 
enough and we knew the medications were working well. 

It is of the utmost importance that you continue to weigh 
yourself every morning, call Dr. your weight goes up 
more than 3 lbs in two days or more than 5 lbs in 3 days. Also 
try to avoid a diet high in salt this will help you to not 
retain as much fluid.

extending to the apex. Repeat echo showed LVEF 15% and 2+ MR.

lesion could not be crossed. Milrinone was discontinued as the 
patient was doing very well clinically. He was started on 
digoxin and PO torsemide. The patient's urine output and 
hemodynamics were preserved the first day after discontinuation 
of milrinone, but quickly deterioriated. He was restarted on 
milrinone c/b hypotension and . He was given gentle IVFs, 
restarted on dobutamine and supported with Levophed overnight.

dobutamine infusion. Transplant evaluation continues. Colonscopy
