Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hosital this time for acute 
decompensated failure meaning that the pumping fucnction 
of your was decreased and you were unable to move fluid 
forward so it back up within your lungs, ankles and belly. Over 
the course of your hositalization we placed you on medication 
called Dobutamine to help your pump more effectively. We 
also made medication changes to help you get rid of the extra 
fluid you had accumulated. Initiatlly while we did this with the 
help of a right catheter to directly measure the pressures 
in the chambers of your and to see how you responded to 
the medciation we then removed the when you were stable 
enough and we knew the medications were working well. 

It is of the utmost importance that you continue to weigh 
yourself every morning, call Dr. your weight goes up 
more than 3 lbs in two days or more than 5 lbs in 3 days. Also 
try to avoid a diet high in salt this will help you to not 
retain as much fluid.

TECHNIQUE: Transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound 
examinations of the 
pelvis were performed, the latter for better delineation of 
uterine /ovarian 
anatomy. Color and spectral Doppler analysis of the bilateral 
ovaries was 
also performed. 
 
FINDINGS: 
 
The uterus is enlarged, measuring 7.8 x 5.2 x 5.0 cm. The 
endometrium is 
thickened and heterogeneous at the fundus, measuring up to 14 mm 
in thickness. 
On Doppler analysis, there is abnormal vascularity in the 
endometrium with 
demonstrable flow on spectral Doppler. No dominant feeding or 
draining vessel 
is identified. Both ovaries are normal in size and appearance. 
There are 
demonstrable arterial and venous waveforms in the bilateral 
ovaries on Doppler 
evaluation. Trace free fluid is noted around the right adnexa. 

 
LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Vascularized retained products of conception. 
 
2. Normal-sized ovaries with symmetrical blood flow 
bilaterally. 
 
3. Trace free fluid around the right adnexa is likely 
physiologic. 
 
 
The study and the report were reviewed by the staff radiologist. 

 
 . 
 . 
 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
On , Ms. was admitted to the gynecology service 
from the emergency room for observation for retained products of 
conception after a termination. Please refer to the H&P for full 
details. 

The patient remained hemodynamically stable, with scant bleeding 
on her pad. Her uterine cramps were controlled with ibuprofen 
and acetaminophen. Her transvaginal ultrasound confirmed 
retained products of conception. The patient was thoroughly 
counselled on all of her options including expectant management, 
misoprostol or dilation and suction curretage. The patient opted 
for expectant management, which is reasonable as she is 
currently hemodynamically stable.

The patient will f/u in 1 week with a TVUS to assess the amount 
of retained products. If there is still a significant amount 
present, we will re-evaluate her situation and possibly proceed 
with a D&C at that time. 

Otherwise, at the time of discharge, she was tolerating a 
regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, 
and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then 
discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up 
scheduled.

Meds: keppra for seizure ppx, amoxicillin x1 wk since D+C

bilaterally.

physiologic.

from the emergency room for observation for retained products of 
conception after a termination. Please refer to the H&P for full 
details. 

The patient remained hemodynamically stable, with scant bleeding 
on her pad. Her uterine cramps were controlled with ibuprofen 
and acetaminophen. Her transvaginal ultrasound confirmed 
retained products of conception. The patient was thoroughly 
counselled on all of her options including expectant management, 
misoprostol or dilation and suction curretage. The patient opted 
for expectant management, which is reasonable as she is 
currently hemodynamically stable.

The patient will f/u in 1 week with a TVUS to assess the amount 
of retained products. If there is still a significant amount 
present, we will re-evaluate her situation and possibly proceed 
with a D&C at that time. 

Otherwise, at the time of discharge, she was tolerating a 
regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, 
and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then 
discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up 
scheduled.

Name: Unit No: 
 
Admission Date: Discharge Date: 
 
Date of Birth: Sex: M
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
 
 
Attending: .
 
Chief Complaint:
Dyspnea 
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Bronchoscopy 

 
History of Present Illness:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias. patient reports 
that he was in his usual state of health when he developed 
flu-like symptoms days ago with myalgias, general malaise, 
and poor appetite. He began to then have progressively 
worsening dyspnea, feeling short of breath even while walking to 
the restroom. Denies fevers, cough, sore throat, rhinorrhea, 
nasal congestion. patient reports he has already received the flu 
shot this season. Reports no family members are ill. Denies 
recent travel outside the country. He is a former smoke, quit 
 years ago. 
.
Of note, patient also states that he was diagnosed with a bacterial 
infection in his blood in and treated at . CT chest was 
performed at after discharge for follow-up on that 
showed paraseptal/centrilobular emphysema (could not rule out 
neoplasm of left lower lobe), multiple pulmonary nodules. 
Subsequent follow-up CT chest on showed decreased size

Brief Hospital Course:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias.
.
#Dyspnea: patient was admitted with profound dyspnea, feeling shortness of breath 
while ambulating to restroom. CXR showed b/l mid and lower lung 
ground glass opacities. CTA did not show PE. CTA chest showed 
emphysema and bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities and 
bibasal consolidations likely reflect resolving pulmonary edema 
or multifocal lung infections. He was febrile with temps in 100s 
and required oxygen by nasal cannula initially. He was 
started on empiric treatment for CAP with levofloxacin. He was 
also put on standing nebulizers for COPD. Pulmonary consult was 
obtained. Numerous labs were sent to further elucidate etiology 
of his multifocal pneumonia. HIV antibody was negative, HIV 
viral load undetectable. Urine legionella also negative. Induced 
sputum was largely unremarkable (PCP negative, legionella 
negative, AFB negative). He underwent bronchoscopy as he 
was not showing improvement on treatment for CAP (continued 
fevers, continued hypoxia requiring oxygen). However, 
bronchoscopy had to be prematurely aborted as patient had oxygen 
desaturation to . He was sent to the ICU for one night of 
further monitoring after his bronchoscopy and was weaned to 
oxygen by time of transfer back to the medical floor. need 
lung biopsy to elucidate etiology; malignancy is a possibility. 
Of note, beta glucan in blood test returned positive; urine 
histoplasma was pending at time of discharge, fungal/AFB blood 
cultures were also pending, aspergillus and cryptococcal was 
negative. By time of discharge, he was weaned to room air and 
ambulatory oxygen saturations remained in low on room air. 
He had been afebrile for >24hours by time of discharge and had 
completed a 10 day course of levofloxacin. He was discharged 
with outpatient pulmonary follow-up and will need repeat CT 
chest and PFTs prior to his next pulmonary visit. 
.
#hypertension, benign: Previously, patient reported that he had been on 
lisinopril but this was discontinued due to cough. He was then 
placed on HCTZ by his PCP. HCTZ was held due to hyponatremia 
during this admission. He was instead started on losartan for BP 
control. It was started at 25mg and increased to 50mg daily. 
.
#Hyponatremia: patient with sodium of 126 on admission. patient appeared 
volume depleted on admission with urine electrolytes that were 
consistent with volume depletion. His hydrochlorothiazide was 
discontinued given his hyponatremia. He was given IV fluids 
without improvement in sodium levels. He was then placed on 
fluid restriction with slow improvement in sodium levels to 132 
by time of discharge. He should have his electrolytes rechecked 
at his next PCP .
.
#Bacteremia: patient reported history of a "bacterial infection" 
treated at in late . patient gave consent to 
obtain his medical records from OSH. Per records, his blood 
culture at that time grew fusobacter thought to be from 
appendix, treated with antibiotics. Incidental finding on cxr 
during that hospitalization showed a pulmonary nodule which was 
followed up by a CT chest in and showing left 
lower lobe consolidation and multiple nodules. He was thereafter 
lost to follow-up. Blood cultures during this hospital stay did 
not show any growth, though fungal/AFB blood cultures were still 
pending by time of discharge. 

#GERD: patient reported symptoms of heartburn during hospital stay. 
He was started on omeprazole 20mg daily.

HCTZ 25mg daily 
Lisinopril 20mg daily (discontinued and switched to HCTZ due to 
cough)

Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you in the hospital. You were 
admitted for shortness of breath. A CT scan of your chest 
showed wide spread pneumonia and emphysema. You were treated 
with antibiotics and nebulizers. You were seen by our pulmonary 
consultants who attempted to perform a bronchoscopy. This 
procedure had to be prematurely aborted because your oxygen 
levels became low and you were admitted to the intensive care 
unit for one night for closer monitoring. With antibiotics and 
nebulizers, your oxygen levels improved and your fevers 
resolved. You will need close follow-up with your PCP and with 
your new pulmonary doctor. You will see the pulmonologist 
within of discharge. Please talk to your PCP about 
scheduling repeat CAT scan of your chest before this 
appointment. 

Your PCP should also recheck your electrolytes because your 
sodium level was low during this hospital admission and because 
you were started on a new medication for your blood pressure. 

Your liver enzymes were also elevated during this admission. 
Several blood tests were sent but we did not have the final 
results before you were discharged. Your PCP should follow up 
the final results and recheck your liver function tests when you 
are seen.

The following changes were made to your medications:
1) START losartan 50mg once a day for your blood pressure
2) START omeprazole 20mg once a day for heartburn
3) START albuterol inhaler 2puffs every as needed for 
shortness of breath
4) STOP hydrochlorothiazide

Brief Hospital Course:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias.
.
#Dyspnea: patient was admitted with profound dyspnea, feeling shortness of breath 
while ambulating to restroom. CXR showed b/l mid and lower lung 
ground glass opacities. CTA did not show PE. CTA chest showed 
emphysema and bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities and 
bibasal consolidations likely reflect resolving pulmonary edema 
or multifocal lung infections. He was febrile with temps in 100s 
and required oxygen by nasal cannula initially. He was 
started on empiric treatment for CAP with levofloxacin. He was 
also put on standing nebulizers for COPD. Pulmonary consult was 
obtained. Numerous labs were sent to further elucidate etiology 
of his multifocal pneumonia. HIV antibody was negative, HIV 
viral load undetectable. Urine legionella also negative. Induced 
sputum was largely unremarkable (PCP negative, legionella 
negative, AFB negative). He underwent bronchoscopy as he 
was not showing improvement on treatment for CAP (continued 
fevers, continued hypoxia requiring oxygen). However, 
bronchoscopy had to be prematurely aborted as patient had oxygen 
desaturation to . He was sent to the ICU for one night of 
further monitoring after his bronchoscopy and was weaned to 
oxygen by time of transfer back to the medical floor. need 
lung biopsy to elucidate etiology; malignancy is a possibility. 
Of note, beta glucan in blood test returned positive; urine 
histoplasma was pending at time of discharge, fungal/AFB blood 
cultures were also pending, aspergillus and cryptococcal was 
negative. By time of discharge, he was weaned to room air and 
ambulatory oxygen saturations remained in low on room air. 
He had been afebrile for >24hours by time of discharge and had 
completed a 10 day course of levofloxacin. He was discharged 
with outpatient pulmonary follow-up and will need repeat CT 
chest and PFTs prior to his next pulmonary visit. 
.
#hypertension, benign: Previously, patient reported that he had been on 
lisinopril but this was discontinued due to cough. He was then 
placed on HCTZ by his PCP. HCTZ was held due to hyponatremia 
during this admission. He was instead started on losartan for BP 
control. It was started at 25mg and increased to 50mg daily. 
.
#Hyponatremia: patient with sodium of 126 on admission. patient appeared 
volume depleted on admission with urine electrolytes that were 
consistent with volume depletion. His hydrochlorothiazide was 
discontinued given his hyponatremia. He was given IV fluids 
without improvement in sodium levels. He was then placed on 
fluid restriction with slow improvement in sodium levels to 132 
by time of discharge. He should have his electrolytes rechecked 
at his next PCP .
.
#Bacteremia: patient reported history of a "bacterial infection" 
treated at in late . patient gave consent to 
obtain his medical records from OSH. Per records, his blood 
culture at that time grew fusobacter thought to be from 
appendix, treated with antibiotics. Incidental finding on cxr 
during that hospitalization showed a pulmonary nodule which was 
followed up by a CT chest in and showing left 
lower lobe consolidation and multiple nodules. He was thereafter 
lost to follow-up. Blood cultures during this hospital stay did 
not show any growth, though fungal/AFB blood cultures were still 
pending by time of discharge. 

#GERD: patient reported symptoms of heartburn during hospital stay. 
He was started on omeprazole 20mg daily. 

 
Medications on Admission:
HCTZ 25mg daily 
Lisinopril 20mg daily (discontinued and switched to HCTZ due to 
cough)

Two (2) puffs Inhalation every hours as needed for shortness 
of breath or wheezing.

Home

GERD

Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you in the hospital. You were 
admitted for shortness of breath. A CT scan of your chest 
showed wide spread pneumonia and emphysema. You were treated 
with antibiotics and nebulizers. You were seen by our pulmonary 
consultants who attempted to perform a bronchoscopy. This 
procedure had to be prematurely aborted because your oxygen 
levels became low and you were admitted to the intensive care 
unit for one night for closer monitoring. With antibiotics and 
nebulizers, your oxygen levels improved and your fevers 
resolved. You will need close follow-up with your PCP and with 
your new pulmonary doctor. You will see the pulmonologist 
within of discharge. Please talk to your PCP about 
scheduling repeat CAT scan of your chest before this 
appointment. 

Your PCP should also recheck your electrolytes because your 
sodium level was low during this hospital admission and because 
you were started on a new medication for your blood pressure. 

Your liver enzymes were also elevated during this admission. 
Several blood tests were sent but we did not have the final 
results before you were discharged. Your PCP should follow up 
the final results and recheck your liver function tests when you 
are seen.

The following changes were made to your medications:
1) START losartan 50mg once a day for your blood pressure
2) START omeprazole 20mg once a day for heartburn
3) START albuterol inhaler 2puffs every as needed for 
shortness of breath
4) STOP hydrochlorothiazide

Brief Hospital Course:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias.
.
#Dyspnea: patient was admitted with profound dyspnea, feeling shortness of breath 
while ambulating to restroom. CXR showed b/l mid and lower lung 
ground glass opacities. CTA did not show PE. CTA chest showed 
emphysema and bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities and 
bibasal consolidations likely reflect resolving pulmonary edema 
or multifocal lung infections. He was febrile with temps in 100s 
and required oxygen by nasal cannula initially. He was 
started on empiric treatment for CAP with levofloxacin. He was 
also put on standing nebulizers for COPD. Pulmonary consult was 
obtained. Numerous labs were sent to further elucidate etiology 
of his multifocal pneumonia. HIV antibody was negative, HIV 
viral load undetectable. Urine legionella also negative. Induced 
sputum was largely unremarkable (PCP negative, legionella 
negative, AFB negative). He underwent bronchoscopy as he 
was not showing improvement on treatment for CAP (continued 
fevers, continued hypoxia requiring oxygen). However, 
bronchoscopy had to be prematurely aborted as patient had oxygen 
desaturation to . He was sent to the ICU for one night of 
further monitoring after his bronchoscopy and was weaned to 
oxygen by time of transfer back to the medical floor. need 
lung biopsy to elucidate etiology; malignancy is a possibility. 
Of note, beta glucan in blood test returned positive; urine 
histoplasma was pending at time of discharge, fungal/AFB blood 
cultures were also pending, aspergillus and cryptococcal was 
negative. By time of discharge, he was weaned to room air and 
ambulatory oxygen saturations remained in low on room air. 
He had been afebrile for >24hours by time of discharge and had 
completed a 10 day course of levofloxacin. He was discharged 
with outpatient pulmonary follow-up and will need repeat CT 
chest and PFTs prior to his next pulmonary visit. 
.
#hypertension, benign: Previously, patient reported that he had been on 
lisinopril but this was discontinued due to cough. He was then 
placed on HCTZ by his PCP. HCTZ was held due to hyponatremia 
during this admission. He was instead started on losartan for BP 
control. It was started at 25mg and increased to 50mg daily. 
.
#Hyponatremia: patient with sodium of 126 on admission. patient appeared 
volume depleted on admission with urine electrolytes that were 
consistent with volume depletion. His hydrochlorothiazide was 
discontinued given his hyponatremia. He was given IV fluids 
without improvement in sodium levels. He was then placed on 
fluid restriction with slow improvement in sodium levels to 132 
by time of discharge. He should have his electrolytes rechecked 
at his next PCP .
.
#Bacteremia: patient reported history of a "bacterial infection" 
treated at in late . patient gave consent to 
obtain his medical records from OSH. Per records, his blood 
culture at that time grew fusobacter thought to be from 
appendix, treated with antibiotics. Incidental finding on cxr 
during that hospitalization showed a pulmonary nodule which was 
followed up by a CT chest in and showing left 
lower lobe consolidation and multiple nodules. He was thereafter 
lost to follow-up. Blood cultures during this hospital stay did 
not show any growth, though fungal/AFB blood cultures were still 
pending by time of discharge. 

#GERD: patient reported symptoms of heartburn during hospital stay. 
He was started on omeprazole 20mg daily.

Brief Hospital Course:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias.
.
#Dyspnea: patient was admitted with profound dyspnea, feeling shortness of breath 
while ambulating to restroom. CXR showed b/l mid and lower lung 
ground glass opacities. CTA did not show PE. CTA chest showed 
emphysema and bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities and 
bibasal consolidations likely reflect resolving pulmonary edema 
or multifocal lung infections. He was febrile with temps in 100s 
and required oxygen by nasal cannula initially. He was 
started on empiric treatment for CAP with levofloxacin. He was 
also put on standing nebulizers for COPD. Pulmonary consult was 
obtained. Numerous labs were sent to further elucidate etiology 
of his multifocal pneumonia. HIV antibody was negative, HIV 
viral load undetectable. Urine legionella also negative. Induced 
sputum was largely unremarkable (PCP negative, legionella 
negative, AFB negative). He underwent bronchoscopy as he 
was not showing improvement on treatment for CAP (continued 
fevers, continued hypoxia requiring oxygen). However, 
bronchoscopy had to be prematurely aborted as patient had oxygen 
desaturation to . He was sent to the ICU for one night of 
further monitoring after his bronchoscopy and was weaned to 
oxygen by time of transfer back to the medical floor. need 
lung biopsy to elucidate etiology; malignancy is a possibility. 
Of note, beta glucan in blood test returned positive; urine 
histoplasma was pending at time of discharge, fungal/AFB blood 
cultures were also pending, aspergillus and cryptococcal was 
negative. By time of discharge, he was weaned to room air and 
ambulatory oxygen saturations remained in low on room air. 
He had been afebrile for >24hours by time of discharge and had 
completed a 10 day course of levofloxacin. He was discharged 
with outpatient pulmonary follow-up and will need repeat CT 
chest and PFTs prior to his next pulmonary visit. 
.
#hypertension, benign: Previously, patient reported that he had been on 
lisinopril but this was discontinued due to cough. He was then 
placed on HCTZ by his PCP. HCTZ was held due to hyponatremia 
during this admission. He was instead started on losartan for BP 
control. It was started at 25mg and increased to 50mg daily. 
.
#Hyponatremia: patient with sodium of 126 on admission. patient appeared 
volume depleted on admission with urine electrolytes that were 
consistent with volume depletion. His hydrochlorothiazide was 
discontinued given his hyponatremia. He was given IV fluids 
without improvement in sodium levels. He was then placed on 
fluid restriction with slow improvement in sodium levels to 132 
by time of discharge. He should have his electrolytes rechecked 
at his next PCP .
.
#Bacteremia: patient reported history of a "bacterial infection" 
treated at in late . patient gave consent to 
obtain his medical records from OSH. Per records, his blood 
culture at that time grew fusobacter thought to be from 
appendix, treated with antibiotics. Incidental finding on cxr 
during that hospitalization showed a pulmonary nodule which was 
followed up by a CT chest in and showing left 
lower lobe consolidation and multiple nodules. He was thereafter 
lost to follow-up. Blood cultures during this hospital stay did 
not show any growth, though fungal/AFB blood cultures were still 
pending by time of discharge. 

#GERD: patient reported symptoms of heartburn during hospital stay. 
He was started on omeprazole 20mg daily. 

 
Medications on Admission:
HCTZ 25mg daily 
Lisinopril 20mg daily (discontinued and switched to HCTZ due to 
cough)

 
Discharge LABTEST Medications: 1. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/Actuation HFA Aerosol Inhaler Sig: 
Two (2) puffs Inhalation every hours as needed for shortness 
of breath or wheezing.
Disp:*1 inhaler* Refills:*0*
2. omeprazole 20 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) 
Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.)(s)* Refills:*0*
3. losartan 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
Disp:*30 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary:
Multifocal pneumonia
Emphysema
Hyponatremia

Secondary:
GERD
Hypertension

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you in the hospital. You were 
admitted for shortness of breath. A CT scan of your chest 
showed wide spread pneumonia and emphysema. You were treated 
with antibiotics and nebulizers. You were seen by our pulmonary 
consultants who attempted to perform a bronchoscopy. This 
procedure had to be prematurely aborted because your oxygen 
levels became low and you were admitted to the intensive care 
unit for one night for closer monitoring. With antibiotics and 
nebulizers, your oxygen levels improved and your fevers 
resolved. You will need close follow-up with your PCP and with 
your new pulmonary doctor. You will see the pulmonologist 
within of discharge. Please talk to your PCP about 
scheduling repeat CAT scan of your chest before this 
appointment. 

Your PCP should also recheck your electrolytes because your 
sodium level was low during this hospital admission and because 
you were started on a new medication for your blood pressure. 

Your liver enzymes were also elevated during this admission. 
Several blood tests were sent but we did not have the final 
results before you were discharged. Your PCP should follow up 
the final results and recheck your liver function tests when you 
are seen.

The following changes were made to your medications:
1) START losartan 50mg once a day for your blood pressure
2) START omeprazole 20mg once a day for heartburn
3) START albuterol inhaler 2puffs every as needed for 
shortness of breath
4) STOP hydrochlorothiazide

Brief Hospital Course:
 male with history of hypertension and history of "bacterial infection 
in blood" in who presented today to ED with days of 
worsening dyspnea, general malaise, and myalgias.
.
#Dyspnea: patient was admitted with profound dyspnea, feeling shortness of breath 
while ambulating to restroom. CXR showed b/l mid and lower lung 
ground glass opacities. CTA did not show PE. CTA chest showed 
emphysema and bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities and 
bibasal consolidations likely reflect resolving pulmonary edema 
or multifocal lung infections. He was febrile with temps in 100s 
and required oxygen by nasal cannula initially. He was 
started on empiric treatment for CAP with levofloxacin. He was 
also put on standing nebulizers for COPD. Pulmonary consult was 
obtained. Numerous labs were sent to further elucidate etiology 
of his multifocal pneumonia. HIV antibody was negative, HIV 
viral load undetectable. Urine legionella also negative. Induced 
sputum was largely unremarkable (PCP negative, legionella 
negative, AFB negative). He underwent bronchoscopy as he 
was not showing improvement on treatment for CAP (continued 
fevers, continued hypoxia requiring oxygen). However, 
bronchoscopy had to be prematurely aborted as patient had oxygen 
desaturation to . He was sent to the ICU for one night of 
further monitoring after his bronchoscopy and was weaned to 
oxygen by time of transfer back to the medical floor. need 
lung biopsy to elucidate etiology; malignancy is a possibility. 
Of note, beta glucan in blood test returned positive; urine 
histoplasma was pending at time of discharge, fungal/AFB blood 
cultures were also pending, aspergillus and cryptococcal was 
negative. By time of discharge, he was weaned to room air and 
ambulatory oxygen saturations remained in low on room air. 
He had been afebrile for >24hours by time of discharge and had 
completed a 10 day course of levofloxacin. He was discharged 
with outpatient pulmonary follow-up and will need repeat CT 
chest and PFTs prior to his next pulmonary visit. 
.
#hypertension, benign: Previously, patient reported that he had been on 
lisinopril but this was discontinued due to cough. He was then 
placed on HCTZ by his PCP. HCTZ was held due to hyponatremia 
during this admission. He was instead started on losartan for BP 
control. It was started at 25mg and increased to 50mg daily. 
.
#Hyponatremia: patient with sodium of 126 on admission. patient appeared 
volume depleted on admission with urine electrolytes that were 
consistent with volume depletion. His hydrochlorothiazide was 
discontinued given his hyponatremia. He was given IV fluids 
without improvement in sodium levels. He was then placed on 
fluid restriction with slow improvement in sodium levels to 132 
by time of discharge. He should have his electrolytes rechecked 
at his next PCP .
.
#Bacteremia: patient reported history of a "bacterial infection" 
treated at in late . patient gave consent to 
obtain his medical records from OSH. Per records, his blood 
culture at that time grew fusobacter thought to be from 
appendix, treated with antibiotics. Incidental finding on cxr 
during that hospitalization showed a pulmonary nodule which was 
followed up by a CT chest in and showing left 
lower lobe consolidation and multiple nodules. He was thereafter 
lost to follow-up. Blood cultures during this hospital stay did 
not show any growth, though fungal/AFB blood cultures were still 
pending by time of discharge. 

#GERD: patient reported symptoms of heartburn during hospital stay. 
He was started on omeprazole 20mg daily. 

 
Medications on Admission:
HCTZ 25mg daily 
Lisinopril 20mg daily (discontinued and switched to HCTZ due to 
cough)

Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you in the hospital. You were 
admitted for shortness of breath. A CT scan of your chest 
showed wide spread pneumonia and emphysema. You were treated 
with antibiotics and nebulizers. You were seen by our pulmonary 
consultants who attempted to perform a bronchoscopy. This 
procedure had to be prematurely aborted because your oxygen 
levels became low and you were admitted to the intensive care 
unit for one night for closer monitoring. With antibiotics and 
nebulizers, your oxygen levels improved and your fevers 
resolved. You will need close follow-up with your PCP and with 
your new pulmonary doctor. You will see the pulmonologist 
within of discharge. Please talk to your PCP about 
scheduling repeat CAT scan of your chest before this 
appointment. 

Your PCP should also recheck your electrolytes because your 
sodium level was low during this hospital admission and because 
you were started on a new medication for your blood pressure. 

Your liver enzymes were also elevated during this admission. 
Several blood tests were sent but we did not have the final 
results before you were discharged. Your PCP should follow up 
the final results and recheck your liver function tests when you 
are seen.

The following changes were made to your medications:
1) START losartan 50mg once a day for your blood pressure
2) START omeprazole 20mg once a day for heartburn
3) START albuterol inhaler 2puffs every as needed for 
shortness of breath
4) STOP hydrochlorothiazide

donor renal transplant c/b delayed graft function from 
ATN admitted with hypotension and left leg edema. He had 
received 40 minutes of HD at on . 

Neuro: The patient remained alert and oriented during hospital 
stay. He did not require pain medication.

CV: The patient is anti-coagulated for mechanic mitral and 
aortic valves (goal INR 2.5 - 3.5) and was admitted with 
subtherapeutic INR. He was placed on a heparin drip during 
hospitalization and given daily coumadin while INR increased. 

Cardiology was consulted for increased mitral valve gradient 
seen on transthoracic echo on . Cardiology did not think the 
gradient needed any further acute inpatient intervention, given 
that the patient shows no signs of heart failture. He will 
follow up as outpatient on . BPs on discharge are in 140s 
systolic. 

Resp: On , the patient complains of shortness of breath that improved 
with duonebs & flutisone (in hospital substitute for 
mometasone.) The patient was never hypoxic and ambulatory sats 
were 96%. A CXR showed moderate pulmonary edema. The patient 
was given 40-60 mg of PO lasix on . He is no longer 
short of breath and O2 sats are excellent on room air. The 
patient is being discharged on 20 of PO Lasix daily and will be 
evaluated by renal and cardiology as outpatient.

GI/FEN: A RUQ ultrasound was done on to assess for occult 
ascites or liver disease and none was found. Ultrasound only 
showed fatty liver and gallbladder wall thickening with no 
ascites. He received 1.5 L of D5W on for a sodium of 149 
and was placed on a low potassium diet for high K. On , his 
K = 6.1 so he received kayexalate -> 5.6 -> 2nd dose of 30g of 
kayaxelate -> 5.1. Potassium on discharge is 5.1. Patient should 
continue on low K diet.

GU: The patient was admitted with hemodialysis schedule 
but did not require any futher HD during this hospital stay. 
His delayed graft function essentially resolved and he no longer 
required dialysis. His creatinine improved from 4.3 to 2.3. His 
urine output increased to 1500-1800 cc/day. Sevelamer and 
nephro caps were discontinued. The patient was given 40-60 mg 
of PO lasix on and is being discharged on 20 of PO 
Lasix daily 

ID: After increased mitral valve gradient was discovered, the 
patient had blood cultures drawn that were negative.

Heme: Because of significant left leg swelling, he had an 
bilateral lower extremity ultrasounds on that showed no DVT. 
 Heparin drip was initiated on in addition to daily coumadin 
for prosthetic valves. Venogram on showed venous outflow 
obstruction in left thigh which was angioplastied. Left leg 
swelling decreased significantly over time and patient now has 
palpaple thrill over HD graft in left thigh. 

On discharge, he is tolerating a regular diet, voiding and 
ambulating without difficulty. He has renal, transplant, and 
cardiology follow-up. INR should be checked every other day. 
Continue coumadin (INR goal 2.5 - 3.5) for mechanical valves. 
Labs every and (including tacrolimus level) with 
results faxed to .

Cardiac Perfusion LABTEST Study: 1. Normal cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular 
enlargement. 
2. Normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
 y/o male well known to transplant service who was admitted 
with pain over the transplant kidney three weeks post transplant 
kidney biopsy.

Ultrasound showed a large subcapsular hematoma. He underwent 
evacuation of the hematoma on . Surgeon was Dr. 
 who noted a large amount of relatively fresh appearing 
hematoma upon opening the renal capsule. Also of note when the 
capsule was opened there was no evidence of high pressure within
the kidney itself. Renal parenchyma was boggy, but pink and did 
exhibit bleeding. Approximately 100 cc of clot was removed. He 
was transferred to the ICU for further management. Heparin drip 
was started following surgery. INR was subtherapeutic on 
admission at 1.9

Baseline creatinine of 2.0 was increased to 2.9 that further 
increased to 3.4. He also had hyperkalemia with K of 5.8 which 
was initially treated with insulin, dextrose and calcium 
gluconate. A temprorary dialysis line with VIP port was placed 
in anticipation of need for dialysis as well as very poor access 
history. 

Potassium was 7.2 post op, and he underwent a single 
hemodialysis session. Urine output on admisison was less than 
400 cc. Urine output improved after hematoma evacuation(~1000 - 
1500cc/day). Creatinine decreased from a peak of 4.3 post 
operatively to 2.5 by POD 12.

On POD 2, he was having complaint of chest pain. Cardiology was 
called and troponins were cycled and negative. A cardiac echo 
was done showing mildly dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle 
with moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation and at least 
moderate pulmonary hypertension. Well seated AVR and MVR with 
elevated
gradient across the mitral valve. Mildly dilated thoracic aorta.

He then underwent a cardiac perfusion study which showed normal 
cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular enlargement and 
normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

He was stable and was able to transfer to the regular transplant 
floor. Heparin drip and warfarin were resumed on POD 2. He 
required 7 days on the heparin drip before the INR was at a 
therapeutic level. 

On , he went to the OR for a PORT placement in a small 
vessel noted on chest CT. Surgeon was Dr. . 
Interventional radiology was contacted and after premed with 
steroid and benadryl prep (3 doses of 50mg of prednisone)he 
underwent removal of the OR placed port and revision of the left 
chest Port-A-Cath, with 28 cm length of tubing terminating 
in the right atrium. Also noted was distally occluded or tightly 
stenosed Left internal jugular vein at junction with 
rachiocephalic vein. Small contrast injection beyond this 
demonstrated entral patency of the brachiocephalic vein into the 
superior vena cava and right atrium. At end of case he 
developed hives and shortness of breath and required emergent 
treatment for anaphylaxis. He was transferred to the ICU for 
management and did well eventually transferring back to the 
med-surg unit. 

Facial swelling and generalized edema was treated with IV doses 
of Lasix then he was transitioned back to torsemide with 
improved edema. He did complain of shortness of breath/cough and 
was evaluated by a pulmonary consult. Repeat CXR was concerning 
for worsened loculated right pleural effusion. TTE revealed 
65-70%EF with moderate TR and severe systolic pulmonary 
hypertension. Pulmonary Consult recommendations were to continue 
diuresis as volume overload may have exacerbated severity of 
pulmonary hypertension. Inhalers were continued. He was also 
given 1 unit of PRBC for hct of 23 and epogen was increased to 
3000units 3x per week in attempt to improve anemia. No 
intervention was planned to intervene on the loculated effusion 
given that he remained afebrile and wbc was wnl.

Overal edema decreased with weight dropping to 74kg by on 
torsemide 40mg daily. Baseline weight pre-hospitalization was 
74kg. 

Overall, he was feeling well and ready for discharge back to 
 . RLQ incision staples were removed. Incision was inctact

bleeding, dizziness or weakness, decreased urine output or dark, 
cloudy urine, swelling of abdomen or ankles, weight gain or loss 
of more than 3 pounds in a day, monitor for signs of elevated 
INR to include nosebleed, rectal bleeding, dark tarry stools or 
easy bruising or any other concerning symptoms.

 as arranged by the transplant clinic, with results to

heparin gtt due to his mechanical valves. Post biopsy his Cr
remained stable at 2.2 for several days until he was discharged
on . He states that his pain over the allograft began
suddenly today while watching the TV. He denies any trauma to 
the
area. He thinks that he may have had some decreased urine output
over the last several days but this has not been recorded. He 
was
given insulin/dextrose/lasix in the ED for his hyperkalemia with

hematemesis, bloating, cramping, melena, BRBPR, dysphagia, chest
pain, shortness of breath, cough, edema, urinary frequency,
urgency

ESRD, hypertension, dCHF, multiple line infections, restless leg
syndrome, asthma, VRE, Mitral valve endocarditis with
aortic valve annular abscess, MRSA, edema, anemia, afib, chronic
peridontitis, GERD, vitamin D deficiency, osteoporosis,
pericardial effusion w/ tamponade

abscess) ( )

-transjugular liver biopsy

mother with hypertension

palpation.

Labs on Admission: 
WBC-8.2 RBC-3.40* Hgb-9.1* Hct-31.6* MCV-93 MCH-26.7* MCHC-28.7*

 HBsAg-NEGATIVE HBsAb-NEGATIVE HBcAb-NEGATIVE
 tacroFK-10.9
.
Cardiac Perfusion LABTEST Study: 1. Normal cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular 
enlargement. 
2. Normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
 y/o male well known to transplant service who was admitted 
with pain over the transplant kidney three weeks post transplant 
kidney biopsy.

Ultrasound showed a large subcapsular hematoma. He underwent 
evacuation of the hematoma on . Surgeon was Dr. 
 who noted a large amount of relatively fresh appearing 
hematoma upon opening the renal capsule. Also of note when the 
capsule was opened there was no evidence of high pressure within
the kidney itself. Renal parenchyma was boggy, but pink and did 
exhibit bleeding. Approximately 100 cc of clot was removed. He 
was transferred to the ICU for further management. Heparin drip 
was started following surgery. INR was subtherapeutic on 
admission at 1.9

Baseline creatinine of 2.0 was increased to 2.9 that further 
increased to 3.4. He also had hyperkalemia with K of 5.8 which 
was initially treated with insulin, dextrose and calcium 
gluconate. A temprorary dialysis line with VIP port was placed 
in anticipation of need for dialysis as well as very poor access 
history. 

Potassium was 7.2 post op, and he underwent a single 
hemodialysis session. Urine output on admisison was less than 
400 cc. Urine output improved after hematoma evacuation(~1000 - 
1500cc/day). Creatinine decreased from a peak of 4.3 post 
operatively to 2.5 by POD 12.

On POD 2, he was having complaint of chest pain. Cardiology was 
called and troponins were cycled and negative. A cardiac echo 
was done showing mildly dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle 
with moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation and at least 
moderate pulmonary hypertension. Well seated AVR and MVR with 
elevated
gradient across the mitral valve. Mildly dilated thoracic aorta.

He then underwent a cardiac perfusion study which showed normal 
cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular enlargement and 
normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

He was stable and was able to transfer to the regular transplant 
floor. Heparin drip and warfarin were resumed on POD 2. He 
required 7 days on the heparin drip before the INR was at a 
therapeutic level. 

On , he went to the OR for a PORT placement in a small 
vessel noted on chest CT. Surgeon was Dr. . 
Interventional radiology was contacted and after premed with 
steroid and benadryl prep (3 doses of 50mg of prednisone)he 
underwent removal of the OR placed port and revision of the left 
chest Port-A-Cath, with 28 cm length of tubing terminating 
in the right atrium. Also noted was distally occluded or tightly 
stenosed Left internal jugular vein at junction with 
rachiocephalic vein. Small contrast injection beyond this 
demonstrated entral patency of the brachiocephalic vein into the 
superior vena cava and right atrium. At end of case he 
developed hives and shortness of breath and required emergent 
treatment for anaphylaxis. He was transferred to the ICU for 
management and did well eventually transferring back to the 
med-surg unit. 

Facial swelling and generalized edema was treated with IV doses 
of Lasix then he was transitioned back to torsemide with 
improved edema. He did complain of shortness of breath/cough and 
was evaluated by a pulmonary consult. Repeat CXR was concerning 
for worsened loculated right pleural effusion. TTE revealed 
65-70%EF with moderate TR and severe systolic pulmonary 
hypertension. Pulmonary Consult recommendations were to continue 
diuresis as volume overload may have exacerbated severity of 
pulmonary hypertension. Inhalers were continued. He was also 
given 1 unit of PRBC for hct of 23 and epogen was increased to 
3000units 3x per week in attempt to improve anemia. No 
intervention was planned to intervene on the loculated effusion 
given that he remained afebrile and wbc was wnl.

Overal edema decreased with weight dropping to 74kg by on 
torsemide 40mg daily. Baseline weight pre-hospitalization was 
74kg. 

Overall, he was feeling well and ready for discharge back to 
 . RLQ incision staples were removed. Incision was inctact

Brief Hospital Course:
 y/o male well known to transplant service who was admitted 
with pain over the transplant kidney three weeks post transplant 
kidney biopsy.

Ultrasound showed a large subcapsular hematoma. He underwent 
evacuation of the hematoma on . Surgeon was Dr. 
 who noted a large amount of relatively fresh appearing 
hematoma upon opening the renal capsule. Also of note when the 
capsule was opened there was no evidence of high pressure within
the kidney itself. Renal parenchyma was boggy, but pink and did 
exhibit bleeding. Approximately 100 cc of clot was removed. He 
was transferred to the ICU for further management. Heparin drip 
was started following surgery. INR was subtherapeutic on 
admission at 1.9

Baseline creatinine of 2.0 was increased to 2.9 that further 
increased to 3.4. He also had hyperkalemia with K of 5.8 which 
was initially treated with insulin, dextrose and calcium 
gluconate. A temprorary dialysis line with VIP port was placed 
in anticipation of need for dialysis as well as very poor access 
history. 

Potassium was 7.2 post op, and he underwent a single 
hemodialysis session. Urine output on admisison was less than 
400 cc. Urine output improved after hematoma evacuation(~1000 - 
1500cc/day). Creatinine decreased from a peak of 4.3 post 
operatively to 2.5 by POD 12.

On POD 2, he was having complaint of chest pain. Cardiology was 
called and troponins were cycled and negative. A cardiac echo 
was done showing mildly dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle 
with moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation and at least 
moderate pulmonary hypertension. Well seated AVR and MVR with 
elevated
gradient across the mitral valve. Mildly dilated thoracic aorta.

He then underwent a cardiac perfusion study which showed normal 
cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular enlargement and 
normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

He was stable and was able to transfer to the regular transplant 
floor. Heparin drip and warfarin were resumed on POD 2. He 
required 7 days on the heparin drip before the INR was at a 
therapeutic level. 

On , he went to the OR for a PORT placement in a small 
vessel noted on chest CT. Surgeon was Dr. . 
Interventional radiology was contacted and after premed with 
steroid and benadryl prep (3 doses of 50mg of prednisone)he 
underwent removal of the OR placed port and revision of the left 
chest Port-A-Cath, with 28 cm length of tubing terminating 
in the right atrium. Also noted was distally occluded or tightly 
stenosed Left internal jugular vein at junction with 
rachiocephalic vein. Small contrast injection beyond this 
demonstrated entral patency of the brachiocephalic vein into the 
superior vena cava and right atrium. At end of case he 
developed hives and shortness of breath and required emergent 
treatment for anaphylaxis. He was transferred to the ICU for 
management and did well eventually transferring back to the 
med-surg unit. 

Facial swelling and generalized edema was treated with IV doses 
of Lasix then he was transitioned back to torsemide with 
improved edema. He did complain of shortness of breath/cough and 
was evaluated by a pulmonary consult. Repeat CXR was concerning 
for worsened loculated right pleural effusion. TTE revealed 
65-70%EF with moderate TR and severe systolic pulmonary 
hypertension. Pulmonary Consult recommendations were to continue 
diuresis as volume overload may have exacerbated severity of 
pulmonary hypertension. Inhalers were continued. He was also 
given 1 unit of PRBC for hct of 23 and epogen was increased to 
3000units 3x per week in attempt to improve anemia. No 
intervention was planned to intervene on the loculated effusion 
given that he remained afebrile and wbc was wnl.

Overal edema decreased with weight dropping to 74kg by on 
torsemide 40mg daily. Baseline weight pre-hospitalization was 
74kg. 

Overall, he was feeling well and ready for discharge back to 
 . RLQ incision staples were removed. Incision was inctact

increased to 3.4. He also had hyperkalemia with K of 5.8 which 
was initially treated with insulin, dextrose and calcium 
gluconate. A temprorary dialysis line with VIP port was placed 
in anticipation of need for dialysis as well as very poor access 
history.

hemodialysis session. Urine output on admisison was less than 
400 cc. Urine output improved after hematoma evacuation(~1000 - 
1500cc/day). Creatinine decreased from a peak of 4.3 post 
operatively to 2.5 by POD 12.

On POD 2, he was having complaint of chest pain. Cardiology was 
called and troponins were cycled and negative. A cardiac echo 
was done showing mildly dilated and hypokinetic right ventricle 
with moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation and at least 
moderate pulmonary hypertension. Well seated AVR and MVR with 
elevated
gradient across the mitral valve. Mildly dilated thoracic aorta.

He then underwent a cardiac perfusion study which showed normal 
cardiac perfusion with moderate left ventricular enlargement and 
normal motion with an ejection fraction of 54%. 

He was stable and was able to transfer to the regular transplant 
floor. Heparin drip and warfarin were resumed on POD 2. He 
required 7 days on the heparin drip before the INR was at a 
therapeutic level. 

On , he went to the OR for a PORT placement in a small 
vessel noted on chest CT. Surgeon was Dr. . 
Interventional radiology was contacted and after premed with 
steroid and benadryl prep (3 doses of 50mg of prednisone)he 
underwent removal of the OR placed port and revision of the left 
chest Port-A-Cath, with 28 cm length of tubing terminating 
in the right atrium. Also noted was distally occluded or tightly 
stenosed Left internal jugular vein at junction with 
rachiocephalic vein. Small contrast injection beyond this 
demonstrated entral patency of the brachiocephalic vein into the 
superior vena cava and right atrium. At end of case he 
developed hives and shortness of breath and required emergent 
treatment for anaphylaxis. He was transferred to the ICU for 
management and did well eventually transferring back to the 
med-surg unit. 

Facial swelling and generalized edema was treated with IV doses 
of Lasix then he was transitioned back to torsemide with 
improved edema. He did complain of shortness of breath/cough and 
was evaluated by a pulmonary consult. Repeat CXR was concerning 
for worsened loculated right pleural effusion. TTE revealed 
65-70%EF with moderate TR and severe systolic pulmonary 
hypertension. Pulmonary Consult recommendations were to continue 
diuresis as volume overload may have exacerbated severity of 
pulmonary hypertension. Inhalers were continued. He was also 
given 1 unit of PRBC for hct of 23 and epogen was increased to 
3000units 3x per week in attempt to improve anemia. No 
intervention was planned to intervene on the loculated effusion 
given that he remained afebrile and wbc was wnl.

Overal edema decreased with weight dropping to 74kg by on 
torsemide 40mg daily. Baseline weight pre-hospitalization was 
74kg. 

Overall, he was feeling well and ready for discharge back to 
 . RLQ incision staples were removed. Incision was inctact

Discharge Instructions:
Please call the transplant clinic at for fever > 
101, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, inability 
to tolerate food, fluids or medications, increased abdominal 
pain, pain at the new port site, incisional redness, drainage or 
bleeding, dizziness or weakness, decreased urine output or dark, 
cloudy urine, swelling of abdomen or ankles, weight gain or loss 
of more than 3 pounds in a day, monitor for signs of elevated 
INR to include nosebleed, rectal bleeding, dark tarry stools or 
easy bruising or any other concerning symptoms.

 as arranged by the transplant clinic, with results to 
the transplant clinic (Fax . CBC, Chem 10,

implemented in conjunction with the transplant clinic.

Follow your medication card, keep it updated with any dosage 
changes, and always bring your card with you to any clinic or 
hospital visits.

You may shower.Drink enough fluids to keep your urine light in 
color. 

Have your blood sugars and blood pressure checked. 
Report consistently elevated values to the transplant clinic

Check your weight daily. If weight decreases 3 pounds in a day, 
hold the torsemide

Do not increase, decrease, stop or start medications without 
consultation with the transplant clinic at . There 
are significant drug interactions with anti-rejection 
medications which must be considered in medication management 
following transplant

Consult transplant binder, and there is always someone on call 
at the transplant clinic with any questions that may arise

Kidney transplant subcapsular hematoma
Left IJ vein stenosis
Need for long term IV access, portacath placed
Loculated R pleural effusion
Pulmonary hypertension

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Please call the transplant clinic at for fever > 
101, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, inability 
to tolerate food, fluids or medications, increased abdominal 
pain, pain at the new port site, incisional redness, drainage or 
bleeding, dizziness or weakness, decreased urine output or dark, 
cloudy urine, swelling of abdomen or ankles, weight gain or loss 
of more than 3 pounds in a day, monitor for signs of elevated 
INR to include nosebleed, rectal bleeding, dark tarry stools or 
easy bruising or any other concerning symptoms.

You will have labwork drawn twice weekly every and 
 as arranged by the transplant clinic, with results to 
the transplant clinic (Fax . CBC, Chem 10, 
AST, T Bili, Trough Tacro level, urinalysis. 

Transition to Belatacept is under discussion and will be 
implemented in conjunction with the transplant clinic.

Follow your medication card, keep it updated with any dosage 
changes, and always bring your card with you to any clinic or 
hospital visits.

You may shower.Drink enough fluids to keep your urine light in 
color. 

Have your blood sugars and blood pressure checked. 
Report consistently elevated values to the transplant clinic

Check your weight daily. If weight decreases 3 pounds in a day, 
hold the torsemide

Do not increase, decrease, stop or start medications without 
consultation with the transplant clinic at . There 
are significant drug interactions with anti-rejection 
medications which must be considered in medication management 
following transplant

Consult transplant binder, and there is always someone on call 
at the transplant clinic with any questions that may arise

Brief Hospital Course:
SUMMARY:
====================
Ms. is a year old woman with a history of mild 
intermittent asthma, mood disorder, and inhalation burn injury 
from a house fire (1 mo ago) who underwent prolonged intubation 
at c/b subglottic tracheal stenosis, status post dilation with 
 IP on , with recurrence of stridor and now status post repeat 
tracheal dilation
 , initially the TSICU for airway monitoring and then 
transferred to medicine for further management. She remained 
hemodynamically stable on room air with SpO2 94-96% while 
walking and at rest. She has oxygen and nursing services at home 
and was discharged home with instructions to follow-up 
outpatient with thoracic surgery.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
====================
[ ] Thoracic surgery working on scheduling her an outpatient 
appointment for pre-op eval/consent. Patient should call 
 to schedule an appointment if she does not hear 
from them business days.
[ ] and ANCA pending at time of discharge to rule out 
autoimmune disease as component of tracheal stenosis.

ACUTE/ACTIVE ISSUES:
====================
#Stridor
#Tracheal stenosis
#status post rigid bronch, electrocautery and balloon dilation 
She first underwent dilation at . CT at 
from showed tracheal inflammation and narrowing. She 
underwent rigid bronch/electrocautery/balloon dilation with IP 
on without complication and with improvement her stridor 
and she received a short course of steroids and unasyn. She 
remained stable on , which is her home O2 requirement, and 
was discharged home on a regular diet.

#Mild intermittent asthma
She was continued on her home albuterol nebs Q6hours as needed and 
home budenoside 0.5mg IH twice a day without incident.

CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES:
======================
#Glaucoma
Her home lumigan drops were non-formulary and she was 
transitioned to timolol 1 drop each eye twice a day and then 
transitioned back to her home lumigan drops at discharge.D

#Unspecified mood d/o
#Anxiety
She was continued on her home carbamazepine 400mg twice a day, doxepin 
150mg qHS, perphenazine 24mg qHS, Seroquel XR 600mg qHS, 
hydroxyzine 50mg q6 as needed, clonidine 0.1mg qHS as needed for anxiety, 
and ramelteon 5mg qHS as needed and her home diazepam 10mg three times a day was 
held without incident.

#hypertension
Her blood pressure remained under good control (<150) with her 
home amlodipine 10mg daily. Her home lisinopril 20 daily was 
held given airway concerns but she remained stable after 
tracheal dilation and she was re-started on lisinopril at 
discharge.

#CAD
She was continued on her home ASA 81 daily

#GERD
She was continued on her home ranitidine 150mg twice a day

#Chronic back pain
She was continued on her home gabapentin 300mg three times a day

ICU COURSE :
======================
 PMHx inhalation burn injuries and intubation c/p tracheal 
stenosis status post tracheal dilation on p/w , CT 
demonstrating tracheal narrowing, admitted to ICU for 
respiratory watch. IP was consulted on presentation, and per 
there recommendations she was started on hydrocortisone 40 mg q8 
hr and unasyn on for edema seen on CT scan from . 
 . She remained on L NC throughout her 
hospitalization. Patient initially NPO. She underwent a 
bronchoscopy and tracheal balloon dilation by IP on , and 
she remained the ICU for >24 hours post procedure on 
respiratory watch. She remained stable and she was deemed stable 
for the floor. She was restarted on clears to regular diet, 
which was well tolerated. Hydrocortisone and unasyn was 
discontinued on per IP. 
Her home meds were restarted and continued throughout her 
hospitalization
She was transferred to the medical floor on for additional 
management

Brief Hospital Course:
SUMMARY:
====================
Ms. is a year old woman with a history of mild 
intermittent asthma, mood disorder, and inhalation burn injury 
from a house fire (1 mo ago) who underwent prolonged intubation 
at c/b subglottic tracheal stenosis, status post dilation with 
 IP on , with recurrence of stridor and now status post repeat 
tracheal dilation
 , initially the TSICU for airway monitoring and then 
transferred to medicine for further management. She remained 
hemodynamically stable on room air with SpO2 94-96% while 
walking and at rest. She has oxygen and nursing services at home 
and was discharged home with instructions to follow-up 
outpatient with thoracic surgery.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
====================
[ ] Thoracic surgery working on scheduling her an outpatient 
appointment for pre-op eval/consent. Patient should call 
 to schedule an appointment if she does not hear 
from them business days.
[ ] and ANCA pending at time of discharge to rule out 
autoimmune disease as component of tracheal stenosis.

ACUTE/ACTIVE ISSUES:
====================
#Stridor
#Tracheal stenosis
#status post rigid bronch, electrocautery and balloon dilation 
She first underwent dilation at . CT at 
from showed tracheal inflammation and narrowing. She 
underwent rigid bronch/electrocautery/balloon dilation with IP 
on without complication and with improvement her stridor 
and she received a short course of steroids and unasyn. She 
remained stable on , which is her home O2 requirement, and 
was discharged home on a regular diet.

#Mild intermittent asthma
She was continued on her home albuterol nebs Q6hours as needed and 
home budenoside 0.5mg IH twice a day without incident.

CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES:
======================
#Glaucoma
Her home lumigan drops were non-formulary and she was 
transitioned to timolol 1 drop each eye twice a day and then 
transitioned back to her home lumigan drops at discharge.D

#Unspecified mood d/o
#Anxiety
She was continued on her home carbamazepine 400mg twice a day, doxepin 
150mg qHS, perphenazine 24mg qHS, Seroquel XR 600mg qHS, 
hydroxyzine 50mg q6 as needed, clonidine 0.1mg qHS as needed for anxiety, 
and ramelteon 5mg qHS as needed and her home diazepam 10mg three times a day was 
held without incident.

#hypertension
Her blood pressure remained under good control (<150) with her 
home amlodipine 10mg daily. Her home lisinopril 20 daily was 
held given airway concerns but she remained stable after 
tracheal dilation and she was re-started on lisinopril at 
discharge.

#CAD
She was continued on her home ASA 81 daily

#GERD
She was continued on her home ranitidine 150mg twice a day

#Chronic back pain
She was continued on her home gabapentin 300mg three times a day

 PMHx inhalation burn injuries and intubation c/p tracheal

hospitalization. Patient initially NPO. She underwent a

respiratory watch. She remained stable and she was deemed stable 
for the floor. She was restarted on clears to regular diet,

hospitalization
She was transferred to the medical floor on for additional 
management

 does not recall having any prodromal symptoms, and denies any

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient is an year old male with a history of CML, CAD 
status post five vessel CABG, complete heart block with PPM, and CKD 
Stage 4 who presents after an unwitnessed fall at home, spiking 
a fever to 102.3 in the ED and having CT chest findings 
concerning for LUL pneumonia. was treated with IV 
zosyn/vancomycin and defervesced, then transitioned to 
cefpodoxime/azithromycin to complete a full course.

ACUTE CARE
# Unwitnessed Fall: Based on his description of the event, his 
fall is likely either mechanical in the setting of ongoing 
confusion, or orthostatic related to his infection, but 
orthostatics were negative during his hospitalization. However, 
 does not fully remember the episode. Seizure seems unlikely 
given his lack of seizure history. Cardiogenic is possible, but 
 neg and pacer currently working with rates in the . No 
events on tele. evaluated the patient and recommended rehab.
 
# Confusion: His presentation with increased confusion is likely 
multifactorial delirium from fever, infection, fall with head 
strike, and it improved after antibiotics, IVF, defevervesced. 
However, his family was concerned that has been off his 
baseline for several weeks and were concerned that had a 
stroke. A family meeting in which discussion of the possibility 
of stroke despite negative CT head, but given his current 
condition and the inability to treat if embolic given his 
thrombocytopenia and risk of bleeding, further investigation 
would not help at this time, and the patient cannot get MRI with 
pacemaker in place, to which his family agreed. had no focal 
neurologic findings on exam. His TSH was also mildly elevated at 
6.9 w/ free T4 of 1; since hypothyroidism could contribute to 
his confusion, his levothyroxine dose was increased to 25mcg.

# Fever/Leukocytosis: His CT chest is concerning for a LUL 
pneumonia. So, pna is most likely cause of his symptoms. 
Initially treated for HAP with vanco/zosyn given recent hospital 
admission on for fever and diarrhea. denies having 
diarrhea at this time. No abd pain. Another possible cause of 
his fever and increase in WBC is his CML. was transitioned to 
azithromycin and cefpoxodime for outpt treatment.

# CKD Stage 4: Creatinine at 3.1 on discharge. Continue 
Calcitriol, Vitamin D, and Sodium bicarbonate. held Lasix 20mg 
PO qday at admission and this resulted in improved Cr. We 
recommend cont to hold it and re-starting it based on daily 
weights, if the patient gains 3LBS, please consider re-starting 
Lasix. Otherwise, did not have crackles on lung exam at time 
of d/c and edema was only trace. 

CHRONIC CARE
# CML: Mulitple regimens used in past. Recently switched from 
Dasatinib to Hydroxyurea last month, with dose reduction last 
week. Continued on Hydroxyurea 500 mg PO DAILY.

# Pulmonary Edema: His CXR and CT in the ED showed pulmonary 
edema, and has chronic edema. His last TTE on 
showed mildly decreased LVEF 40%, severe TR, and moderate MR. 
 maintained SpO2 in the high . Cont on Lasix at home dose 
of 20mg. 

# Hypothyroidism: Increase in TSH to 6.9 and free T4 at 1, so 
increased dose from 12.5-> 25mcg. Will need repeat levels in 
weeks

# CAD: No current chest pain or concerning findings on EKG, 
though evaluation limited by paced rhythm. Aspirin was held on 
 for low platelets. Trop at 0.07-0.08 which is his 
baseline given prior admission. Flat CK-MB, denies having any 
chest discomfort. ASA was held given thrombocytopenia, continue 
Simvastatin 20 mg PO daily, substituted Metoprolol 25 mg PO twice a day 
for home long acting.

TRANSITIONS OF CARE
# CODE: Discussion with family and attending, is DNR/DNI 
# EMERGENCY CONTACT: (daughter, Phone: 
 , Cell: 
# PENDING STUDIES: Blood culture x2
# ISSUES TO ADDRESS AT FOLLOW UP
- resolution of pneumonia
- completion of abx
- recheck TFTs in a weeks.
- address resuming lasix (held at time of d/c given incr in Cr, 
no crackles on lung exam and only mild edema)

Vitamin B12

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient is an year old male with a history of CML, CAD 
status post five vessel CABG, complete heart block with PPM, and CKD 
Stage 4 who presents after an unwitnessed fall at home, spiking 
a fever to 102.3 in the ED and having CT chest findings 
concerning for LUL pneumonia. was treated with IV 
zosyn/vancomycin and defervesced, then transitioned to 
cefpodoxime/azithromycin to complete a full course.

ACUTE CARE
# Unwitnessed Fall: Based on his description of the event, his 
fall is likely either mechanical in the setting of ongoing 
confusion, or orthostatic related to his infection, but 
orthostatics were negative during his hospitalization. However, 
 does not fully remember the episode. Seizure seems unlikely 
given his lack of seizure history. Cardiogenic is possible, but 
 neg and pacer currently working with rates in the . No 
events on tele. evaluated the patient and recommended rehab.
 
# Confusion: His presentation with increased confusion is likely 
multifactorial delirium from fever, infection, fall with head 
strike, and it improved after antibiotics, IVF, defevervesced. 
However, his family was concerned that has been off his 
baseline for several weeks and were concerned that had a 
stroke. A family meeting in which discussion of the possibility 
of stroke despite negative CT head, but given his current 
condition and the inability to treat if embolic given his 
thrombocytopenia and risk of bleeding, further investigation 
would not help at this time, and the patient cannot get MRI with 
pacemaker in place, to which his family agreed. had no focal 
neurologic findings on exam. His TSH was also mildly elevated at 
6.9 w/ free T4 of 1; since hypothyroidism could contribute to 
his confusion, his levothyroxine dose was increased to 25mcg.

# Fever/Leukocytosis: His CT chest is concerning for a LUL 
pneumonia. So, pna is most likely cause of his symptoms. 
Initially treated for HAP with vanco/zosyn given recent hospital 
admission on for fever and diarrhea. denies having 
diarrhea at this time. No abd pain. Another possible cause of 
his fever and increase in WBC is his CML. was transitioned to 
azithromycin and cefpoxodime for outpt treatment.

# CKD Stage 4: Creatinine at 3.1 on discharge. Continue 
Calcitriol, Vitamin D, and Sodium bicarbonate. held Lasix 20mg 
PO qday at admission and this resulted in improved Cr. We 
recommend cont to hold it and re-starting it based on daily 
weights, if the patient gains 3LBS, please consider re-starting 
Lasix. Otherwise, did not have crackles on lung exam at time 
of d/c and edema was only trace. 

CHRONIC CARE
# CML: Mulitple regimens used in past. Recently switched from 
Dasatinib to Hydroxyurea last month, with dose reduction last 
week. Continued on Hydroxyurea 500 mg PO DAILY.

# Pulmonary Edema: His CXR and CT in the ED showed pulmonary 
edema, and has chronic edema. His last TTE on 
showed mildly decreased LVEF 40%, severe TR, and moderate MR. 
 maintained SpO2 in the high . Cont on Lasix at home dose 
of 20mg. 

# Hypothyroidism: Increase in TSH to 6.9 and free T4 at 1, so 
increased dose from 12.5-> 25mcg. Will need repeat levels in 
weeks

# CAD: No current chest pain or concerning findings on EKG, 
though evaluation limited by paced rhythm. Aspirin was held on 
 for low platelets. Trop at 0.07-0.08 which is his 
baseline given prior admission. Flat CK-MB, denies having any 
chest discomfort. ASA was held given thrombocytopenia, continue 
Simvastatin 20 mg PO daily, substituted Metoprolol 25 mg PO twice a day 
for home long acting.

TRANSITIONS OF CARE

- resolution of pneumonia
- completion of abx

- address resuming lasix (held at time of d/c given incr in Cr, 
no crackles on lung exam and only mild edema)

 morning, she went to urgent care. She again had an

Brief Hospital Course:
 with asthma, emphysema (FEV1:FVC 81% predicted and 
nasal polyps presents to the emergency room with dyspnea after 
referral from urgent care.

#ASTHMA/COPD EXACERBATION: Patient reports non-productive cough 
and progressive dyspnea/chest tightness x2 days prior to 
admission. She was unable to get relieve from her as needed albuterol. 
She states that she is compliant with her home medications which 
include albuterol, budesonide, fluticasone-salmeterol, 
tiotropium and montelukast. Patient notes starting a new job at 
 2 months ago but otherwise notes no new exposures, 
however does endorse seasonal allergies and exposure to heavy 
dust loads at work. Of note she is currently being treated for 
chronic sinus infections which may be a contributing factor to 
her exacerbation.
Received albuterol and ipratropium in ED in addition to 
prednisone 60mg. Improved symptoms upon arrival to the floor and 
breathing comfortably while satting well on room air without 
wheezing on exam. On the floor patient was treated with 
albuterol, duonebs, advair and fluticasone in addition to 
prednisone 60mg QG (D1 . Given the patient's history of 
nasal polyps, allergy to aspirin and asthma she fits 
Triade for aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease and should 
avoid NSAIDs and ASA in the future. In addition, given her 
elevated eosinophilia in addition to severe asthma on optimized 
outpatient therapy, patient should have allergy referral for 
consideration of omalizumab therapy. Patient was discharged on 
prednisone 60mg QD with plans to taper after evaluation of 
symptoms at follow up appointments. In addition patient's Advair 
dose was increased. Patient continued other asthma/COPD 
medications.

 Triade: Patient is followed in clinic for chronic 
sinus infection. In addition, patient has nasal polyps and 
aspirin allergy. This in addition to her severe asthma is 
consistent with triade or Aspirin-exacerbated 
respiratory disease. Currently being treated with Augmentin x3 
weeks by outpatient ENT. Patient notified to avoid taking NSAIDs 
in addition to aspirin. NSAIDs added as allergy in OMR.

#Concern for ACS: ED initially concerned for ACS, however story 
seems more consistent with asthma attack as patient endorses 
chest tightness that occurred with onset of dyspnea that also 
improved with improvement of dyspnea. Troponin negative x 2 and 
EKG with stable ST changes from years prior. 
 
# Emphysema 
- Resume home inhalers once symptoms are stably improved 

# Osteoporosis 
- Continue alendronate 

# Homelessness 
- SW consult 

# Medication Reconciliation 
- Continue gabapentin 
 
TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
[]assess symptoms and continue prednisone taper
[]ensure ENT, pulm and allergy follow up
[]consider Omalizumab therapy if indicated
[]Patient's son was concerned about anxiety contributing to his 
mother's symptoms and would like further discussion about 
starting anti-anxiety therapy

 morning, she went to urgent care. She again had an

with asthma, emphysema (FEV1:FVC 81% predicted and 
nasal polyps presents to the emergency room with dyspnea after 
referral from urgent care.

#ASTHMA/COPD EXACERBATION: Patient reports non-productive cough 
and progressive dyspnea/chest tightness x2 days prior to 
admission. She was unable to get relieve from her as needed albuterol. 
She states that she is compliant with her home medications which 
include albuterol, budesonide, fluticasone-salmeterol, 
tiotropium and montelukast. Patient notes starting a new job at 
 2 months ago but otherwise notes no new exposures, 
however does endorse seasonal allergies and exposure to heavy 
dust loads at work. Of note she is currently being treated for 
chronic sinus infections which may be a contributing factor to 
her exacerbation.
Received albuterol and ipratropium in ED in addition to 
prednisone 60mg. Improved symptoms upon arrival to the floor and 
breathing comfortably while satting well on room air without 
wheezing on exam. On the floor patient was treated with 
albuterol, duonebs, advair and fluticasone in addition to 
prednisone 60mg QG (D1 . Given the patient's history of 
nasal polyps, allergy to aspirin and asthma she fits 
Triade for aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease and should 
avoid NSAIDs and ASA in the future. In addition, given her 
elevated eosinophilia in addition to severe asthma on optimized 
outpatient therapy, patient should have allergy referral for 
consideration of omalizumab therapy. Patient was discharged on 
prednisone 60mg QD with plans to taper after evaluation of 
symptoms at follow up appointments. In addition patient's Advair 
dose was increased. Patient continued other asthma/COPD 
medications.

 Triade: Patient is followed in clinic for chronic 
sinus infection. In addition, patient has nasal polyps and 
aspirin allergy. This in addition to her severe asthma is 
consistent with triade or Aspirin-exacerbated 
respiratory disease. Currently being treated with Augmentin x3 
weeks by outpatient ENT. Patient notified to avoid taking NSAIDs 
in addition to aspirin. NSAIDs added as allergy in OMR.

#Concern for ACS: ED initially concerned for ACS, however story 
seems more consistent with asthma attack as patient endorses 
chest tightness that occurred with onset of dyspnea that also 
improved with improvement of dyspnea. Troponin negative x 2 and 
EKG with stable ST changes from years prior. 
 
# Emphysema 
- Resume home inhalers once symptoms are stably improved 

# Osteoporosis 
- Continue alendronate 

# Homelessness 
- SW consult 

# Medication Reconciliation 
- Continue gabapentin

[]assess symptoms and continue prednisone taper
[]ensure ENT, pulm and allergy follow up
[]consider Omalizumab therapy if indicated
[]Patient's son was concerned about anxiety contributing to his 
mother's symptoms and would like further discussion about 
starting anti-anxiety therapy
