8.1* 2.1* 1. year-old W with a recent healthcare-associated pneumonia 
( ), presented to the ED with dyspnea and palpitations, 
was found to be in hypoxic respiratory failure and sepsis in 
setting of a new large R-sided pneumonia. She was intubated in 
the Emergency Room and transferred to the Medical Intensive Care 
Unit (MICU) where she spent a brief period of time on levophed 
to keep her blood pressures adequate. She was started on broad 
spectrum antibiotics, extubated without difficulty, and 
transferred to the Medicine floor for further management.
.
# Pneumonia: Possibly occurred secondary to aspiration, although 
unable to tell definitively per a Speech and Swallow evaluation. 
The patient received eight days of intravenous vancomycin, 
aztreonem, and ciprofloxacin to treat her large right-sided 
pneumonia. These antibiotics were chosen in light of her many 
antibiotic allergies. She also received schedueled nebulizer 
treatments, chest physical therapy, beside respiratory 
suctioning, and an acapella valve. She never had a leukocytosis 
or fever. Her blood and sputum cultures were all negative. 
Repeat chest imaging suggested an increasing component of fluid 
overload, for which she was treated with diuretics. Her oxygen 
requirement was slowly weaned from 2 liters via nasal canula to 
1 liter at the time of discharge. This should continue to be 
weaned, as tolerated, at rehab. She did not have a home oxygen 
requirement prior to admission. 
.
# Acute on chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: The 
patient's diuretics were held upon admission to the hospital 
secondary to low blood pressures and concern for sepsis. After 
transfer to the floor the patient began to demonstrate signs of 
increasing volume overload on her CXR and physical exam. She was 
diuresed with IV lasix then transitioned to her oral home 
regimen. Her dyspnea and peripheral edema improved. A repeat 
echocardiogram was obtained and showed severe pulmonary 
hypertension, moderate right ventricular dilation and mild 
global hypokinesis, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved left ventricular function, moderate tricuspid 
regurgitation, and mild to moderate aortic and mitral 
regurgitation. She will need continued monitoring of her daily 
weight, Ins and Outs (weigh diapers/pads), and physical exam to 
determine her current volume status and need for additional 
diuresis. 
.
# Atrial Fibrillation: Once her blood pressures were stable, the 
patient was continued on her home regimen of Metoprolol 25 mg 
twice daily for rate control, and Coumadin for anticoagulation. 
Her INR level fluctuated, likely secondary to concurrent 
antibiotics, so this will need to be monitored and re-dosed as 
needed in the future (goal INR .
.
# Acute exacerbation of chronic venous stasis: Upon transfer to 
the floor it was evident that the patient was experiencing an 
acute worsening of her chronic venous stasis. This improved with 
initiation of diuresis and topical triamcinolone applied twice 
daily.
. 
# Delirium (resolved): During majority of her hospital admission 
the patient was alert, attentive, and oriented to person, 
location, and date. She did experience an episode of visual 
hallucinations and waxing/waning consciousness in the setting of 
not having a bowel movement in two days. Additionally, she had 
been receiving ciprofloxacin, which can cause such effects in 
elderly individuals. A more aggressive bowel regimen was started 
and the patient improved to baseline. 
. 
# Anemia: The patient has evidence of a chronic normocytic 
anemia. Her hematocrit remained close to baseline during 
this admission. Fe studies revealed a low Fe (19), low-normal 
ferritin (74), and decreased TIBC. The benefit of starting iron 
supplementation, or pursuing a further evaluation should be 
addressed in the future by her outpatient provider. Fe studies 
should be repeated when not actively ill. 
. 
# hypertension: The patient's blood pressures remained in the systolic 
range of 90-110 and diastolic range of 50-60 while being 
actively diuresed. 
. 
# GERD: The patient's home PPI regimen of Omeprazole was 
continued. 
. 
# history of Breast CA: The patient was on Exemestane prior to 
admission. This was not continued during her hospital admission, 
but re-started upon discharge. 
. 
# Speech and Swallow: The patient was evaluated a number of 
times by Speech who advanced her diet to soft solids and thin 
liquids, meds whole with puree, and recommended swallow follow 
up in rehab to ensure diet tolerance and consider further 
upgrades.
.
# Physical Therapy: The patient was evaluated by physical 
therapy who suggested rehab for continued strength building. 
.
# Code status: The patient was full code during this admission.
.
# Health Care Proxy: Granddaughter ( ) 
 
. 
# Other Considerations: Prior to hospitalization that patient 
was quite physically functional, able to climb 17 stairs without 
getting dyspneic. She went to church weekly. She had an aide 
come to bath her, and occasionally help her dress, but most of 
the time she could dress herself. She has help with cooking and 
cleaning, but can feed herself.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,
.
You were recently admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit of 
 for palpitations and were 
found to have a pneumonia. You were provided respiratory support 
by intubation, and started on intravenous antibiotics, and you 
improved. You were doing well and so you were transferred to the 
General Medicine floor, where we continued your antibiotics and 
re-started medications to remove excess fluid from your body. 
Your breathing and leg swelling improved. We also obtained a 
picture of your heart called an echocardiogram, which showed 
slight worsening of your valve function which you should follow 
up with your cardiologist. You are being discharged to a 
 facility for continued care. There they will 
provide you with your medications, and additional physical 
therapy services to help you build strength. You will need to 
follow up with your primary care physician after you leave 
rehab.
.
We are only making one change to your home medications regimen. 
We are discharging you on a higher dose of Furosemide for 
continued fluid removal.
-Please INCREASE the FREQUENCY of Furosemide to 80 mg twice 
daily
-You will be discharged to the rehab facility with nebulizers to 
help your breathing as your pneumonia resolves

Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.

8.1* 2.1* 1. year-old W with a recent healthcare-associated pneumonia 
( ), presented to the ED with dyspnea and palpitations, 
was found to be in hypoxic respiratory failure and sepsis in 
setting of a new large R-sided pneumonia. She was intubated in 
the Emergency Room and transferred to the Medical Intensive Care 
Unit (MICU) where she spent a brief period of time on levophed 
to keep her blood pressures adequate. She was started on broad 
spectrum antibiotics, extubated without difficulty, and 
transferred to the Medicine floor for further management.
.
# Pneumonia: Possibly occurred secondary to aspiration, although 
unable to tell definitively per a Speech and Swallow evaluation. 
The patient received eight days of intravenous vancomycin, 
aztreonem, and ciprofloxacin to treat her large right-sided 
pneumonia. These antibiotics were chosen in light of her many 
antibiotic allergies. She also received schedueled nebulizer 
treatments, chest physical therapy, beside respiratory 
suctioning, and an acapella valve. She never had a leukocytosis 
or fever. Her blood and sputum cultures were all negative. 
Repeat chest imaging suggested an increasing component of fluid 
overload, for which she was treated with diuretics. Her oxygen 
requirement was slowly weaned from 2 liters via nasal canula to 
1 liter at the time of discharge. This should continue to be 
weaned, as tolerated, at rehab. She did not have a home oxygen 
requirement prior to admission. 
.
# Acute on chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: The 
patient's diuretics were held upon admission to the hospital 
secondary to low blood pressures and concern for sepsis. After 
transfer to the floor the patient began to demonstrate signs of 
increasing volume overload on her CXR and physical exam. She was 
diuresed with IV lasix then transitioned to her oral home 
regimen. Her dyspnea and peripheral edema improved. A repeat 
echocardiogram was obtained and showed severe pulmonary 
hypertension, moderate right ventricular dilation and mild 
global hypokinesis, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved left ventricular function, moderate tricuspid 
regurgitation, and mild to moderate aortic and mitral 
regurgitation. She will need continued monitoring of her daily 
weight, Ins and Outs (weigh diapers/pads), and physical exam to 
determine her current volume status and need for additional 
diuresis. 
.
# Atrial Fibrillation: Once her blood pressures were stable, the 
patient was continued on her home regimen of Metoprolol 25 mg 
twice daily for rate control, and Coumadin for anticoagulation. 
Her INR level fluctuated, likely secondary to concurrent 
antibiotics, so this will need to be monitored and re-dosed as 
needed in the future (goal INR .
.
# Acute exacerbation of chronic venous stasis: Upon transfer to 
the floor it was evident that the patient was experiencing an 
acute worsening of her chronic venous stasis. This improved with 
initiation of diuresis and topical triamcinolone applied twice 
daily.
. 
# Delirium (resolved): During majority of her hospital admission 
the patient was alert, attentive, and oriented to person, 
location, and date. She did experience an episode of visual 
hallucinations and waxing/waning consciousness in the setting of 
not having a bowel movement in two days. Additionally, she had 
been receiving ciprofloxacin, which can cause such effects in 
elderly individuals. A more aggressive bowel regimen was started 
and the patient improved to baseline. 
. 
# Anemia: The patient has evidence of a chronic normocytic 
anemia. Her hematocrit remained close to baseline during 
this admission. Fe studies revealed a low Fe (19), low-normal 
ferritin (74), and decreased TIBC. The benefit of starting iron 
supplementation, or pursuing a further evaluation should be 
addressed in the future by her outpatient provider. Fe studies 
should be repeated when not actively ill. 
. 
# hypertension: The patient's blood pressures remained in the systolic 
range of 90-110 and diastolic range of 50-60 while being 
actively diuresed. 
. 
# GERD: The patient's home PPI regimen of Omeprazole was 
continued. 
. 
# history of Breast CA: The patient was on Exemestane prior to 
admission. This was not continued during her hospital admission, 
but re-started upon discharge. 
. 
# Speech and Swallow: The patient was evaluated a number of 
times by Speech who advanced her diet to soft solids and thin 
liquids, meds whole with puree, and recommended swallow follow 
up in rehab to ensure diet tolerance and consider further 
upgrades.
.
# Physical Therapy: The patient was evaluated by physical 
therapy who suggested rehab for continued strength building. 
.
# Code status: The patient was full code during this admission.
.
# Health Care Proxy: Granddaughter ( ) 
 
. 
# Other Considerations: Prior to hospitalization that patient 
was quite physically functional, able to climb 17 stairs without 
getting dyspneic. She went to church weekly. She had an aide 
come to bath her, and occasionally help her dress, but most of 
the time she could dress herself. She has help with cooking and 
cleaning, but can feed herself.

Dear ,
.
You were recently admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit of 
 for palpitations and were 
found to have a pneumonia. You were provided respiratory support 
by intubation, and started on intravenous antibiotics, and you 
improved. You were doing well and so you were transferred to the 
General Medicine floor, where we continued your antibiotics and 
re-started medications to remove excess fluid from your body. 
Your breathing and leg swelling improved. We also obtained a 
picture of your heart called an echocardiogram, which showed 
slight worsening of your valve function which you should follow 
up with your cardiologist. You are being discharged to a 
 facility for continued care. There they will 
provide you with your medications, and additional physical 
therapy services to help you build strength. You will need to

.

-You will be discharged to the rehab facility with nebulizers to

8.1* 2.1* 1. year-old W with a recent healthcare-associated pneumonia 
( ), presented to the ED with dyspnea and palpitations, 
was found to be in hypoxic respiratory failure and sepsis in 
setting of a new large R-sided pneumonia. She was intubated in 
the Emergency Room and transferred to the Medical Intensive Care 
Unit (MICU) where she spent a brief period of time on levophed 
to keep her blood pressures adequate. She was started on broad 
spectrum antibiotics, extubated without difficulty, and 
transferred to the Medicine floor for further management.
.
# Pneumonia: Possibly occurred secondary to aspiration, although 
unable to tell definitively per a Speech and Swallow evaluation. 
The patient received eight days of intravenous vancomycin, 
aztreonem, and ciprofloxacin to treat her large right-sided 
pneumonia. These antibiotics were chosen in light of her many 
antibiotic allergies. She also received schedueled nebulizer 
treatments, chest physical therapy, beside respiratory 
suctioning, and an acapella valve. She never had a leukocytosis 
or fever. Her blood and sputum cultures were all negative. 
Repeat chest imaging suggested an increasing component of fluid 
overload, for which she was treated with diuretics. Her oxygen 
requirement was slowly weaned from 2 liters via nasal canula to 
1 liter at the time of discharge. This should continue to be 
weaned, as tolerated, at rehab. She did not have a home oxygen 
requirement prior to admission. 
.
# Acute on chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: The 
patient's diuretics were held upon admission to the hospital 
secondary to low blood pressures and concern for sepsis. After 
transfer to the floor the patient began to demonstrate signs of 
increasing volume overload on her CXR and physical exam. She was 
diuresed with IV lasix then transitioned to her oral home 
regimen. Her dyspnea and peripheral edema improved. A repeat 
echocardiogram was obtained and showed severe pulmonary 
hypertension, moderate right ventricular dilation and mild 
global hypokinesis, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved left ventricular function, moderate tricuspid 
regurgitation, and mild to moderate aortic and mitral 
regurgitation. She will need continued monitoring of her daily 
weight, Ins and Outs (weigh diapers/pads), and physical exam to 
determine her current volume status and need for additional 
diuresis. 
.
# Atrial Fibrillation: Once her blood pressures were stable, the 
patient was continued on her home regimen of Metoprolol 25 mg 
twice daily for rate control, and Coumadin for anticoagulation. 
Her INR level fluctuated, likely secondary to concurrent 
antibiotics, so this will need to be monitored and re-dosed as 
needed in the future (goal INR .
.
# Acute exacerbation of chronic venous stasis: Upon transfer to 
the floor it was evident that the patient was experiencing an 
acute worsening of her chronic venous stasis. This improved with 
initiation of diuresis and topical triamcinolone applied twice 
daily.
. 
# Delirium (resolved): During majority of her hospital admission 
the patient was alert, attentive, and oriented to person, 
location, and date. She did experience an episode of visual 
hallucinations and waxing/waning consciousness in the setting of 
not having a bowel movement in two days. Additionally, she had 
been receiving ciprofloxacin, which can cause such effects in 
elderly individuals. A more aggressive bowel regimen was started 
and the patient improved to baseline. 
. 
# Anemia: The patient has evidence of a chronic normocytic 
anemia. Her hematocrit remained close to baseline during 
this admission. Fe studies revealed a low Fe (19), low-normal 
ferritin (74), and decreased TIBC. The benefit of starting iron 
supplementation, or pursuing a further evaluation should be 
addressed in the future by her outpatient provider. Fe studies 
should be repeated when not actively ill. 
. 
# hypertension: The patient's blood pressures remained in the systolic 
range of 90-110 and diastolic range of 50-60 while being 
actively diuresed. 
. 
# GERD: The patient's home PPI regimen of Omeprazole was 
continued. 
. 
# history of Breast CA: The patient was on Exemestane prior to 
admission. This was not continued during her hospital admission, 
but re-started upon discharge. 
. 
# Speech and Swallow: The patient was evaluated a number of 
times by Speech who advanced her diet to soft solids and thin 
liquids, meds whole with puree, and recommended swallow follow 
up in rehab to ensure diet tolerance and consider further 
upgrades.
.
# Physical Therapy: The patient was evaluated by physical 
therapy who suggested rehab for continued strength building. 
.
# Code status: The patient was full code during this admission.
.
# Health Care Proxy: Granddaughter ( ) 
 
. 
# Other Considerations: Prior to hospitalization that patient 
was quite physically functional, able to climb 17 stairs without 
getting dyspneic. She went to church weekly. She had an aide 
come to bath her, and occasionally help her dress, but most of 
the time she could dress herself. She has help with cooking and 
cleaning, but can feed herself.

was found to be in hypoxic respiratory failure and sepsis in 
setting of a new large R-sided pneumonia. She was intubated in 
the Emergency Room and transferred to the Medical Intensive Care 
Unit (MICU) where she spent a brief period of time on levophed 
to keep her blood pressures adequate. She was started on broad 
spectrum antibiotics, extubated without difficulty, and 
transferred to the Medicine floor for further management.
.
# Pneumonia: Possibly occurred secondary to aspiration, although 
unable to tell definitively per a Speech and Swallow evaluation. 
The patient received eight days of intravenous vancomycin, 
aztreonem, and ciprofloxacin to treat her large right-sided 
pneumonia. These antibiotics were chosen in light of her many 
antibiotic allergies. She also received schedueled nebulizer 
treatments, chest physical therapy, beside respiratory 
suctioning, and an acapella valve. She never had a leukocytosis 
or fever. Her blood and sputum cultures were all negative. 
Repeat chest imaging suggested an increasing component of fluid 
overload, for which she was treated with diuretics. Her oxygen 
requirement was slowly weaned from 2 liters via nasal canula to 
1 liter at the time of discharge. This should continue to be 
weaned, as tolerated, at rehab. She did not have a home oxygen 
requirement prior to admission. 
.
# Acute on chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: The 
patient's diuretics were held upon admission to the hospital 
secondary to low blood pressures and concern for sepsis. After 
transfer to the floor the patient began to demonstrate signs of 
increasing volume overload on her CXR and physical exam. She was 
diuresed with IV lasix then transitioned to her oral home 
regimen. Her dyspnea and peripheral edema improved. A repeat 
echocardiogram was obtained and showed severe pulmonary 
hypertension, moderate right ventricular dilation and mild 
global hypokinesis, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved left ventricular function, moderate tricuspid 
regurgitation, and mild to moderate aortic and mitral 
regurgitation. She will need continued monitoring of her daily 
weight, Ins and Outs (weigh diapers/pads), and physical exam to 
determine her current volume status and need for additional 
diuresis. 
.
# Atrial Fibrillation: Once her blood pressures were stable, the 
patient was continued on her home regimen of Metoprolol 25 mg 
twice daily for rate control, and Coumadin for anticoagulation. 
Her INR level fluctuated, likely secondary to concurrent 
antibiotics, so this will need to be monitored and re-dosed as 
needed in the future (goal INR .
.
# Acute exacerbation of chronic venous stasis: Upon transfer to 
the floor it was evident that the patient was experiencing an 
acute worsening of her chronic venous stasis. This improved with 
initiation of diuresis and topical triamcinolone applied twice 
daily.
. 
# Delirium (resolved): During majority of her hospital admission 
the patient was alert, attentive, and oriented to person, 
location, and date. She did experience an episode of visual 
hallucinations and waxing/waning consciousness in the setting of 
not having a bowel movement in two days. Additionally, she had 
been receiving ciprofloxacin, which can cause such effects in 
elderly individuals. A more aggressive bowel regimen was started 
and the patient improved to baseline. 
. 
# Anemia: The patient has evidence of a chronic normocytic 
anemia. Her hematocrit remained close to baseline during 
this admission. Fe studies revealed a low Fe (19), low-normal 
ferritin (74), and decreased TIBC. The benefit of starting iron 
supplementation, or pursuing a further evaluation should be 
addressed in the future by her outpatient provider. Fe studies 
should be repeated when not actively ill. 
. 
# hypertension: The patient's blood pressures remained in the systolic 
range of 90-110 and diastolic range of 50-60 while being 
actively diuresed. 
. 
# GERD: The patient's home PPI regimen of Omeprazole was 
continued. 
. 
# history of Breast CA: The patient was on Exemestane prior to 
admission. This was not continued during her hospital admission, 
but re-started upon discharge. 
. 
# Speech and Swallow: The patient was evaluated a number of 
times by Speech who advanced her diet to soft solids and thin 
liquids, meds whole with puree, and recommended swallow follow 
up in rehab to ensure diet tolerance and consider further 
upgrades.
.
# Physical Therapy: The patient was evaluated by physical 
therapy who suggested rehab for continued strength building. 
.
# Code status: The patient was full code during this admission.
.
# Health Care Proxy: Granddaughter ( ) 
 
. 
# Other Considerations: Prior to hospitalization that patient 
was quite physically functional, able to climb 17 stairs without 
getting dyspneic. She went to church weekly. She had an aide 
come to bath her, and occasionally help her dress, but most of 
the time she could dress herself. She has help with cooking and 
cleaning, but can feed herself.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ,
.
You were recently admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit of 
 for palpitations and were 
found to have a pneumonia. You were provided respiratory support 
by intubation, and started on intravenous antibiotics, and you 
improved. You were doing well and so you were transferred to the 
General Medicine floor, where we continued your antibiotics and 
re-started medications to remove excess fluid from your body. 
Your breathing and leg swelling improved. We also obtained a 
picture of your heart called an echocardiogram, which showed 
slight worsening of your valve function which you should follow 
up with your cardiologist. You are being discharged to a 
 facility for continued care. There they will 
provide you with your medications, and additional physical 
therapy services to help you build strength. You will need to 
follow up with your primary care physician after you leave 
rehab.
.
We are only making one change to your home medications regimen. 
We are discharging you on a higher dose of Furosemide for 
continued fluid removal.
-Please INCREASE the FREQUENCY of Furosemide to 80 mg twice 
daily
-You will be discharged to the rehab facility with nebulizers to 
help your breathing as your pneumonia resolves

Weigh yourself every morning, call MD if weight goes up more 
than 3 lbs.

was taken to the operating room for above described procedure. 
Please see separately dictated operative report for details. The 
surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the 
procedure well. Patient received perioperative IV antibiotics.

a transition to oral pain medications on POD#1. The patient 
received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of 
POD#1. The foley was removed on POD#2 and the patient was 
voiding independently thereafter. The surgical dressing was 
changed on POD#2 and the surgical incision was found to be clean 
and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. The patient 
was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs were checked throughout 
the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of 
discharge the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling 
well. The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. The 
patient's hematocrit was acceptable and pain was adequately 
controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was 
neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign.

Discharge LABTEST Instructions: 1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
 
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect. Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled. These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house. Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc).

8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Lovenox for four (4) 
weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots). If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking this medication. 
 STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.
 
9. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed by the visiting 
nurse or rehab facility in two (2) weeks.
 
10. (once at home): Home , dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after 
surgery.
 
11. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Posterior precautions. No strenuous exercise or heavy 
lifting until follow up appointment. Mobilize frequently

Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge LABTEST Instructions: 1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
 
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect. Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled. These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house. Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc).

8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Lovenox for four (4) 
weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots). If 
you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose while taking this medication. 
 STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.
 
9. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed by the visiting 
nurse or rehab facility in two (2) weeks.
 
10. (once at home): Home , dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after 
surgery.
 
11. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Posterior precautions. No strenuous exercise or heavy 
lifting until follow up appointment. Mobilize frequently

History of Present Illness:
This is a year old AAF with history of severe GERD, DMII, 
hypertension, and hyperlipidemia who presents with chest pain. Reports 
that pain began gradually yesterday afternoon when she was 
washing the dishes. Pain is located substernally, is 
pressure-like in quality, and has a similar quality as her prior 
GERD symptoms with the exception that is located higher in the 
chest (normally present around the epigastric area). Pain does 
not radiate. No nausea/vomiting/shortness of breath/diaphoresis/palpitations. 
Pain not reproduced with palpation. No pleuritic chest pain. 
Took nexium, carafate, and bottle of mylanta with no relief. 
Did not resolve with rest, and was present for approximately 5 
hours.
.
In the ED, VS were 98.2, 101, 159/95, 14, 100%RA. On transfer, 
VS were 98, 72, 115/55, 14, 98%2L. Initially EKG did not show 
any ST changes. Thus far, 3 sets of cardiac enzymes have 
returned negative.
.
On the floor and overnight, patient reports that she is symptoms free 
and would like to return home.

hypertension

GERD

Brief Hospital Course:
ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
 yo AAF with history of severe GERD, DMII, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension 
admitted for CP and .
.
# Chest pain: Patient was admitted for chest pain and to rule 
out myocardial infarction. Presented with atypical chest pain, 
as pain was not aggravated by exertion nor relieved by rest. 
However, given her sex and her multiple comorbidities, she 
needed to be ruled out for MI given its possibility of 
presenting atypically. In the ED, she was given aspirin 325mg. 
Cardiac enzymes were cycled times three and were all negative. 
Patient underwent three ECG tracings that when compared to an 
older tracing and to each other, revealed no ST changes or 
evidence of ischemia or infarction. Patient was asymptomatic 
upon reaching the floor and pain did not recur with ambulation. 
Telemetry overnight exhibited no concerning changes. These 
findings all suggested that this chest pain is unlikely cardiac 
in nature. Differential for chest pain is broad, and pain could 
be attributed to GERD or esophageal spasm, especially given the 
fact that the pain was reported as being similar in nature to 
her usual GERD symptoms with the exception of its higher 
location in the chest. Less likely a pulmonary process such as 
PE, since patient had no tachycardia, tachypnea, absence of 
pleuritic pain, and normal oxygen saturation levels.
.
Patient will follow up with her primary care physician 
 weeks of discharge. It was considered by primary team to 
repeat stress test but patient reported that she had had a 
maximal test that was within normal limits recently. We were 
unable to confirm with the PCP during this admission, but 
patient was instructed to follow up with PCP regarding the 
necessity for repeat testing.
.
# GERD: Given significant history of GERD, this was considered as a 
possible etiology of patient's chest pain. Pain is similar in 
quality but higher in the chest. Cardiac cause of chest pain 
was unlikely based on workup, as above. Patient was continued 
on protonix 40mg PO twice a day and sucralfate. Patient to follow up 
with her gastroenterologist upon discharge.
.
# hypertension: Blood pressures were stable throughout this admission, 
controlled on home diovan. 
.
# Hyperlipidemia: Stable during this admission. Patient was 
continued on simvastatin, her home medication. 
.
# DM, type II: Blood sugars were well controlled during this 
admission. At home, patient is on Glyburide 5mg PO daily and 
Metformin 1000 mg PO twice a day. In the hospital, she was placed on an 
insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet. 
.
# CONTACT: Husband 
Patient is FULL code.
.

Brief Hospital Course:
ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
 yo AAF with history of severe GERD, DMII, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension 
admitted for CP and .
.
# Chest pain: Patient was admitted for chest pain and to rule 
out myocardial infarction. Presented with atypical chest pain, 
as pain was not aggravated by exertion nor relieved by rest. 
However, given her sex and her multiple comorbidities, she 
needed to be ruled out for MI given its possibility of 
presenting atypically. In the ED, she was given aspirin 325mg. 
Cardiac enzymes were cycled times three and were all negative. 
Patient underwent three ECG tracings that when compared to an 
older tracing and to each other, revealed no ST changes or 
evidence of ischemia or infarction. Patient was asymptomatic 
upon reaching the floor and pain did not recur with ambulation. 
Telemetry overnight exhibited no concerning changes. These 
findings all suggested that this chest pain is unlikely cardiac 
in nature. Differential for chest pain is broad, and pain could 
be attributed to GERD or esophageal spasm, especially given the 
fact that the pain was reported as being similar in nature to 
her usual GERD symptoms with the exception of its higher 
location in the chest. Less likely a pulmonary process such as 
PE, since patient had no tachycardia, tachypnea, absence of 
pleuritic pain, and normal oxygen saturation levels.
.
Patient will follow up with her primary care physician 
 weeks of discharge. It was considered by primary team to 
repeat stress test but patient reported that she had had a 
maximal test that was within normal limits recently. We were 
unable to confirm with the PCP during this admission, but 
patient was instructed to follow up with PCP regarding the 
necessity for repeat testing.
.
# GERD: Given significant history of GERD, this was considered as a 
possible etiology of patient's chest pain. Pain is similar in 
quality but higher in the chest. Cardiac cause of chest pain 
was unlikely based on workup, as above. Patient was continued 
on protonix 40mg PO twice a day and sucralfate. Patient to follow up 
with her gastroenterologist upon discharge.
.
# hypertension: Blood pressures were stable throughout this admission, 
controlled on home diovan. 
.
# Hyperlipidemia: Stable during this admission. Patient was 
continued on simvastatin, her home medication. 
.
# DM, type II: Blood sugars were well controlled during this 
admission. At home, patient is on Glyburide 5mg PO daily and 
Metformin 1000 mg PO twice a day. In the hospital, she was placed on an 
insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet. 
.
# CONTACT: Husband 
Patient is FULL code.
.

Brief Hospital Course:
ASSESSMENT & PLAN:
 yo AAF with history of severe GERD, DMII, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension 
admitted for CP and .
.
# Chest pain: Patient was admitted for chest pain and to rule 
out myocardial infarction. Presented with atypical chest pain, 
as pain was not aggravated by exertion nor relieved by rest. 
However, given her sex and her multiple comorbidities, she 
needed to be ruled out for MI given its possibility of 
presenting atypically. In the ED, she was given aspirin 325mg. 
Cardiac enzymes were cycled times three and were all negative. 
Patient underwent three ECG tracings that when compared to an 
older tracing and to each other, revealed no ST changes or 
evidence of ischemia or infarction. Patient was asymptomatic 
upon reaching the floor and pain did not recur with ambulation. 
Telemetry overnight exhibited no concerning changes. These 
findings all suggested that this chest pain is unlikely cardiac 
in nature. Differential for chest pain is broad, and pain could 
be attributed to GERD or esophageal spasm, especially given the 
fact that the pain was reported as being similar in nature to 
her usual GERD symptoms with the exception of its higher 
location in the chest. Less likely a pulmonary process such as 
PE, since patient had no tachycardia, tachypnea, absence of 
pleuritic pain, and normal oxygen saturation levels.
.
Patient will follow up with her primary care physician 
 weeks of discharge. It was considered by primary team to 
repeat stress test but patient reported that she had had a 
maximal test that was within normal limits recently. We were 
unable to confirm with the PCP during this admission, but 
patient was instructed to follow up with PCP regarding the 
necessity for repeat testing.
.
# GERD: Given significant history of GERD, this was considered as a 
possible etiology of patient's chest pain. Pain is similar in 
quality but higher in the chest. Cardiac cause of chest pain 
was unlikely based on workup, as above. Patient was continued 
on protonix 40mg PO twice a day and sucralfate. Patient to follow up 
with her gastroenterologist upon discharge.
.
# hypertension: Blood pressures were stable throughout this admission, 
controlled on home diovan. 
.
# Hyperlipidemia: Stable during this admission. Patient was 
continued on simvastatin, her home medication. 
.
# DM, type II: Blood sugars were well controlled during this 
admission. At home, patient is on Glyburide 5mg PO daily and 
Metformin 1000 mg PO twice a day. In the hospital, she was placed on an 
insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet. 
.
# CONTACT: Husband 
Patient is FULL code.
.
 
 
Medications on Admission:
Diovan 80 mg Tab q at bedtime
Calcium-Cholecalciferol (D3) 600 mg-400 unit Qday
Aspirin 81 mg Chewable Tab q day
Multivitamin Cap qday
Omega-3 Fatty Acids 1,000 mg Cap
Simvastatin 80 mg Tab
Glyburide 2.5 mg Tab twice a day
Metformin 1000 mg Tab twice a day
Nexium 40mg twice a day
Sucralfate 1 gm PO Q6H 
 
Discharge LABTEST Medications: 1. Valsartan 40 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO HS (at 
bedtime). 
2. Calcium Carbonate 500 mg Tablet, Chewable Sig: One (1) 
Tablet, Chewable PO three times a day (3 times a day). 
3. Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 400 unit Tablet Sig: Two (2) 
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 
4. Multivitamin Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). 
 
5. Omega-3 Fatty Acids Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO DAILY 
(Daily). 
6. Simvastatin 40 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO HS (at 
bedtime). 
7. Aspirin 81 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. 
8. Glyburide 2.5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day. 
9. Metformin 1,000 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day. 
 
10. Sucralfate 1 gram Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO four times a day (4 times 
a day). 
11. Nexium 40 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) 
Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO twice a day.

Home

Atypical Chest Pain

Hyperlipidemia

Stable

You came to the hospital with chest pain. Your EKG and lab 
tests indicated that this was NOT due to damage to your heart.

.

.
Please follow up with your doctors as below.
.

Patient underwent three ECG tracings that when compared to an 
older tracing and to each other, revealed no ST changes or 
evidence of ischemia or infarction. Patient was asymptomatic 
upon reaching the floor and pain did not recur with ambulation. 
Telemetry overnight exhibited no concerning changes. These 
findings all suggested that this chest pain is unlikely cardiac 
in nature. Differential for chest pain is broad, and pain could 
be attributed to GERD or esophageal spasm, especially given the 
fact that the pain was reported as being similar in nature to 
her usual GERD symptoms with the exception of its higher 
location in the chest. Less likely a pulmonary process such as 
PE, since patient had no tachycardia, tachypnea, absence of 
pleuritic pain, and normal oxygen saturation levels.
.
Patient will follow up with her primary care physician 
 weeks of discharge. It was considered by primary team to 
repeat stress test but patient reported that she had had a 
maximal test that was within normal limits recently. We were 
unable to confirm with the PCP during this admission, but 
patient was instructed to follow up with PCP regarding the 
necessity for repeat testing.
.
# GERD: Given significant history of GERD, this was considered as a 
possible etiology of patient's chest pain. Pain is similar in 
quality but higher in the chest. Cardiac cause of chest pain 
was unlikely based on workup, as above. Patient was continued 
on protonix 40mg PO twice a day and sucralfate. Patient to follow up 
with her gastroenterologist upon discharge.
.
# hypertension: Blood pressures were stable throughout this admission, 
controlled on home diovan. 
.
# Hyperlipidemia: Stable during this admission. Patient was 
continued on simvastatin, her home medication. 
.
# DM, type II: Blood sugars were well controlled during this 
admission. At home, patient is on Glyburide 5mg PO daily and 
Metformin 1000 mg PO twice a day. In the hospital, she was placed on an 
insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet. 
.
# CONTACT: Husband 
Patient is FULL code.
.

She describes the sensation as both numbness of her right leg

symptoms are worst at night and in the morning.

pain. It is a pain located in the posterior Right calf and Right

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. is a yo woman with no prior relevant medical 
history who presented with five days of progressive right leg 
weakness, pain and dysesthesia. Also, found hyperglycemic to 
the 300s.

On admission her physical exam was remarkable for dysesthesia, 
as well as weakness of her right leg following the distribution 
of L4-S1, with S1 more prominent. During her stay she later 
developed pain of her right leg. 

Work up included MRI of the brain, spine, and sacrum which were 
unremarkable. A lumbar puncture and CSF was bland. CSF 
infectious workup and cytology were negative. 

Given the onset of acute, progressive right leg numbness, 
tingling, pain and weakness in the setting of severe 
hyperglycemia and polyuria and weight loss, the diagnosis is 
most consistent with diabetic amyotrophy. She was given three 
days of high dose IV solumedrol. Pain was managed with 
gabapentin 300mg PO three times a day, as well as tramadol twice a day. She had some 
symptomatic improvement of her dysesthesias and weakness. 

In terms of hyperglycemia, her A1C was 13%. She was managed by 
the for glucose control, and started on standing 
NPH insulin and sliding scale which were uptitrated in the 
setting of steroid administration. Nutrition service was 
consulted for diabetes teaching. She was discharged with PCP 
follow up for endocrinology referral. She was discharged with 
follow up at the neurology outpatient clinic.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to Neurology service at with right leg 
weakness, tingling and pain. You were also found to have high 
blood sugar and new onset diabetes. Your leg weakness and 
tingling is caused by the effects of high blood sugar on your 
nerves, a syndrome we call "diabetic amyotrophy." The diabetes 
has caused inflammation of your nerves which we have treated 
with steroids and anti- inflammatory medications (which you will 
continue). To eliminate other causes of nerve dysfunction, an 
MRI of your spine and nerves was completed and this was normal. 
A lumbar puncture was also performed and all the results have 
returned normal. 

You were also seen by the diabetes doctors and were started 
on insulin which you will need to continue at home. Nutrition 
has aslo recommended a strict diabetic diet. 

1. Please continue all your medications as directed by this 
document.
2. We have scheduled a follow up appointment with your NP 
 at office at . (Phone on . We have 
asked them to direct you to an endocrinologist in your group. 
3. Please do not hesitate to call your primary care doctor with 
questions.
4. You will have follow up with neurology as below.

Ms. is a yo woman with no prior relevant medical 
history who presented with five days of progressive right leg 
weakness, pain and dysesthesia. Also, found hyperglycemic to 
the 300s.

On admission her physical exam was remarkable for dysesthesia, 
as well as weakness of her right leg following the distribution 
of L4-S1, with S1 more prominent. During her stay she later 
developed pain of her right leg. 

Work up included MRI of the brain, spine, and sacrum which were 
unremarkable. A lumbar puncture and CSF was bland. CSF 
infectious workup and cytology were negative. 

Given the onset of acute, progressive right leg numbness, 
tingling, pain and weakness in the setting of severe 
hyperglycemia and polyuria and weight loss, the diagnosis is 
most consistent with diabetic amyotrophy. She was given three 
days of high dose IV solumedrol. Pain was managed with 
gabapentin 300mg PO three times a day, as well as tramadol twice a day. She had some 
symptomatic improvement of her dysesthesias and weakness. 

In terms of hyperglycemia, her A1C was 13%. She was managed by 
the for glucose control, and started on standing 
NPH insulin and sliding scale which were uptitrated in the 
setting of steroid administration. Nutrition service was 
consulted for diabetes teaching. She was discharged with PCP 
follow up for endocrinology referral. She was discharged with 
follow up at the neurology outpatient clinic.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to Neurology service at with right leg 
weakness, tingling and pain. You were also found to have high 
blood sugar and new onset diabetes. Your leg weakness and 
tingling is caused by the effects of high blood sugar on your 
nerves, a syndrome we call "diabetic amyotrophy." The diabetes 
has caused inflammation of your nerves which we have treated 
with steroids and anti- inflammatory medications (which you will 
continue). To eliminate other causes of nerve dysfunction, an 
MRI of your spine and nerves was completed and this was normal. 
A lumbar puncture was also performed and all the results have 
returned normal. 

You were also seen by the diabetes doctors and were started 
on insulin which you will need to continue at home. Nutrition 
has aslo recommended a strict diabetic diet.

document.

 at office at . (Phone on . We have

questions.

History of Present Illness:
Ms. is a right handed woman with no 
significant past medical history who presents with a 5 day 
history of progressive RLE ascending sensory change and 
weakness. 

She awoke 5 days ago feeling well and at her baseline. While 
getting ready to go to a 16 party in the evening, she 
noticed that her right foot felt "asleep" below the ankle, with 
pins and needles paresthesias. She otherwise felt well and went 
to the party. However, at the party, the unusual sensation 
continued. She had trouble walking in heels and felt she was 
limping because she did not know "where her foot was". She had 
to take her heels off to ambulate. Additionally, she felt she 
was perhaps slightly weak in the right leg.

She describes the sensation as both numbness of her right leg 
and "pins and needles" that is worsened to the touch. Her 
symptoms are worst at night and in the morning.

She went to bed, and when she awoke the next morning, the 
unusual sensation extended slightly higher than it did before. 
Additionally, she had onset of intermittent sharp Right leg 
pain. It is a pain located in the posterior Right calf and Right 
popliteal fossa. It is incited by activity or dorsiflexion/ 
stretching of her right foot. It does not radiate down from her 
back or further into her foot.

Following this, she initially took it "easy" and mostly relaxed 
at home, hoping the symptoms would improve. She avoided driving 
for a while due to concern that she could not feel her foot (but 
subsequently has restarted due to work requirements). When her 
symptoms did not improve, she presented to her PCP and underwent 
 Spine x-rays (available in atrius records and benign) and 
RLE duplex, with no evidence of clot. 

She was referred to neurology with an appointment Planned for 
 . However, she was contacted by his office and recommended 
and ED visit given the progressive nature of her symptoms. 

The following days her symptoms progressed and the sensory 
change/paresthesia gradually reached the approximately the 
way up her leg. Her gait gradually worsened, she continued to 
"limp" and she felt constantly unbalanced (but did not fall). 
There are not, and have never been symptoms in the left leg.

There is no recent history of trauma, heavy lifting or unusual 
activities. There is no associated back pain. She has never had 
anything like this before. No prior episodes of numbness or 
weakness (transient or otherwise). No prior episodes of 
transient eye pain or visual changes. No recent 
illnesses-diarrheal or otherwise. Her has mild headaches 
intermittently (and has for years). These headaches have not 
changed and are not positional. They do not wake her at night.

Otherwise ROS positive for intentional weight loss over the past 
year.

On neurologic review of systems, the patient denies 
lightheadedness, or confusion. Denies difficulty with producing 
or comprehending speech. Denies loss of vision, blurred vision, 
diplopia, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, dysarthria, or 
dysphagia. Denies focal muscle weakness, numbness, 
parasthesia(excluding that detailed above). Denies loss of 
sensation. Denies
bowel or bladder incontinence or retention. Denies difficulty 
with gait.

On general review of systems, the patient denies fevers, rigors, 
night sweats, or noticeable weight loss. Denies chest pain, 
palpitations, dyspnea, or cough. Denies nausea, vomiting, 
diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal pain. No recent change in 
bowel or bladder habits. Denies dysuria or hematuria. Denies 
myalgias, arthralgias, or rash.

paraphasias. No dysarthria. Normal prosody. Able to register 3 
objects and recall at 5 minutes. No apraxia. No evidence of 
hemineglect. No left-right confusion. Able to follow both 
midline and appendicular commands. Follows 2 step commands.

- Cranial Nerves - PERRL 5->2 brisk. No RAPD. VF full to number 
counting. No red desaturation. EOMI, no nystagmus. V1-V3 
without deficits to light touch and pin prick bilaterally. No 
facial movement asymmetry. Hearing intact to finger rub 
bilaterally. Palate elevation symmetric. Trapezius strength 
bilaterally.
Tongue midline.

- Motor - Normal bulk and tone. No drift. No tremor or 
asterixis.

- Sensory - In the Right lower extremity, sensation above the 
knee is normal. Starting at approximately the anterior tibial 
tuberosity, she has onset of decrease sensation and paresthesia 
to pinprick. This is present circumferentially around her calf 
and down into her feet. Pinprick feels like "pins and needles". 
 Starting slightly below the anterior tibial tuberosity, she has 
decreased sensation to light touch (approximately 25% of 
normal). There is perhaps a mild gradient to this, with more 
distal sensation perhaps being decreased compared to more 
proximally, but it is subtle. Vibration in the RLE at the great

the right. Propriopception is entirely intact at the Right 
great
toe. In the other extremities, there are no deficits to light

alternating movements bilaterally. Toe to target accurate on 
RLE.

gait appears stable, with small steps and a normal base. There 
is a subtle, possible antalgic gait protecting the right foot. 
She is able to tandem without falls. She has trouble heel and 
toe walking due to limited ability to plantar and dorsiflex the 
right foot, but is otherwise stable.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. is a yo woman with no prior relevant medical 
history who presented with five days of progressive right leg 
weakness, pain and dysesthesia. Also, found hyperglycemic to 
the 300s.

On admission her physical exam was remarkable for dysesthesia, 
as well as weakness of her right leg following the distribution 
of L4-S1, with S1 more prominent. During her stay she later 
developed pain of her right leg. 

Work up included MRI of the brain, spine, and sacrum which were 
unremarkable. A lumbar puncture and CSF was bland. CSF 
infectious workup and cytology were negative. 

Given the onset of acute, progressive right leg numbness, 
tingling, pain and weakness in the setting of severe 
hyperglycemia and polyuria and weight loss, the diagnosis is 
most consistent with diabetic amyotrophy. She was given three 
days of high dose IV solumedrol. Pain was managed with 
gabapentin 300mg PO three times a day, as well as tramadol twice a day. She had some 
symptomatic improvement of her dysesthesias and weakness. 

In terms of hyperglycemia, her A1C was 13%. She was managed by 
the for glucose control, and started on standing 
NPH insulin and sliding scale which were uptitrated in the 
setting of steroid administration. Nutrition service was 
consulted for diabetes teaching. She was discharged with PCP 
follow up for endocrinology referral. She was discharged with 
follow up at the neurology outpatient clinic.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to Neurology service at with right leg 
weakness, tingling and pain. You were also found to have high 
blood sugar and new onset diabetes. Your leg weakness and 
tingling is caused by the effects of high blood sugar on your 
nerves, a syndrome we call "diabetic amyotrophy." The diabetes 
has caused inflammation of your nerves which we have treated 
with steroids and anti- inflammatory medications (which you will 
continue). To eliminate other causes of nerve dysfunction, an 
MRI of your spine and nerves was completed and this was normal. 
A lumbar puncture was also performed and all the results have 
returned normal. 

You were also seen by the diabetes doctors and were started 
on insulin which you will need to continue at home. Nutrition 
has aslo recommended a strict diabetic diet. 

1. Please continue all your medications as directed by this 
document.

 at office at . (Phone on . We have 
asked them to direct you to an endocrinologist in your group.

questions.

getting ready to go to a 16 party in the evening, she 
noticed that her right foot felt "asleep" below the ankle, with 
pins and needles paresthesias. She otherwise felt well and went 
to the party. However, at the party, the unusual sensation 
continued. She had trouble walking in heels and felt she was 
limping because she did not know "where her foot was". She had 
to take her heels off to ambulate. Additionally, she felt she 
was perhaps slightly weak in the right leg.

She describes the sensation as both numbness of her right leg 
and "pins and needles" that is worsened to the touch. Her 
symptoms are worst at night and in the morning.

She went to bed, and when she awoke the next morning, the 
unusual sensation extended slightly higher than it did before. 
Additionally, she had onset of intermittent sharp Right leg 
pain. It is a pain located in the posterior Right calf and Right 
popliteal fossa. It is incited by activity or dorsiflexion/ 
stretching of her right foot. It does not radiate down from her 
back or further into her foot.

Following this, she initially took it "easy" and mostly relaxed 
at home, hoping the symptoms would improve. She avoided driving 
for a while due to concern that she could not feel her foot (but 
subsequently has restarted due to work requirements). When her 
symptoms did not improve, she presented to her PCP and underwent 
 Spine x-rays (available in atrius records and benign) and 
RLE duplex, with no evidence of clot. 

She was referred to neurology with an appointment Planned for

and ED visit given the progressive nature of her symptoms.

change/paresthesia gradually reached the approximately the

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. is a yo woman with no prior relevant medical 
history who presented with five days of progressive right leg 
weakness, pain and dysesthesia. Also, found hyperglycemic to 
the 300s.

On admission her physical exam was remarkable for dysesthesia, 
as well as weakness of her right leg following the distribution 
of L4-S1, with S1 more prominent. During her stay she later 
developed pain of her right leg. 

Work up included MRI of the brain, spine, and sacrum which were 
unremarkable. A lumbar puncture and CSF was bland. CSF 
infectious workup and cytology were negative. 

Given the onset of acute, progressive right leg numbness, 
tingling, pain and weakness in the setting of severe 
hyperglycemia and polyuria and weight loss, the diagnosis is 
most consistent with diabetic amyotrophy. She was given three 
days of high dose IV solumedrol. Pain was managed with 
gabapentin 300mg PO three times a day, as well as tramadol twice a day. She had some 
symptomatic improvement of her dysesthesias and weakness. 

In terms of hyperglycemia, her A1C was 13%. She was managed by 
the for glucose control, and started on standing 
NPH insulin and sliding scale which were uptitrated in the 
setting of steroid administration. Nutrition service was 
consulted for diabetes teaching. She was discharged with PCP 
follow up for endocrinology referral. She was discharged with 
follow up at the neurology outpatient clinic.

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to Neurology service at with right leg 
weakness, tingling and pain. You were also found to have high 
blood sugar and new onset diabetes. Your leg weakness and 
tingling is caused by the effects of high blood sugar on your 
nerves, a syndrome we call "diabetic amyotrophy." The diabetes 
has caused inflammation of your nerves which we have treated 
with steroids and anti- inflammatory medications (which you will 
continue). To eliminate other causes of nerve dysfunction, an 
MRI of your spine and nerves was completed and this was normal. 
A lumbar puncture was also performed and all the results have 
returned normal. 

You were also seen by the diabetes doctors and were started 
on insulin which you will need to continue at home. Nutrition 
has aslo recommended a strict diabetic diet.

document.

 at office at . (Phone on . We have 
asked them to direct you to an endocrinologist in your group. 
3. Please do not hesitate to call your primary care doctor with 
questions.
4. You will have follow up with neurology as below.

History of Present Illness:
Ms. is a right handed woman with no 
significant past medical history who presents with a 5 day 
history of progressive RLE ascending sensory change and 
weakness. 

She awoke 5 days ago feeling well and at her baseline. While 
getting ready to go to a 16 party in the evening, she 
noticed that her right foot felt "asleep" below the ankle, with 
pins and needles paresthesias. She otherwise felt well and went 
to the party. However, at the party, the unusual sensation 
continued. She had trouble walking in heels and felt she was 
limping because she did not know "where her foot was". She had 
to take her heels off to ambulate. Additionally, she felt she 
was perhaps slightly weak in the right leg.

She describes the sensation as both numbness of her right leg 
and "pins and needles" that is worsened to the touch. Her 
symptoms are worst at night and in the morning.

She went to bed, and when she awoke the next morning, the 
unusual sensation extended slightly higher than it did before. 
Additionally, she had onset of intermittent sharp Right leg 
pain. It is a pain located in the posterior Right calf and Right 
popliteal fossa. It is incited by activity or dorsiflexion/ 
stretching of her right foot. It does not radiate down from her 
back or further into her foot.

Following this, she initially took it "easy" and mostly relaxed 
at home, hoping the symptoms would improve. She avoided driving 
for a while due to concern that she could not feel her foot (but 
subsequently has restarted due to work requirements). When her 
symptoms did not improve, she presented to her PCP and underwent 
 Spine x-rays (available in atrius records and benign) and 
RLE duplex, with no evidence of clot. 

She was referred to neurology with an appointment Planned for 
 . However, she was contacted by his office and recommended 
and ED visit given the progressive nature of her symptoms. 

The following days her symptoms progressed and the sensory 
change/paresthesia gradually reached the approximately the 
way up her leg. Her gait gradually worsened, she continued to 
"limp" and she felt constantly unbalanced (but did not fall). 
There are not, and have never been symptoms in the left leg.

There is no recent history of trauma, heavy lifting or unusual 
activities. There is no associated back pain. She has never had 
anything like this before. No prior episodes of numbness or 
weakness (transient or otherwise). No prior episodes of 
transient eye pain or visual changes. No recent 
illnesses-diarrheal or otherwise. Her has mild headaches 
intermittently (and has for years). These headaches have not 
changed and are not positional. They do not wake her at night.

Otherwise ROS positive for intentional weight loss over the past 
year.

On neurologic review of systems, the patient denies 
lightheadedness, or confusion. Denies difficulty with producing 
or comprehending speech. Denies loss of vision, blurred vision, 
diplopia, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, dysarthria, or 
dysphagia. Denies focal muscle weakness, numbness, 
parasthesia(excluding that detailed above). Denies loss of 
sensation. Denies
bowel or bladder incontinence or retention. Denies difficulty 
with gait.

On general review of systems, the patient denies fevers, rigors, 
night sweats, or noticeable weight loss. Denies chest pain, 
palpitations, dyspnea, or cough. Denies nausea, vomiting, 
diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal pain. No recent change in 
bowel or bladder habits. Denies dysuria or hematuria. Denies 
myalgias, arthralgias, or rash.

Brief Hospital Course:
 with history of gastric bypass, hypertension, hld, paroxysmal afib on 
coumadin intially presented to OSH on with suprapubic pain, 
dizziness, and inability to focus where he was found to have 
acute cholecystitis and choledocolithiasis status post lap chole, 
choledocotomy, placement of t tube and jp c/b self-removal of 
t-tube and failed oversewing of cbd p/f ercp for management of 
bile leak. 

#) Biliary Leak - This was a complication of self removal of 
t-tube from CBD setting of agitation at OSH. Oversewing of 
CBD was unsuccessful and 2 jp drains place upon transfer 
here. patient went to ERCP for possible stent placement but this was 
unsuccessful. Sphincterotomy and spincteroplasty were performed 
and removal of stone was successful. Unable to recanulate duct 
however for bridging stent placement. JP drains have been 
monitored while here and output has downtrended basically no 
output over the last 24 hrs. 

#) Choledocolithiasis/Cholecystitis status post surgical management - 
status post cholecystectomy, cholangiogram, and choledocotomy on 
presentation. LFTs trending down over course of stay here. 
Blood cx have been negative. Initially covered with 
cipro/flagyl while house but transitioned to zosyn given 
strep pneumo sputum. 

#) Agitation - required precidex/midaz after extubation which 
was transitioned to haldol/ativan. Finally, adequate management 
was achieved with dilaudid for pain with as needed haldol. Felt to be 
primarily ICU delirium with underlying infection after prolonged 
intuibation. Mental status was clearing on discharge. 

#) ? VAP - patient arrived intubated. Was extubated on HD2. patient 
spiked fever with sputum cx growing strep pneumo. At this 
point, patient was switched to zosyn from flagyl/cipro to cover 
strep. CXR showed ? bibasilar infiltrates. He should complete 
8 day course for pna. 

#) PAF - on coumadin at home. INR downtrending while coumadin 
held. Off lovenox bridge as of the . Currently NSR. Not 
on rate control at home. CHADS score of 2. Will need 
consideration of heparin bridge or reinitiation of coumadin.
 
#) hypertension - mildly hypotensive here while on prop. Normalized once 
extubated. Have been holding lisinopril. 

TRANSITIONAL
#) heparin/coumadin for afib
#) cont abx fo vap/biliary source

Brief Hospital Course:
 with history of gastric bypass, hypertension, hld, paroxysmal afib on 
coumadin intially presented to OSH on with suprapubic pain, 
dizziness, and inability to focus where he was found to have 
acute cholecystitis and choledocolithiasis status post lap chole, 
choledocotomy, placement of t tube and jp c/b self-removal of 
t-tube and failed oversewing of cbd p/f ercp for management of 
bile leak. 

#) Biliary Leak - This was a complication of self removal of 
t-tube from CBD setting of agitation at OSH. Oversewing of 
CBD was unsuccessful and 2 jp drains place upon transfer 
here. patient went to ERCP for possible stent placement but this was 
unsuccessful. Sphincterotomy and spincteroplasty were performed 
and removal of stone was successful. Unable to recanulate duct 
however for bridging stent placement. JP drains have been 
monitored while here and output has downtrended basically no 
output over the last 24 hrs. 

#) Choledocolithiasis/Cholecystitis status post surgical management - 
status post cholecystectomy, cholangiogram, and choledocotomy on 
presentation. LFTs trending down over course of stay here. 
Blood cx have been negative. Initially covered with 
cipro/flagyl while house but transitioned to zosyn given 
strep pneumo sputum. 

#) Agitation - required precidex/midaz after extubation which 
was transitioned to haldol/ativan. Finally, adequate management 
was achieved with dilaudid for pain with as needed haldol. Felt to be 
primarily ICU delirium with underlying infection after prolonged 
intuibation. Mental status was clearing on discharge. 

#) ? VAP - patient arrived intubated. Was extubated on HD2. patient 
spiked fever with sputum cx growing strep pneumo. At this 
point, patient was switched to zosyn from flagyl/cipro to cover 
strep. CXR showed ? bibasilar infiltrates. He should complete 
8 day course for pna. 

#) PAF - on coumadin at home. INR downtrending while coumadin 
held. Off lovenox bridge as of the . Currently NSR. Not 
on rate control at home. CHADS score of 2. Will need 
consideration of heparin bridge or reinitiation of coumadin.
 
#) hypertension - mildly hypotensive here while on prop. Normalized once 
extubated. Have been holding lisinopril. 

TRANSITIONAL
#) heparin/coumadin for afib
#) cont abx fo vap/biliary source

Left Femur 4 views 
Although there is no convincing fracture, there is a small to
moderate-sized joint effusion, sometimes may be associated with
occult fracture or quadriceps injury.

Suspected early pulmonary edema. Patchy airspace opacities at

suspicious for superimposed infection/aspiration, for clinical
correlation.

1. No acute fracture or traumatic malalignment. 
2. Benign-appearing fatty lucency within the left lateral mass 
of
the C2 vertebra, may represent area of fatty marrow adjacent to
the venous plexus, small hemangioma or less likely intraosseous
lipoma. 
3. Given presence chondrocalcinosis in the cervical spine, lower
extremity involving the pubic symphysis and knee, patient may
have CPPD. 
 
CT Lower Extremity 
1. No acute fracture or dislocation. 
2. There is no joint effusion or lipohemarthrosis. Apparent
density over the suprapatellar recess, likely artifactual and 
due
to confluent shadow on recent radiograph. 
3. No large retracted quadriceps or patellar tendon tear,
although there is likely remote injury to the rectus femoris on

Brief Hospital Course:
 year old woman with history of anemia (Hb and hypertension (not 
on medication) admitted after a mechanical fall. Trauma survey 
including CT head/C-spine and L knee x-ray notable only for 
moderate knee effusion. Incidentally found to be in AFib on 
routine EKG. Patient declined anticoagulation or further 
evaluation with TTE. CXR on admission abnormal (edema vs 
infection vs inflammation) however patient asymptomatic so did 
not receive treatment. evaluated and recommended rehab 
however patient declined, and was discharged home (she lives 
with children) with and home . After thorough discussion, 
patient signed MOLST form changing code status to DNR/DNI.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- Newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation, patient declined further 
evaluation with TTE and anticoagulation; daughter/patient 
open/amenable to discussion of aspirin and were made aware of 
its limited efficacy in this scenario
- Abnormal CXR of unclear clinical relevance, can consider 
further evaluation with repeat CXR and possible chest CT if 
patient desires
- Signed MOLST indicating DNR/DNI

ACUTE ISSUES:
#L knee effusion
#Fall: Mechanical, witnessed fall with headstrike onto L knee. 
CT head/neck without bleed or fracture. L knee plain film with 
moderate effusion, no major fracture, unable to rule out smaller 
fracture. AROM and PROM limited by joint effusion. Ligaments 
intact on exam. Evaluated by who recommended rehab however 
patient declined, arranged home and . Pain control with 
tylenol, ice, NSAIDs after ~72 hours due to possible 
hemarthrosis.

# Atrial fibrillation: Newly diagnosed, rates well controlled. 
CHADSVASC at least 3 (age, sex). Patient declined 
anticoagulation and understood risks of stroke. Also declined 
TTE. Prior to discharge, the patient's daughter inquired about 
taking aspirin for stroke prevention. I informed her that 
aspirin is not typically recommended given the efficacy of 
anticoagulants; I suggested that the data for aspirin is weak, 
suggesting a possibly a 20% reduction in stroke risk, however, 
also an increase in bleeding risk, similar to that of the 
anticoagulants. I have encouraged her to continue to discuss 
this with her outpatient providers. 

#Abnormal CXR
#Elevated BNP: Patient declines significant respiratory 
symptoms. Did not want further evaluation with TTE given would 
not take any recommended medications or have any procedures. 
Received one dose of CTX/azithromycin in ED, no further 
antibiotics.

#GOC: After thorough code status discussion, given preferences 
for avoiding medical care, patient signed MOLST changing code 
status to DNR/DNI

concern her deafness will contribute to cognitive decline

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to the hospital after you fell on your knee. 
The physical therapists thought you would benefit from rehab, 
but you preferred to return home. We discussed your abnormal 
heart rhythm, atrial fibrillation, which is where the top 
portions of the heart quiver. This heart rhythm increases your 
risk for stroke and the risk of stroke can be decreased by 
taking a blood thinner medication. We spoke about aspirin as a 
possible alternative. The research suggests that there is some, 
but not a strong benefit of taking aspirin for this reason. I 
encourage you to continue to discuss blood thinners with your 
primary care doctor. 

You should take acetaminophen (tylenol) to treat your pain. 
Taking Tylenol three times a day can really improve pain so that 
you can participate in physical therapy. You should avoid NSAIDs 
(i.e. ibuprofen, naproxen) for the next 2 days as they might 
worsen your knee swelling, but then can start ibuprofen to help 
with pain.

During this hospital stay, we also signed a MOLST form which you 
should put on your refrigerator. We've scheduled a follow up 
appointment for you and hope that you keep it! 

It was a pleasure participating in your care,

Your Team
