text:
Name: Unit No: 
 
Admission Date: Discharge Date: 
 
Date of Birth: Sex: F
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
penicillin V
 
Attending: .
 
Chief Complaint:
mechanical fall
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

 
History of Present Illness:
 y.o F status post mechanical fall in Back , during which time she
tripped on a sidewalk, and landed on her left hip and knee. She
also hit her right forehead on the cement. She was walking with
her son, who partially caught her, so her head had minimal
impact. She denied loss of consciousness, but had a right
forehead abrasion, for which wound care done. 

In the ED, initial VS were 99.0, HR 61, BP 145/94, RR 16.
Labs showed H/H of 9.3/30.4. BMP WNL. Troponin negative x 1. 
CT Head negative. CT Cspine negative.
CT lower extremity negative for acute fracture.
CXR significant for ventral venous congestion, possible early
pulmonary edema, and patchy airspace opacities at the lung bases
suggestive of superimposed infection/aspiration.
She was given CTX and azithromycin for possible pneumonia.

Upon arrival to the floor, the patient is very hard of hearing 
so
the history is slightly limited. She reports she has not seen a
doctor in years. She has no medical problems that she knows
about. She reports she was walking with her son in Back when
she tripped on uneven sidewalk and fell and hit the cement. She
denies loss of consciousness or headstrike. She denies feeling
dizzy, or having chest pain. She reports that she has
longstanding shortness of breath with exertion, but states this
occurs after walking half a mile. She otherwise denies fevers,
chills, diarrhea, dysuria. She endorses post nasal drip and a 
dry
cough. She endorses left leg numbness and pain. It is difficult
to pinpoint the pain in her left leg. She describes it as
"vague." 

ROS: Pertinent positives and negatives as noted in the HPI. All
other systems were reviewed and are negative. 

 
Past Medical History:
- Hypothyroidism
- HLD
- Hearing loss
- hypertension
- H pylori
- Iron deficiency 
- Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
 
Social History:
 
Family History:
Denies FH of cardiac problems.

 
Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM
VITALS: Afebrile and vital signs stable (see eFlowsheet)
GENERAL: Alert and in no apparent distress, very hard of hearing
EYES: Anicteric, pupils equally round
ENT: Abrasion over right lip and near R eyebrow
Mucous membranes moist 
CV: Heart irregular, + systolic ejection murmur
RESP: Lungs clear to auscultation with good air movement
bilaterally
BACK: No spinal tenderness
GI: Abdomen soft, non-distended, non-tender to palpation
MSK: grip strength bilaterally, plantar/dorsiflexion 
bilaterally, initially, very limited motion of R knee, however,
on revaluation, will bend knee against gravity and with some
resistance, will flex hip slightly
SKIN: facial abrasions as described above
EXT: + DP pulses of both foot equal 
NEURO: Alert, oriented, face symmetric, gaze conjugate with 
EOMI,
speech fluent, moves all limbs
PSYCH: pleasant, appropriate affect
 
Pertinent Results:
LABS:
 05:03PM BLOOD WBC-7.1 RBC-3.96 Hgb-9.3* Hct-30.4* 
MCV-77* MCH-23.5* MCHC-30.6* RDW-18.6* RDWSD-50.8* Plt 
 07:06AM BLOOD WBC-7.9 RBC-4.03 Hgb-9.6* Hct-31.2* 
MCV-77* MCH-23.8* MCHC-30.8* RDW-18.7* RDWSD-52.1* Plt 
 05:03PM BLOOD Neuts-76.8* Lymphs-12.6* Monos-6.8 
Eos-2.8 Baso-0.4 Im AbsNeut-5.43 AbsLymp-0.89* 
AbsMono-0.48 AbsEos-0.20 AbsBaso-0.03
 07:00AM BLOOD Glucose-86 UreaN-13 Creat-0.7 Na-144 
K-3.6 Cl-103 HCO3-26 AnGap-15
 05:03PM BLOOD Glucose-102* UreaN-13 Creat-0.7 Na-140 
K-4.2 Cl-102 HCO3-25 AnGap-13
 05:03PM BLOOD ALT-7 AST-13 LD(LDH)-158 CK(CPK)-43 
AlkPhos-88 TotBili-0.3
 05:03PM BLOOD cTropnT-<0.01
 05:03PM BLOOD proBNP-1092*
 07:00AM BLOOD calTIBC-445 Ferritn-12* TRF-342
 07:00AM BLOOD Calcium-8.9 Phos-3.8 Mg-2.0 Iron-58

IMAGING:
Left Femur 4 views 
Although there is no convincing fracture, there is a small to
moderate-sized joint effusion, sometimes may be associated with
occult fracture or quadriceps injury. 

CXR 
Suspected early pulmonary edema. Patchy airspace opacities at
the lung bases, although may represent alveolar edema, are
suspicious for superimposed infection/aspiration, for clinical
correlation. 

CT CSPINE 
1. No acute fracture or traumatic malalignment. 
2. Benign-appearing fatty lucency within the left lateral mass 
of
the C2 vertebra, may represent area of fatty marrow adjacent to
the venous plexus, small hemangioma or less likely intraosseous
lipoma. 
3. Given presence chondrocalcinosis in the cervical spine, lower
extremity involving the pubic symphysis and knee, patient may
have CPPD. 
 
CT Lower Extremity 
1. No acute fracture or dislocation. 
2. There is no joint effusion or lipohemarthrosis. Apparent
density over the suprapatellar recess, likely artifactual and 
due
to confluent shadow on recent radiograph. 
3. No large retracted quadriceps or patellar tendon tear,
although there is likely remote injury to the rectus femoris on
the basis of chronic appearing dystrophic calcifications. 

CT HEAD 
No acute intracranial posttraumatic sequela. 

L ANKLE 
Diffusely osteopenic bones. No acute displaced fractures or
dislocations. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
 year old woman with history of anemia (Hb and hypertension (not 
on medication) admitted after a mechanical fall. Trauma survey 
including CT head/C-spine and L knee x-ray notable only for 
moderate knee effusion. Incidentally found to be in AFib on 
routine EKG. Patient declined anticoagulation or further 
evaluation with TTE. CXR on admission abnormal (edema vs 
infection vs inflammation) however patient asymptomatic so did 
not receive treatment. evaluated and recommended rehab 
however patient declined, and was discharged home (she lives 
with children) with and home . After thorough discussion, 
patient signed MOLST form changing code status to DNR/DNI.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- Newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation, patient declined further 
evaluation with TTE and anticoagulation; daughter/patient 
open/amenable to discussion of aspirin and were made aware of 
its limited efficacy in this scenario
- Abnormal CXR of unclear clinical relevance, can consider 
further evaluation with repeat CXR and possible chest CT if 
patient desires
- Signed MOLST indicating DNR/DNI

ACUTE ISSUES:
#L knee effusion
#Fall: Mechanical, witnessed fall with headstrike onto L knee. 
CT head/neck without bleed or fracture. L knee plain film with 
moderate effusion, no major fracture, unable to rule out smaller 
fracture. AROM and PROM limited by joint effusion. Ligaments 
intact on exam. Evaluated by who recommended rehab however 
patient declined, arranged home and . Pain control with 
tylenol, ice, NSAIDs after ~72 hours due to possible 
hemarthrosis.

# Atrial fibrillation: Newly diagnosed, rates well controlled. 
CHADSVASC at least 3 (age, sex). Patient declined 
anticoagulation and understood risks of stroke. Also declined 
TTE. Prior to discharge, the patient's daughter inquired about 
taking aspirin for stroke prevention. I informed her that 
aspirin is not typically recommended given the efficacy of 
anticoagulants; I suggested that the data for aspirin is weak, 
suggesting a possibly a 20% reduction in stroke risk, however, 
also an increase in bleeding risk, similar to that of the 
anticoagulants. I have encouraged her to continue to discuss 
this with her outpatient providers. 

#Abnormal CXR
#Elevated BNP: Patient declines significant respiratory 
symptoms. Did not want further evaluation with TTE given would 
not take any recommended medications or have any procedures. 
Received one dose of CTX/azithromycin in ED, no further 
antibiotics.

#GOC: After thorough code status discussion, given preferences 
for avoiding medical care, patient signed MOLST changing code 
status to DNR/DNI

CHRONIC ISSUES:
#Hearing loss: Patient has declined hearing aides, however 
concern her deafness will contribute to cognitive decline
 
Medications on Admission:
None
 
Discharge LABTEST Medications: 1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H:as needed Pain - Mild/Fever 
2. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 
3. Ibuprofen 400 mg PO Q8H:as needed Pain - Mild 

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service
 
Facility:
 
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Mechanical fall
Atrial fibrillation
Knee effusion

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. ,

You were admitted to the hospital after you fell on your knee. 
The physical therapists thought you would benefit from rehab, 
but you preferred to return home. We discussed your abnormal 
heart rhythm, atrial fibrillation, which is where the top 
portions of the heart quiver. This heart rhythm increases your 
risk for stroke and the risk of stroke can be decreased by 
taking a blood thinner medication. We spoke about aspirin as a 
possible alternative. The research suggests that there is some, 
but not a strong benefit of taking aspirin for this reason. I 
encourage you to continue to discuss blood thinners with your 
primary care doctor. 

You should take acetaminophen (tylenol) to treat your pain. 
Taking Tylenol three times a day can really improve pain so that 
you can participate in physical therapy. You should avoid NSAIDs 
(i.e. ibuprofen, naproxen) for the next 2 days as they might 
worsen your knee swelling, but then can start ibuprofen to help 
with pain.

During this hospital stay, we also signed a MOLST form which you 
should put on your refrigerator. We've scheduled a follow up 
appointment for you and hope that you keep it! 

It was a pleasure participating in your care,

Your Team

The physical therapists thought you would benefit from rehab, 
but you preferred to return home. We discussed your abnormal 
heart rhythm, atrial fibrillation, which is where the top 
portions of the heart quiver. This heart rhythm increases your 
risk for stroke and the risk of stroke can be decreased by 
taking a blood thinner medication. We spoke about aspirin as a 
possible alternative. The research suggests that there is some, 
but not a strong benefit of taking aspirin for this reason. I 
encourage you to continue to discuss blood thinners with your 
primary care doctor. 

You should take acetaminophen (tylenol) to treat your pain. 
Taking Tylenol three times a day can really improve pain so that 
you can participate in physical therapy. You should avoid NSAIDs 
(i.e. ibuprofen, naproxen) for the next 2 days as they might 
worsen your knee swelling, but then can start ibuprofen to help 
with pain.

During this hospital stay, we also signed a MOLST form which you 
should put on your refrigerator. We've scheduled a follow up 
appointment for you and hope that you keep it! 

It was a pleasure participating in your care,

Your Team

The physical therapists thought you would benefit from rehab, 
but you preferred to return home. We discussed your abnormal 
heart rhythm, atrial fibrillation, which is where the top 
portions of the heart quiver. This heart rhythm increases your 
risk for stroke and the risk of stroke can be decreased by 
taking a blood thinner medication. We spoke about aspirin as a 
possible alternative. The research suggests that there is some, 
but not a strong benefit of taking aspirin for this reason. I 
encourage you to continue to discuss blood thinners with your 
primary care doctor. 

You should take acetaminophen (tylenol) to treat your pain. 
Taking Tylenol three times a day can really improve pain so that 
you can participate in physical therapy. You should avoid NSAIDs 
(i.e. ibuprofen, naproxen) for the next 2 days as they might 
worsen your knee swelling, but then can start ibuprofen to help 
with pain.

During this hospital stay, we also signed a MOLST form which you 
should put on your refrigerator. We've scheduled a follow up 
appointment for you and hope that you keep it! 

It was a pleasure participating in your care,

Your Team

Left Femur 4 views 
Although there is no convincing fracture, there is a small to
moderate-sized joint effusion, sometimes may be associated with
occult fracture or quadriceps injury.

Suspected early pulmonary edema. Patchy airspace opacities at
the lung bases, although may represent alveolar edema, are
suspicious for superimposed infection/aspiration, for clinical
correlation. 

CT CSPINE 
1. No acute fracture or traumatic malalignment. 
2. Benign-appearing fatty lucency within the left lateral mass 
of
the C2 vertebra, may represent area of fatty marrow adjacent to
the venous plexus, small hemangioma or less likely intraosseous
lipoma. 
3. Given presence chondrocalcinosis in the cervical spine, lower
extremity involving the pubic symphysis and knee, patient may
have CPPD. 
 
CT Lower Extremity 
1. No acute fracture or dislocation. 
2. There is no joint effusion or lipohemarthrosis. Apparent
density over the suprapatellar recess, likely artifactual and 
due
to confluent shadow on recent radiograph. 
3. No large retracted quadriceps or patellar tendon tear,
although there is likely remote injury to the rectus femoris on
the basis of chronic appearing dystrophic calcifications. 

CT HEAD 
No acute intracranial posttraumatic sequela. 

L ANKLE 
Diffusely osteopenic bones. No acute displaced fractures or
dislocations. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
 year old woman with history of anemia (Hb and hypertension (not 
on medication) admitted after a mechanical fall. Trauma survey 
including CT head/C-spine and L knee x-ray notable only for 
moderate knee effusion. Incidentally found to be in AFib on 
routine EKG. Patient declined anticoagulation or further 
evaluation with TTE. CXR on admission abnormal (edema vs 
infection vs inflammation) however patient asymptomatic so did 
not receive treatment. evaluated and recommended rehab 
however patient declined, and was discharged home (she lives 
with children) with and home . After thorough discussion, 
patient signed MOLST form changing code status to DNR/DNI.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- Newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation, patient declined further 
evaluation with TTE and anticoagulation; daughter/patient 
open/amenable to discussion of aspirin and were made aware of 
its limited efficacy in this scenario
- Abnormal CXR of unclear clinical relevance, can consider 
further evaluation with repeat CXR and possible chest CT if 
patient desires
- Signed MOLST indicating DNR/DNI

ACUTE ISSUES:
#L knee effusion
#Fall: Mechanical, witnessed fall with headstrike onto L knee. 
CT head/neck without bleed or fracture. L knee plain film with 
moderate effusion, no major fracture, unable to rule out smaller 
fracture. AROM and PROM limited by joint effusion. Ligaments 
intact on exam. Evaluated by who recommended rehab however 
patient declined, arranged home and . Pain control with 
tylenol, ice, NSAIDs after ~72 hours due to possible 
hemarthrosis.

# Atrial fibrillation: Newly diagnosed, rates well controlled. 
CHADSVASC at least 3 (age, sex). Patient declined 
anticoagulation and understood risks of stroke. Also declined 
TTE. Prior to discharge, the patient's daughter inquired about 
taking aspirin for stroke prevention. I informed her that 
aspirin is not typically recommended given the efficacy of 
anticoagulants; I suggested that the data for aspirin is weak, 
suggesting a possibly a 20% reduction in stroke risk, however, 
also an increase in bleeding risk, similar to that of the 
anticoagulants. I have encouraged her to continue to discuss 
this with her outpatient providers. 

#Abnormal CXR
#Elevated BNP: Patient declines significant respiratory 
symptoms. Did not want further evaluation with TTE given would 
not take any recommended medications or have any procedures. 
Received one dose of CTX/azithromycin in ED, no further 
antibiotics.

#GOC: After thorough code status discussion, given preferences 
for avoiding medical care, patient signed MOLST changing code 
status to DNR/DNI

CHRONIC ISSUES:
#Hearing loss: Patient has declined hearing aides, however 
concern her deafness will contribute to cognitive decline

None

HISTORY: HIV. History of distant cholecystectomy presenting with recurrent
colicky right upper quadrant pain. ? stone. 

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar T1 and T2 weighted images were acquired on a 1.5 tesla
magnet including dynamic 3D imaging obtained prior to, during and after the
uneventful intravenous administration of 17 mL of Gadavist. 

FINDINGS:

The patient is status post cholecystectomy. There is mild prominence of the
common bile duct and central intrahepatic ducts, with the common bile duct
measuring 0.8 cm in diameter. However, this is within normal limits in a
patient that is status post cholecystectomy. No filling defects are
identified within the biliary tree. 

The liver has a nodular contour and the caudate lobe is enlarged, consistent
with cirrhosis. There is signal loss in the liver between the in-phase and
out-of-phase sequences consistent with hepatic steatosis. No focal liver
lesions. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. The hepatic artery is patent
with conventional hepatic arterial anatomy. There are two mildly enlarged
portocaval lymph nodes, measuring up to 1 cm in short axis diameter (se 6 im
21 and se 15 im 52).

There is splenomegaly with the spleen measuring 14.7 cm in length. The spleen
is otherwise unremarkable. Pancreas divisum is noted. The pancreas is
otherwise unremarkable. The pancreatic duct is of normal caliber.
Subcentimeter simple cysts are noted within the right kidney. The kidneys are
otherwise unremarkable. There are single renal arteries bilaterally. 

The adrenals are within normal limits. The visualized small and large bowel is
unremarkable. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy. 

There is a compression fracture at T8 with approximately 50% loss of vertebral
body height - this is unchanged since the previous CT in . There is
also collapse of the superior vertebral endplate at L3 which is also unchanged
since . A Schmorl's node is noted within the superior vertebral endplate
of L1. The L4 and L5 vertebral bodies are fused. No destructive osseous
lesions. 

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of biliary obstruction. Mild prominence of the central
intrahepatic and extrahepatic ducts which is within normal limits post
cholecystectomy. 

2. Cirrhotic liver. Mild splenomegaly which is likely secondary to portal
hypertension. Mild-moderate hepatic steatosis. 

3. Pancreas divisum.

4. Chronic compression fracture at T8 and superior vertebral endplate collapse
at L3.

otherwise unremarkable. The pancreatic duct is of normal caliber.
Subcentimeter simple cysts are noted within the right kidney. The kidneys are
otherwise unremarkable. There are single renal arteries bilaterally. 

The adrenals are within normal limits. The visualized small and large bowel is
unremarkable. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric adenopathy. 

There is a compression fracture at T8 with approximately 50% loss of vertebral
body height - this is unchanged since the previous CT in . There is
also collapse of the superior vertebral endplate at L3 which is also unchanged
since . A Schmorl's node is noted within the superior vertebral endplate
of L1. The L4 and L5 vertebral bodies are fused. No destructive osseous
lesions. 

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of biliary obstruction. Mild prominence of the central
intrahepatic and extrahepatic ducts which is within normal limits post
cholecystectomy. 

2. Cirrhotic liver. Mild splenomegaly which is likely secondary to portal
hypertension. Mild-moderate hepatic steatosis. 

3. Pancreas divisum.

4. Chronic compression fracture at T8 and superior vertebral endplate collapse
at L3.

text:
INDICATION: year old man with C5-C6 paraplegia who presents with pneumonia
from MDR bacteria. aware and recommended this type of line.//
Please place non powered single tunneled line

COMPARISON: CHEST RADIOGRAPH ON 

TECHNIQUE: OPERATORS: Dr. (Interventional Radiology Fellow) and
Dr. performed the procedure. 
The attending, Dr. was present and supervising throughout the
procedure. Dr. radiologist, personally supervised the
trainee during the key components of the procedure and reviewed and agreed
with the trainee's findings.
ANESTHESIA: Moderate sedation was provided by administrating divided doses of
50mcg of fentanyl and 0.5 mg of midazolam throughout the total intra-service
time of 17 minutes during which the patient's hemodynamic parameters were
continuously monitored by an independent trained radiology nurse. 1% lidocaine
was injected in the skin and subcutaneous tissues overlying the access site.
MEDICATIONS: Fentanyl, Versed
LABTEST CONTRAST: 0 ml of Optiray contrast.
FLUOROSCOPY TIME AND LABTEST DOSE: 2 min, 2 mGy

LABTEST PROCEDURE: 1. Tunneled non-dialysis line placement.

PROCEDURE DETAILS:

Following the explanation of the risks, benefits and alternatives to the
procedure, written informed consent was obtained from the patient. The patient
was then brought to the angiography suite and placed supine on the exam table.
A pre-procedure time-out was performed per protocol. The access site was
prepped and draped in the usual sterile fashion.

Under continuous ultrasound guidance, the patent right internal jugular vein
was compressible and accessed using a micropuncture needle. Permanent
ultrasound images were obtained before and after intravenous access, which
confirmed vein patency. Subsequently a Nitinol wire was passed into the right
atrium using fluoroscopic guidance. The needle was exchanged for a
micropuncture sheath. The Nitinol wire was removed and a short wire was
advanced to make appropriate measurements for catheter length. The wire
was then passed distally into the IVC.

Next, attention was turned towards creation of a tunnel over the upper
anterior chest wall. After instilling superficial and deeper local anesthesia
using lidocaine mixed with epinephrine, a small skin incision was made at the
tunnel entry site. A single lumen Hi catheter was selected. The catheter
was tunneled from the entry site towards the venotomy site from where it was
brought out using a tunneling device. The venotomy tract was dilated using the
introducer of the peel-away sheath supplied. Following this, the peel-away
sheath was placed over the wire through which the catheter was threaded
into the right side of the heart with the tip in the right atrium. The sheath
was then peeled away. Final spot fluoroscopic image demonstrating good
alignment of the catheter and no kinking. The tip is in the right atrium. The
catheter was flushed and each lumen was capped. The catheter was sutured in
place with 0 silk sutures. Steri-strips were used to close the venotomy
incision site. Steri-Strips were applied. Sterile dressings were applied. The
patient tolerated the procedure well.

FINDINGS: 

Patent right internal jugular vein. Final fluoroscopic image showing RIJ
approach catheter with tip terminating in the right atrium.

IMPRESSION: 

Successful placement of a tunneled line via the right internal jugular
venous approach. The tip of the catheter terminates in the right atrium. The
catheter is ready for use.

EXAMINATION: MRI thoracic spine without contrast.

INDICATION: Assess for spondylopathy.

TECHNIQUE: Coronal T1, sagittal T2, sagittal STIR, and sagittal T1 imaging,
as well as axial T2 imaging was performed.

COMPARISON: MRA neck . MRI cervical spine . MRI
thoracic and lumbar spine .

FINDINGS: 

There is rightward convex curvature of the thoracic at the
T6-T7 level. There is mild anterior compression of the T12 vertebral body with
a degenerative Schmorl's node within the superior endplate. This appearance is
unchanged from .

The thoracic spinal cord is normal in signal and morphology.

There are several bilateral cystic renal lesions which are incompletely imaged
though appear similar to prior exams. The remaining paraspinal and
prevertebral soft tissues appear unremarkable.

There is degenerative spondylosis within the lower cervical spine. There is no
significant spinal canal narrowing or evidence of neural impingement within
the thoracic spine.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Rightward convex curvature of the thoracic spine.
2. Mild anterior compression of the T12 vertebral body, unchanged from .
3. No significant spinal canal narrowing or evidence of neural impingement
within the thoracic spine.

HISTORY: man with subdural hematoma along tent and posterior
falx. Evaluate for interval change.

TECHNIQUE: Contiguous axial images were obtained through the brain. No
intravenous contrast was administered.

COMPARISON: NECT head from , , roughly 7.5
hrs earlier.

FINDINGS: Scan is slightly suboptimal because the superior-most portion of
the brain and calvaria was not imaged. The hyperattenuation along the
tentorium and posterior falx appears less conspicuous compared to prior study,
could be due to redistribution. There is no new hemorrhage. There is no
large acute territorial infarction or large mass. There are bilateral
subcortical periventricular hypodensities, unchanged, suggesting sequelae of
chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Ventricles and sulci are normal in
size and configuration. There is no shift of midline structures. There is no
evidence of fracture. There are small mucus-retention cysts in the maxillary
sinuses.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. No interval hemorrhage.
2. The hyperattenuation seen on outside study along tent and posterior falx
appears less conspicuous, could be due to redistribution.

N.B. Please note that the cranial vertex, including the superiormost portion
of the brain, was not completely imaged on this scan; study slightly
suboptimal.

NOTE ADDED IN ATTENDING REVIEW: There is no convincing evidence of subdural or
other extra-axial hemorrhage on either study.

text:
EXAMINATION: CT HEAD W/O CONTRAST Q111 CT HEAD

INDICATION: year old woman with Right infarct// Right infarct,
worsening somnolence

TECHNIQUE: Contiguous axial images of the brain were obtained without
contrast.

DOSE: Acquisition sequence:
 1) Sequenced Acquisition 4.0 s, 16.0 cm; CTDIvol = 46.7 mGy (Head) DLP =
747.3 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 747 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON: MR dated . CTA dated .

FINDINGS: 

There is redemonstration of a right greater than left cerebellar infarct,
demonstrating evolution with increased edema and foci of hyperdensity
suggesting hemorrhage. There is also increased right tonsillar herniation. 
The lateral and third ventricles are slightly increased in size from prior,
and there is near complete obliteration of the fourth ventricle, suggesting a
degree of obstruction.

There is no evidence of fracture. The visualized portion of the paranasal
sinuses, mastoid air cells, and middle ear cavities are clear. The visualized
portion of the orbits are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

Evolution of right greater than left cerebellar infarct, with increased edema
and hemorrhage. Increased mass-effect results in increased crowding of the
foramen magnum which could be seen in the setting of right tonsillar
herniation and worsening effacement of the fourth ventricle, with dilation of
the lateral and third ventricles suggesting a degree of
obstruction/hydrocephalus.

NOTIFICATION: The findings were discussed with , M.D. by 
 , M.D. on the telephone on at 5:11 am, 2 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

text:
EXAMINATION: CT ABD AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION: NO_PO contrast; History: with HIV, here with abdominal
painNO_PO contrast// Evaluate for evidence of colitis or acute abdominal
process

TECHNIQUE: Single phase contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired through the
abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE: Acquisition sequence:
 1) Stationary Acquisition 5.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 26.5 mGy (Body) DLP =
13.2 mGy-cm.
 2) Spiral Acquisition 6.7 s, 53.1 cm; CTDIvol = 16.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 848.6
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 862 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Visualized lung fields are within normal limits. There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary dilatation. The gallbladder contains gallstones without
wall thickening or surrounding inflammation.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation. There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
4 mm nonobstructing renal calculus in the left lower pole. Left renal
subcentimeter hypodensity too small to characterize is likely a cyst. There
is no evidence of concerning focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis. There is
no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: No bowel obstruction or bowel wall thickening is seen. 
Diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon is noted, without evidence of wall
thickening and fat stranding. The appendix is normal.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable. There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm. Mild atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
Moderate to severe degenerative changes are seen most prominently at T1-T2
through T3-4. Moderate to severe central canal narrowing is seen at L2-3 and
L3-4. Moderate canal narrowing is seen at L1-2 and L4-5.

SOFT TISSUES: The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:


1. 4 mm nonobstructing left lower pole renal calculus.
2. Cholelithiasis without evidence of acute cholecystitis.
3. Moderate to severe degenerative changes of the lumbar spine. Moderate to
severe canal narrowing seen at L1-2-L4-5.
4. Diverticulosis without evidence of acute diverticulitis.

INDICATION: female with distal femur fracture and apparent
intra-articular involvement.

COMPARISON: Radiograph .

TECHNIQUE: MDCT axial images of the right femur were obtained without
intravenous contrast and displayed in multiplanar reformats.

FINDINGS: There is a vertically oriented fracture involving the medial
femoral condyle exiting anteriorly through the anterior medial femoral condyle
and posteriorly through the intercondylar notch. There is approximately 3 mm
superior displacement of the distal fracture fragment. A nondisplaced
component of the fracture extends anteriorly and exits the trochlear groove.

A second nondisplaced fracture is seen through the lateral femoral condyle
(series 2:140).

There are mild subchondral cystic changes within the patellar apex and lateral
patellar facet.

The remainder of the osseous structures are notable for avascular necrosis of
the right femoral head without articular collapse. Serpiginous sclerotic
appearing lesions within the distal femoral metaphysis and proximal tibial
metaphysis are consistent with bone infarcts. There is an additional infarct
abutting the lateral femoral condyle articular surface.

Soft tissues are notable for mild stranding adjacent to the fracture line as
well as marked muscle atrophy with relative sparing of the sartorius, biceps
femoris and gastrocnemius muscles. The posterior cruciate ligament inserts
near the dominant fracture line.

There is a small joint effusion.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Vertically oriented fracture through the medial femoral condyle with 3 mm
of superior displacement of the dominant posterior fracture fragment.
Nondisplaced fracture line extends through the trochlear groove.

2. Nondisplaced lateral femoral condyle fracture.

3. Avascular necrosis of the right femoral head without articular collapse.

4. Bone infarcts in the distal femoral metaphysis and proximal tibial
metaphysis.

5. Marked muscle atrophy.

EXAMINATION: MRI CERVICAL, THORACIC, AND LUMBAR PT22 MR SPINE

INDICATION: male with sudden onset of bilateral lower extremity 
strength, sensory level to the umbilicus, absent reflexes in the lower
extremity. Concern for spinal process.

TECHNIQUE: Sagittal imaging was performed with T2, T1, and IDEAL technique. 
Axial T2 imaging was performed. Axial GRE images of the cervical spine were
performed. After the uneventful administration of 8 mL of Gadavist contrast
agent, additional axial and sagittal T1 images were obtained.

COMPARISON: CT from .

FINDINGS: 

Study is moderately degraded by motion, especially on imaging of the spinal
cord. Within these confines:

CERVICAL:
Alignment is normal. Vertebral body and intervertebral disc signal intensity
appear normal. The spinal cord appears normal in caliber and configuration.
There is no evidence of infection or neoplasm. There is no abnormal
enhancement after contrast administration.

At C2-3 there is a disc protrusion with no significant spinal canal or neural
foraminal stenosis.

At C4-5, there is a small central disc protrusion with no significant spinal
canal or neural foraminal stenosis.

At C5-6, there is a disc protrusion resulting in mild spinal canal stenosis
and no significant neural foraminal stenosis.

THORACIC:
Alignment is normal. Vertebral body and intervertebral disc signal intensity
appear normal.

There is an approximately 0.8 (AP) x 1.1 (TV) x 5.9 (SI) cm T7 through T9
intramedullary multiloculated mixed T2 hyperintense and T1 hyperintense mass
with peripheral T2 hypointensity, which expands the spinal cord, and was
associated with no definite evidence of enhancement, and with associated edema
extending from the T3 through T11 levels (see 3:9, 11:15, 08:10, 14:9 ). No
definite vascular flow voids are noted in or adjacent to the lesion.

At T8-9 there is a central disc protrusion, effacing the ventral thecal sac
and resulting in indentation of the ventral spinal cord resulting in mild
spinal canal stenosis is noted.

At T10-11 there is a disc bulge with ligamentum flavum thickening with no
significant spinal canal or neural foraminal stenosis.

LUMBAR:
Alignment is normal. Vertebral body and intervertebral disc signal intensity
appear normal. The spinal cord appears normal in caliber and configuration. 
There is no abnormal enhancement after contrast administration. There is
layering T2 hypointense signal posteriorly within the deep and dense thecal
sac at the level of L5-S2.

At L3-4 there is a disc bulge with mild facet arthropathy and ligamentum
flavum thickening with no significant spinal canal or neural foraminal
stenosis.

At L4-5 there is a disc bulge, facet arthropathy and ligamentum flavum
thickening with no significant spinal canal or neural foraminal stenosis.

At L5-S1 there is a disc bulge and facet arthropathy with no significant
spinal canal or neural foraminal stenosis.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Study is moderately degraded by motion.
2. T7-T9 5.9 cm centrally multiloculated cystic intramedullary lesion within
with associated blood products, cord edema extending from T3-T11 and cord
expansion, with no internal contrast enhancement, and no definite large flow
voids identified. In addition, layering hemorrhage is noted in the dependent
thecal sac at L5-S2. Differential considerations include vascular lesion,
such as cavernoma or AVM with hemorrhage, with underlying malignant lesion,
such as astrocytoma, ependymoma or hemangioblastoma less likely given lack of
enhancement. Please note that due to motion degradation, small vascular flow
voids cannot be excluded on the basis of this examination. If clinically
indicated, spine MRA may be obtained for further evaluation.
3. C5-6 and T8-9 disc protrusions resulting in mild spinal canal stenosis.

NOTIFICATION: The findings were discussed with , M.D. by
 , M.D. in person on at 6:00 , 0 minutes after
discovery of the findings.

The findings were discussed with , M.D. by , M.D.
on the telephone on at 8:00 , 5 minutes after discovery of the
findings.

text:
HISTORY: Fungating cervical mass. Evaluate for metastatic disease. 

COMPARISON: MR of the pelvis on 

TECHNIQUE: MDCT images were obtained from the lung bases to the pubic
symphysis after administration of 130 cc of Omnipaque. Multiplanar
reformatted images in coronal and sagittal planes were generated. 

LABTEST DLP: 787.8 mGycm

FINDINGS:

Lungs and heart: The lung bases are clear, with no pleural effusions,
nodules, or opacities. The visualized heart and pericardium are normal. 
There is no pericardial effusion. 

Liver: Liver is normal in size and homogeneous in enhancement. There are
multiple hypodensities in the liver, the largest measuring 0.9 x 1.1 cm, which
show enhancement consistent with cysts. The portal and hepatic veins are
patent and there is no intra or extrahepatic biliary duct dilatation. 

Gallbladder: There is a small stone in the gallbladder, but there is no
evidence of cholecystitis. The common bile duct is not dilated. 

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously without duct dilatation or
peripancreatic fat stranding. 

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and attenuation. 

Adrenals: The adrenal glands are normal in size and shape. 

Kidneys: The kidneys are normal in size and display symmetric nephrograms and
contrast excretion. Multiple hypodensities in the kidneys and are too small
to characterize. The ureters are normal in caliber along their course to the
bladder. No mass lesions are seen within the kidneys. There is no
perinephric abnormality seen. 

Bowel: The distal esophagus is normal appearing with no hiatal hernia. The
stomach is grossly normal. The small bowel is opacified with contrast and
does not show abnormal dilatation or focal wall thickening. The large bowel
contains feces and does not show obstructive mass lesions, diverticula, or
wall thickening. There is no intraperitoneal free air or free fluid. 

Lymph nodes: There is a large retroperitoneal lymph node measuring 1.8 cm.
There are also prominent, bilateral iliac nodes which do not meet size
criteria for pathological enlargement. 

Pelvis: The bladder is relatively well distended and does not show focal wall
thickening. There is no pelvic free fluid. There are no pathologically
enlarged pelvic sidewall or inguinal lymph nodes by CT size criteria. The
sigmoid and rectum are unremarkable. The uterine cavity is distended with
fluid and soft tissue density material. A cervical mass is seen extending
superiorly into the uterus. 

Vessels: There is no aneurysmal dilatation of the abdominal aorta. The aorta
and its major branches are patent. 

Osseous structures and soft tissues: There are no hernias is seen. There are
bony luncencies at L3 and T6, which likely represent osteopenia.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Cervical mass seen extending superiorly into the uterus, concerning for
malignancy.

2. Large retroperitoneal lymph node as well prominent bilateral iliac nodes
suggestive of possible metastatic disease. 

3. Multiple liver hypodensities consistent with cysts. 

4. L3 and T6 rounded, bony lucencies probably due to osteopenia. Attention on
follow up exam. 

5. Cholelithiasis with no evidence of cholecystitis.

EXAMINATION: CTA ABD/PEL WANDW/O C W/REONS

INDICATION: year old woman with epigastric pain. Assess for
ischemia/pancreatitis.

TECHNIQUE: MDCT images were obtained from the lung bases to the pubic
symphysis after the administration of 150 cc of intravenous contrast. Axial
images were interpreted in conjunction with coronal and sagittal reformats. 
Oral contrast was not administered.

LABTEST DLP: 1286.57 mGy-cm

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST: Limited view of the lung bases demonstrate bibasilar atelectasis. No
pleural effusion. The visualized heart is normal in size without pericardial
effusion. No large pneumothorax.

ABDOMEN:

The liver is homogeneous in enhancement. No focal lesion identified.No
intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation. The gallbladder is normal
with 2 punctate 1-2 mm calcified gallstones in the dependent portions. No
pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening.

The spleen is normal. Pancreatic atrophy is noted. No peripancreatic
stranding or fluid collection. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No pancreatic
calcifications. The adrenal glands are unremarkable.

The kidneys display symmetric nephrograms. No focal renal lesions. No
hydronephrosis or hydroureter identified. No renal or proximal ureter calculi.

The stomach is grossly unremarkable in appearance. The small bowel is normal
in caliber without wall thickening or loss of mucosal enhancement. The large
bowel is normal in caliber without wall thickening, fat stranding, or focal
mass lesion. Colonic diverticulosis is present without evidence of acute
diverticulitis.Mild fecal loading. The appendix is not visualized however no
evidence of acute appendicitis.

No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph node enlargement by CT size criteria.
No free abdominal fluid, abdominal wall hernia, or pneumoperitoneum.

PELVIS: The bladder is well distended and normal. No pelvic side-wall or
inguinal lymph node enlargement by CT size criteria. No free pelvic fluid
seen. The uterus is unremarkable. The ovaries are not visualized.

OSSEOUS STRUCTURES: Multilevel, multifactorial degenerative changes are seen
within the visualized thoracolumbar spine. No focal lytic or sclerotic lesion
concerning for malignancy.

CTA: The portal vein, SMV, and splenic vein are patent. Mild infrarenal aortic
ectasia is noted with maximal diameter of 2.3 cm. (4a: 83). Moderate amount of
atherosclerotic calcification noted. Within the common hepatic artery just
proximal to the take-off of the (4a: 44) with extension into the proximal
 is a faint linear hypodense intraluminal density, which is equivocal for a
focal dissection. This appears to persist on portal venous phase and is seen
on reformats. ( 601b:53; 602b:73). Of note, there is no differential
perfusion within the lumen at the level of the potential intimal flap. The
celiac axis, SMA, and are otherwise patent. Calcified and noncalcified
atherosclerotic disease is noted at the proximal SMA (602b: 85) with minimal
distal dilatation. The celiac axis demonstrates coarse calcifications at the
ostia with poststenotic dilatation (602b:86) and approximately 50% narrowing. 
The common iliac, external, and internal iliac arteries are normal in course
and caliber. Bilateral single renal arteries are patent. Hepatic vasculature
is conventional.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Subtle linear intraluminal density within a short segment of the common
hepatic artery with ? extension into the represent a focal
dissection. Given the lack of associated perfusion abnormality, this finding
is of unknown age and significance. No evidence of mesenteric ischemia.
2. Patent with approximate 50% narrowing of the celiac axis with mild
poststenotic dilatation.
3. Atherosclerotic disease along the proximal SMA with minimal distal
dilatation, less than 50% narrowing.
4. No acute pancreatitis.
5. Cholelithiasis without evidence of acute cholecystitis.
6. Mild infrarenal aortic ectasia.

NOTIFICATION: The findings were discussed by Dr. with Dr. 
on the telephone on at 4:06 , 5 minutes after discovery of the
findings.

INDICATION: Shortness of breath.

opacities, more confluent at the lung bases with bilateral small- to- moderate
pleural effusions. There is a calcified right lung base plaque. The aortic
contour is unfolded. Heart size is mildly enlarged. Hilar contours are
unremarkable. The patient is status post median sternotomy with mediastinal
clips, likely due to prior CABG. There is a slightly kyphotic appearance of
the thoracic cage.

bases and could reflect underlying edema however superimposed infectious
process can not be excluded.

text:
EXAMINATION: CTA HEAD AND CTA NECK PQ147 CT HEADNECK

INDICATION: female with headaches.

TECHNIQUE: Contiguous MDCT axial images were obtained through the brain
without contrast material. Subsequently, helically acquired rapid axial
imaging was performed from the aortic arch through the brain during the
infusion of 70 mL of Omnipaque intravenous contrast material.
Three-dimensional angiographic volume rendered, curved reformatted and
segmented images were generated on a dedicated workstation. This report is
based on interpretation of all of these images.

DOSE: Acquisition sequence:
 1) Sequenced Acquisition 6.4 s, 16.0 cm; CTDIvol = 56.1 mGy (Head) DLP =
897.1 mGy-cm.
 2) Stationary Acquisition 4.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 43.6 mGy (Head) DLP =
21.8 mGy-cm.
 3) Spiral Acquisition 4.8 s, 37.7 cm; CTDIvol = 32.0 mGy (Head) DLP =
1,208.0 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 2,127 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS: 

CT head: The gray-white matter differentiation is intact without evidence of
infarct, hemorrhage, or mass effect. There is periventricular and subcortical
white matter heterogeneous hypodensity, likely reflecting sequela chronic
microangiopathy. The ventricles and cortical sulci normal caliber and
configuration. The extra-axial spaces are unremarkable.

The orbits, soft tissues, and calvarium are unremarkable. There is a small
mucous retention cyst within the left maxillary sinus. There is soft tissue
debris within the left external auditory canal, likely representing cerumen.
The mastoid air cells and middle ears are clear.

CTA head: There is a 5 mm SI oblique x 3 mm AP x 3 mm TV posterior, medial,
and inferiorly projecting aneurysm from the cavernous ophthalmic junction of
the right internal carotid artery (5:239). The anterior communicating artery
is visualized. The bilateral posterior communicating arteries are not
definitively seen. The vertebral arteries are codominant. The anterior and
posterior circulations are patent without occlusion, dissection, or
significant stenosis. There is normal enhancement of the dural venous
sinuses. There is no evidence of vascular malformation.

CTA neck: There is a 3 vessel aortic arch. The subclavian arteries are
patent. The carotid arteries are patent without significant stenosis by
NASCET criteria. There is a tortuous course of the left cervical segment
internal carotid artery. There is an inferior broad-based shallow ulceration
versus aneurysm at the right V3 vertebral artery measuring 4 mm in with an 1
mm and depth (05:203). The vertebral arteries are otherwise patent and
demonstrate codominant.

The pharynx, larynx, oral cavity, and nasal cavities are unremarkable. The
masticator and parapharyngeal spaces are unremarkable. There is a
heterogeneous and multinodular thyroid gland with dominant left thyroid nodule
measuring 2.0 x 1.9 cm (5:85). There are no suspicious lymph nodes by size or
morphology. The lung apices are clear. There multilevel degenerative changes
of the cervical spine without fracture or osseous lesion. There is streak
artifact secondary to dental mg which obscures adjacent structures. There is
bony sclerosis at the left parasymphyseal mandible.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. No evidence of infarct, hemorrhage, or mass effect.
2. Periventricular and subcortical white matter heterogeneous hypodensity,
likely reflecting sequela chronic microangiopathy.
3. 5 x 3 x 3 mm posterior medial and inferiorly projecting aneurysm from the
right cavernous-ophthalmic junction internal carotid artery. This appears
largely or entirely intradural.
4. Broad-based shallow ulceration versus aneurysm at the inferior aspect of
the right V3 vertebral artery.
5. Otherwise patent intracranial and neck vasculature without occlusion,
dissection, or significant stenosis.
6. Heterogeneous multinodular thyroid gland with a dominant left 2.0 x 1.9 cm
thyroid nodule. The College of Radiology guidelines recommend
follow-up imaging for thyroid nodules measuring greater than 1.5 cm in
patient's greater than years of age. If this has not been previously
characterized, recommend dedicated thyroid ultrasound

RECOMMENDATION(S): Ultrasound to further evaluate the thyroid if this has not
been previously characterized.

text:
CLINICAL HISTORY: woman with head bleed.

COMPARISON: No relevant comparisons available.

TECHNIQUE: Non-contrast head CT. 

FINDINGS: Within the right basal ganglia, there is a 4.2 x 2.1 x 3.9 cm (AP x
TV x CC) hemorrhage with surrounding edema and 6-7 mm leftward shift of
normally midline structures. There is some compression on the right lateral
ventricle which remains patent and slight dilatation of the right temporal
horn. The basal cisterns are patent. No other definite hemorrhage is
identified. Gray-white matter differentiation is well preserved. The
visualized paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear. No osseous
abnormality is identified.

IMPRESSION: Right basal ganglia bleed with surrounding edema with 6-7 mm
leftward shift of normally midline structures. The location is typical for
hemorrhage associated with hypertension although the etiology is not specific.
If hypertension is doubted as the likely etiology on clinical grounds, then
follow-up contrast-enhanced CT or preferably MR imaging may be helpful to
evaluate further if clinically indicated. 

Findings posted to the ED dashboard at the time of study interpretation,
 .

text:
INDICATION: female partial liver donor with cholecystectomy 
years ago. Now has upper abdominal pain radiating to flank x1 week. 
Laboratory values significant for leukocytosis of 11.5, alkaline phosphatase
of 358, and bacteria on urinalysis.

No prior examinations for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Helical MDCT images were acquired from the lung bases through the
greater trochanters without and with intravenous contrast, per the CT urogram
protocol. 5-mm axial, coronal, and sagittal multiplanar reformats were
generated.

FINDINGS: There is mild atelectasis at the lung bases. No pleural effusions
are present. The heart is normal in size, without pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN: The right kidney is edematous and enlarged, measuring 12.8 cm
(compared to 11.5 cm on the left). It demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement
with striated pattern, compatible with widespread acute pyelonephritis. Mild
fat stranding is noted in the perinephric fat, and extending inferiorly along
the right paracolic gutter. Within the renal parenchyma, there are several
subcentimter hypodensities suggesting cystic content. Although too small to
characterize and noting that many or perhaps all of these could be compatible
with small cysts, the presence of hazy borders along a pair of small hypodense
lesions in the interpolar region and the fact that none are present in the
left kidney raise concern that some of these may represent very small
abscesses. 

Mild wall thickening is also noted in the right proximal ureter, again
consistent with inflammation. Prominent retroperitoneal lymph nodes measure
up to 6 mm in the aortocaval and left periaortic regions and are likely
reactive. The left kidney is normal. Adrenals are unremarkable.

Changes of right partial hepatectomy and cholecystectomy are present. There
is mild diffuse intrahepatic and biliary ductal dilation, the latter measuring
up to 12 mm, and tapering smoothly to the level of the ampulla; biliary
prominence does not appear out of proportion to prior cholecystectomy. The
pancreas is normal, without ductal dilation. The spleen is borderline in size
at 13.3 cm in length.

There is wall thickening along the gastric pylorus and first portion of the
duodenum, possibly exaggerated by underdistension, but raising concern for an
inflammatory process. The distal small bowel is within normal limits.

PELVIS: The appendix is normal. Focal calcification is noted in the lateral
right lower quadrant, likely representing a phlebolith. The colon and rectum
are within normal limits. Bladder is distended with urine. Uterus and
ovaries are unremarkable.

BONES: There are no suspicious lytic or blastic lesions. 

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Findings consistent with severe acute pyelonephritis of the right kidney,
with multiple small parenchymal hypodensities, some of which may potentially
represent abscesses, although some may represent cysts. 

2. Pyloric and proximal duodenal thickening, suggestive of an inflammatory
process.

3. Borderline splenomegaly.

text:
EXAMINATION: CT ABDOMEN PELVIS WITH CONTRAST

INDICATION: NO_PO contrast; History: with RLQ abd painNO_PO contrast//
eval for appendicitis

TECHNIQUE: Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were
acquired through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast
administration with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE: Acquisition sequence:
 1) Stationary Acquisition 0.5 s, 1.0 cm; CTDIvol = 1.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.2
mGy-cm.
 2) Stationary Acquisition 2.5 s, 1.0 cm; CTDIvol = 5.8 mGy (Body) DLP = 5.8
mGy-cm.
 3) Spiral Acquisition 15.1 s, 51.9 cm; CTDIvol = 11.5 mGy (Body) DLP =
579.9 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 601 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON: Patient has no prior imaging examinations for comparison at this
institution.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Subsegmental atelectasis is seen in the lingula.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver is unremarkable. There is no evidence of
intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation. The gallbladder is
surgically absent.

PANCREAS: The pancreas is unremarkable. There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN: Spleen is unremarkable

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are unremarkable.

URINARY: The kidneys are unremarkable aside for a few bilateral subcentimeter
hypodensities are too small to characterize. There is no hydronephrosis or
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: No evidence of gastrointestinal obstruction. The appendix
is dilated, measuring up to 1.0 cm (5:63), and there is thickening of the
appendiceal wall. There is adjacent free fluid and fat stranding in the right
lower quadrant. No drainable fluid collection. There is no intra-abdominal
air.

PELVIS: There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

LYMPH NODES: There is no abdominal, pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.

BONES: There is no evidence of aggressive osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
Bilateral pars defects are noted at L5, with minimal anterolisthesis of L5 on
S1.

IMPRESSION: 

Acute appendicitis, associated with trace free fluid and fat stranding noted
in the right lower quadrant. No drainable fluid collection or free air.

text:
EXAMINATION: MRCP

INDICATION: year old man with cholecystitis and possible mass // eval of
gallbladder for malignancy

TECHNIQUE: T1- and T2-weighted multiplanar images of the abdomen were
acquired in a 1.5 T magnet.
Intravenous contrast: Gadavist.
Oral LABTEST contrast: 1 cc of Gadavist mixed with 50 cc of water was administered
for oral contrast.

COMPARISON: Outside hospital CT scan done 

FINDINGS: 

The study is limited by motion artifact.

Lower Thorax: There is no pleural or pericardial effusion.

Liver: Normal hepatic morphology. No hepatic steatosis. There is reactive
hyperemia of the liver adjacent to the gallbladder fossa. The inflammatory
process in the gallbladder fossa does not infiltrate the liver. Subcentimeter
biliary hamartoma noted in the right lobe of liver (series 1203, image 49). 
Trace free fluid in the right upper abdomen.

Biliary: There is no intrahepatic or extrahepatic bile duct dilation. The
gallbladder is packed with gallstones with marked gallbladder wall thickening
and evidence of gallbladder perforation noted at neck of the gallbladder, as
well as at the lateral and inferior aspects of the fundus.
At the neck of the gallbladder there is a large fistulizing gallstone
measuring 30 mm in diameter eroding into the portion of the duodenum.
There is a second fistulous tract from the inferior aspect of the gallbladder
fundus (series 1202, image 93) which appears to be connected to the hepatic
flexure.
Lateral to the fundus of the gallbladder, secondary to rupture, there is
extensive abscess formation/phlegmonous change measuring approximately 31 x
 mm in diameter (series 1202, image 85).

Pancreas: Largely fatty replaced. No focal suspicious lesions.

Spleen: Unremarkable

Adrenal Glands: Unremarkable

Kidneys: Normal renal morphology. No hydronephrosis. A couple of small
bilateral subcentimeter simple cortical cysts. Bladder trabeculation is
noted.

Gastrointestinal Tract: As noted above there is a large 30 mm gallstone which
is fistulizing into the part of the duodenum. There is no evidence of
gastric outlet/duodenal obstruction. Incidental note is made of a duodenal
diverticulum. Fistula tract is seen extending from the inferior aspect of the
fundus of the gallbladder to the hepatic flexure, with marked colonic wall
thickening in this area (series 4, image 32). Note is made of a cecal
bascule.

Lymph Nodes: Multiple periportal lymph nodes are noted measuring up to 12 mm
in diameter (series 1203, image 60).

Vasculature: The major vasculature are patent. Mild ectasia of the abdominal
aorta.

Osseous and Soft Tissue Structures: No suspicious bony lesions.

IMPRESSION: 

The gallbladder is packed with gallstones with marked gallbladder wall
thickening and evidence of gallbladder perforation at the neck of the
gallbladder as well as at the lateral and inferior aspects of the fundus.
At the neck of the gallbladder there is a large fistulising gallstone
measuring 30 mm in diameter eroding into the part of the duodenum.
There is a second fistulous tract from the inferior aspect of the gallbladder
fundus which appears to be connected to the hepatic flexure with marked
colonic wall thickening.
Lateral to the fundus of the gallbladder, secondary to rupture, there is
extensive abscess formation/phlegmonous change measuring approximately 31 x
 mm.
Indeterminate mildly enlarged periportal lymph nodes are noted.

The findings above likely represent sequela of acute on chronic cholecystitis
or xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis.

Although much less likely, malignancy cannot be excluded.

text:
AP PORTABLE CHEST AT 1231 HOURS.

HISTORY: Trauma.

COMPARISON: None.

FINDINGS: There are prominent interstitial markings, presumably mild
interstitial edema from volume overload. No focal consolidation is noted. 
The mediastinum is widened. The descending thoracic aorta is well defined. 
The trachea is slightly deviated to the right presumably due to aortic
tortuosity. The cardiac silhouette size is difficult to assess but is grossly
enlarged. No effusion or pneumothorax is noted on the supine radiograph. 
There are no displaced fractures. The patient has undergone prior median
sternotomy and CABG.

IMPRESSION: Mild interstitial edema presumably due to fluid resuscitation and
possibly cardiogenic factors. There is mediastinal widening likely due to
tortuosity of the aorta. However, in the setting of trauma, mediastinal
vascular injury cannot be excluded. Otherwise, there is no definite evidence
of a chest trauma.

INDICATION: Intracranial hemorrhage status post endotracheal tube placement.

COMPARISON: Outside chest radiograph at 18:39.

SUPINE AP VIEW OF THE CHEST: Endotracheal tube tip is approximately 6 cm from
the carina. An orogastric tube is with side port within the stomach. There
are low lung volumes. The heart size is likely mildly enlarged. The aortic
knob is calcified. There is widening of the superior mediastinum likely due
to supine technique and low lung volumes. Crowding of the bronchovascular
structures is present, but no definite pulmonary edema is noted. Ill-defined
patchy opacities are noted within the left upper lung field as well as both
lower lung fields, which could reflect areas of atelectasis, aspiration, or
infection. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax is identified. There are no
acute osseous abnormalities.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Endotracheal and orogastric tubes in standard positions.
2. Ill-defined opacities in the left upper lung field as well as both lower
lung fields. Findings could reflect atelectasis, infection, or aspiration,
and continued radiographic followup is advised.

at the right perihilar area and left lower lung base, most consistent with
bibasilar compressive atelectasis, right greater than left. No pneumothorax. 
Cardiomediastinal silhouette is enlarged, but unchanged.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Interval worsening of the mild pulmonary edema.
2. Bilateral pleural effusions, right greater than left, with associated
bibasilar atelectasis, right greater than left.

the edge of the film. Cardiomediastinal contours are normal. There are
patchy opacities in the lung bilaterally, particularly in the right and left
upper lobes, which may reflect contusions and/or aspiration. The lungs are
otherwise clear. There is no pleural effusion or pneumothorax. No displaced
rib fractures are identified. Radiopaque density projects over the right
upper abdomen.

dislocation is identified. Symphysis pubis and sacroiliac joints appear
unremarkable. Contrast is noted within the bladder, which contains a Foley
catheter. Radiopaque densities overlie the soft tissues of the left lower
extremity.

LABTEST IMPRESSION: 1. Multifocal lung contusion and/or aspiration.
2. Radiopaque densities overlying the left lower extremity and right upper
abdomen.
